problem: Existing few-shot evaluations for learning word embeddings (DN, Chimera, CRW) do not fairly compare context-only and hybrid (form+context) models: hybrid models can exploit lexical leakage from related word forms in the background embeddings, and the Chimera test set is too small for stable comparisons.
method: Introduce three new evaluation setups: (1) Full Chimera by adding the training set to the test set (330 words) to stabilize results; (2) Filtered DN and (3) Filtered CRW, where background embeddings are trained on a corpus filtered to remove any words sharing a stem with test items, preventing form leakage. An analysis shows many test words have near neighbors with the same stem, and a simple stem-based baseline dominates DN, motivating the filtered tasks and enabling balanced comparisons between context-based and hybrid methods.
paper_title: Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models

problem: Standard few-shot updating with Word2Vec/FastText can distort background embeddings and conflicts with aggressive learning rates; additive/ALC/FCM-style context aggregation treats all tokens uniformly, underusing informative context structure.
method: Propose selective Word2Vec and selective FastText that update only the target (test) word vector and prevent test words from appearing as negative samples, avoiding background drift and enabling higher-risk learning when beneficial. Further, integrate Word2Vec principles into additive and A La Carte (and thus FCM’s context part): apply sampled window-based distance weights, subsampling weights for frequent words, and adjust sums by subtracting the expected negative sample vector before weighting. With tuned hyperparameters, these simple modifications consistently improve baselines and advanced models across tasks, contributing to new state of the art results on multiple evaluations.
paper_title: Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models

problem: Linear transformations in A La Carte and fixed combination schemes limit adaptability across scenarios with varying amounts of form and context information.
method: Replace the A La Carte linear map with a one-hidden-layer neural network (ReLU, ~1000 hidden units) trained with Adam and MSE, and use the same neural transformation within a new Neural Form-Context Model. Using the same window-based weighting, these neural variants provide greater flexibility; Neural FCM achieves top performance on the Filtered CRW task and strong results across trials, contributing to several new state-of-the-art scores.
paper_title: Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models

problem: Indonesian NLP resources are scattered, inconsistently formatted, and in many cases not publicly accessible, which hampers research across Indonesian and its many regional languages.
method: They create NusaCrowd, a two-platform, open initiative that standardizes access to datasets via NusaCatalogue (datasheets/metadata) and NusaCrowd Data Hub (118 standardized data loaders) across 13 unified task schemas spanning text, speech, and image. The pipeline includes submission, expert curation, dataloader implementation, and maintainer review, and it performs both automatic language ID checks (langid.py, FastText, CLD3) and manual quality review. NusaCrowd aggregates 137 datasets and opens access to 14 previously non-public datasets, providing a centralized, schema-consistent entry point for Indonesian NLP research.
paper_title: NusaCrowd: Open Source Initiative for Indonesian NLP Resources

problem: There is no zero-shot benchmark to evaluate natural language understanding and generation in Indonesian and regional languages, and it is unclear how prompt-based large LMs compare to cross-task transfer on these low-resource settings.
method: They build NusaNLU (26 datasets, 12 languages) and NusaNLG (36 datasets; 33 MT + 3 summarization tasks, 12 languages) as the first Indonesian multilingual zero-shot benchmarks. For NLU they compare cross-task transfer with XLM-R (intermediate-task training on XNLI/IndoNLI) against prompt-based decoding with XGLM and instruction-tuned BLOOMZ using English vs Indonesian prompts; for NLG they evaluate XGLM and BLOOMZ under similar prompting. Results show BLOOMZ prompting > XGLM, and notably that XLM-R trained on XNLI can match BLOOMZ and outperform XGLM in zero-shot NLU despite being much smaller; English prompts often work better. Performance on local languages remains poor, e.g., ~2 SacreBLEU for ind↔local MT pairs, highlighting significant gaps.
paper_title: NusaCrowd: Open Source Initiative for Indonesian NLP Resources

problem: Token-erasure-based NLP interpretability methods replace words with predefined values (e.g., [PAD]/zero), pushing inputs out of the training distribution and yielding misleading attributions.
method: Replace zero erasure with input marginalization: compute the contribution of token xi by measuring a weight-of-evidence (log-odds) difference between the original prediction and the prediction marginalized over all candidate replacements x̃i. The marginalization p(yc|x−i) = Σx̃i p(yc|x−i, x̃i) p(x̃i|x−i) uses BERT’s MLM to estimate p(x̃i|x−i) by masking position i, and evaluates the target model on each replacement x̃i. The approach extends to multi-token attribution via conditional MLM masking. This mitigates OOD artifacts and yields clearer, more faithful attributions, quantitatively outperforming zero/[UNK] erasure with lower AUCrep on SST-2 (0.4972 vs. 0.5284/0.5170).
paper_title: Interpretation of NLP models through input marginalization

problem: Exact marginalization over the full vocabulary is computationally prohibitive (|V| > 30k per token).
method: Use adaptively truncated marginalization: only consider candidate tokens whose MLM likelihood exceeds a threshold σ, and renormalize the truncated mass when approximating p(yc|x−i). This reduces computation while preserving accuracy; with σ = 1e−5 the method achieves near-perfect correlation with full marginalization (Pearson ≈ 0.9999) using ~791 candidates on average (~2.5% of 30,522), substantially cutting cost without degrading attribution quality.
paper_title: Interpretation of NLP models through input marginalization

problem: Common evaluation of NLP explanations (e.g., deleting or zeroing tokens) reintroduces OOD perturbations, undermining faithfulness assessments.
method: Introduce AUCrep: evaluate deletion curves by progressively replacing top-attributed tokens with samples drawn from BERT MLM’s conditional distribution (instead of zeros/UNK) and compute area under the curve up to 20% token replacements to respect MLM’s training regime. This provides an in-distribution, faithful metric that shows the proposed marginalization yields the lowest AUCrep among baselines on SST-2.
paper_title: Interpretation of NLP models through input marginalization

problem: Deployed NLP models face boundary-agnostic, non-stationary OOD data streams and must be continually refined from their own prediction errors without catastrophic forgetting, while maintaining upstream/online knowledge and generalizing to unseen data.
method: Introduce the Continual Model Refinement (CMR) formulation that operates on error streams Et extracted from incoming queries Qt (mis-predictions by the current model). Provide a comprehensive evaluation protocol with five metrics—instant Error-Fixing Rate (EFR), Upstream/Online Knowledge Retention (UKR/OKR), Cumulative Success Rate (CSR), and Knowledge Generalization (KG)—and an overall criterion (OEC). Propose a general sampling algorithm to generate dynamic OOD query streams: at each episode, sample from (i) in-distribution V0 with a decaying proportion controlled by α, (ii) a major OOD cluster Vc_t chosen via a Markov chain with transition probability β, and (iii) remaining OOD clusters to ensure diversity controlled by γ; use multiple validation/testing streams for robust benchmarking.
paper_title: On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams

problem: How to practically refine NLP models online on error-centric OOD streams while mitigating forgetting and preserving generalization in the CMR setting.
method: Adapt and extend continual learning methods to CMR. For regularization, use Online L2 parameter penalty and Online EWC with an efficient running sum of Fisher information to constrain parameter drift. For replay, employ Experience Replay and conditional replay with two CMR-specific design choices: (i) Bi-Memory that separates upstream memory Mu=D from a growing online memory Mo built from Et, and (ii) Mixed-Tuning that jointly fine-tunes on Rt and Et. Propose MaxLoss, a variant of MIR that ranks replay candidates by the virtual model’s loss loss(ˆft(x),y) rather than the loss delta. Empirically, replay improves both OKR and UKR over pure regularization, Online EWC attains the strongest KG on held-out OOD data, MaxLoss yields more stable/high OKR(T) than MIR as buffer size grows, and combining MIR with Online L2Reg further boosts overall OEC(T).
paper_title: On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams

problem: In LMaaS, users can only access large language models via black-box inference APIs without gradients, making it difficult to learn task-specific continuous prompts; moreover, prompt spaces are tens of thousands of dimensions, where standard derivative-free optimization struggles.
method: Black-Box Tuning (BBT) performs derivative-free optimization of a continuous prompt in a low-dimensional subspace that is randomly projected into the full prompt space, leveraging the low intrinsic dimensionality of PTMs. It optimizes a vector z in R^d with CMA-ES and maps it via a random linear projection A to an increment that is added to an initial prompt p0 to form the final prompt; entries of A are sampled from a uniform distribution for better convergence. BBT uses informative losses (cross-entropy or multi-class hinge with margin 2) instead of sparse accuracy to guide optimization via API-only forward calls. This enables effective few-shot prompt optimization that, in experiments, surpasses manual prompts, in-context learning, and gradient-based prompt/model tuning on several NLP tasks.
paper_title: Black-Box Tuning for Language-Model-as-a-Service

problem: Derivative-free prompt optimization is sensitive to initialization, particularly for sentence-pair tasks that share structure across datasets.
method: BBT pre-trains an initial prompt embedding p0 on a public NLI dataset (MNLI) and then optimizes only an additive low-dimensional increment Az on top of p0 (for other tasks, p0 is formed from randomly sampled token embeddings). This initialization integrates with the subspace CMA-ES optimization and improves convergence and performance on NLI and paraphrase tasks under the black-box API setting.
paper_title: Black-Box Tuning for Language-Model-as-a-Service

problem: Chemists lack an open, comprehensive resource to flexibly retrieve reaction conditions and operational details (e.g., concentrations, reaction times, order of addition) from the literature; existing databases like Reaxys are proprietary and/or omit such fine-grained procedural information.
method: Build SYNKB by collecting 6 million synthetic procedures from US and EU patents (extending Lowe’s pipeline to EPO ‘C07’ patents) and automatically annotating them with two layers: (a) sentence-level semantic action graphs in the CHEMSYN schema via Transformer models split into Mention Identification and Argument Role Labeling (ProcBERT backbone), and (b) protocol-level CHEMU slot filling. Index the corpus to support structured, slot-based search with Elasticsearch. On six chemist-proposed slot-based questions (Q1–Q6), SYNKB retrieves more relevant procedures and distinct answers than Reaxys/USPTO-Lowe on 5/6 questions while maintaining high precision, demonstrating complementary and higher-recall coverage at scale.
paper_title: SYNKB : Semantic Search for Synthetic Procedures

problem: Existing reaction databases cannot answer operation-specific questions that require reasoning over relations between entities within procedural steps (e.g., which reagent is used to dilute a target, pH after a titration, or concentrations conditioned on a specific solvent).
method: Introduce a semantic graph search over action graphs using Odinson’s labeled, directed pattern language with node constraints, typed edges (e.g., acts-on, measure), and named captures, optionally combined with slot metadata filters. This enables queries like extracting HATU molarity when dissolved in DMF by constraining measure edges from reagent and solvent nodes. A two-step Odinson matching (node constraints via inverted index, then relation verification) enables interactive querying. Evaluated on four operation-specific questions (Q7–Q10), SYNKB achieves high precision (100% for Q7 and Q10; 95% for Q8; 92% for Q9) and answers queries not supported by Reaxys or USPTO-Lowe.
paper_title: SYNKB : Semantic Search for Synthetic Procedures

problem: In truly low-resource vision tasks, training data is extremely scarce, so models overfit and standard augmentations (or label-preserving synthetic data alone) do not provide enough diversity to generalize.
method: Augment the tiny training set with diffusion-generated images from Stable Diffusion using two regimes: label-preserving variants via small-noise reverse diffusion (t = γ·T, γ=0.3) and label-breaking variants via larger-noise sampling (t = τ·T, τ=0.6). Train with the task loss on original + label-preserving images, and add a contrastive objective over label-breaking pairs by generating a lightly denoised sibling for each label-breaking sample, using a memory bank (N=100) and combining with the task loss via λ=0.1. This leverages many more diverse image instances than prior generative augmentations and improves performance over standard transforms and label-preserving-only methods on the LITE benchmark.
paper_title: Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models

problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
method: Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.
paper_title: Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models

problem: Transformer self-attention in vision foundation models fails to highlight domain-specific spatial relations under large distribution shifts to specialized imagery, limiting transfer.
method: Learn a small set of global attention maps shared across images and tokens (e.g., C=10) that encode reusable domain-specific patterns (e.g., horizontal/vertical context for circuits, local neighborhoods for maps). For each token at location (i, j), crop an aligned h×h sub-map from each global map (top-left at (h−i, h−j)), normalize to form token-to-token attention, apply it to values V, and mix the result with the original multi-head attention via learned weights r and balance α, inserting this module in a single mid transformer layer (e.g., 16th). This adds minimal parameters, reduces overfitting, and consistently improves over parameter-efficient transfer baselines like LoRA/AdaptFormer on low-resource tasks.
paper_title: Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models

problem: Pharmacovigilance lacks a large, realistic, and scalable dataset for extracting adverse drug events (ADEs) from full-text biomedical literature, and existing ADE corpora rely on costly span-level annotations that limit scale.
method: The authors introduce BioDEX, a document-level dataset created by linking FDA FAERS Individual Case Safety Reports (2012–2022) to their originating PubMed articles via parsing unstructured literature references and exact title matching. It comprises 65,648 abstracts, 19,433 full-text papers, and 256,240 associated reports, grounded in real PV workflows without requiring new expert span labels. They define a core-report schema (seriousness, patientsex, drugs, reactions) and release code/data for community use.
paper_title: BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance

problem: Automatically extracting structured ADE reports from long biomedical articles remains challenging; few-shot LLMs struggle with context limits and accuracy, impeding practical PV automation.
method: They formulate Report-Extraction as a sequence-to-sequence task, stringifying core report fields and fine-tuning instruction-tuned encoder–decoder LMs (FLAN-T5). The best model (FLAN-T5-Large, 2048-token source, 256-token target, Adafactor, greedy decoding) achieves 62.28% F1 on validation and 59.1% F1 on test, approaching an estimated human lower bound of 72.0% F1 and outperforming few-shot GPT-4 (max 53.11% test F1). Attribute-level analysis shows the fine-tuned model notably improves drug and reaction extraction over in-context learning baselines.
paper_title: BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance

problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
method: The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.
paper_title: BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance

problem: Safety-aligned LLMs remain fragile to jailbreak prompts and can be further compromised by model editing; practitioners need a context-aware, inference-time method to suppress harmful generations without retraining the target model or degrading utility.
method: SAFEINFER couples two inference-time interventions. (1) Safety Amplification (SA): From a small set of unsafe-question/safe-answer demonstrations, it computes a safety amplification vector by identifying influential attention heads via activation patching and averaging safety-conditioned activations; this vector is injected into the target model’s hidden state at an early/mid layer with a scaling coefficient γ to steer representations toward safe behavior. (2) Safety-Guided Decoding (sGDS): It fine-tunes an auxiliary harmful-biased model on harmful Q/A pairs, then at decoding combines the target’s and auxiliary models’ next-token distributions using the Union operator from language model arithmetic and constrains the influence of a selected subset of tokens via a λ-controlled step to obtain a safer distribution. This jointly leverages latent-space steering and distribution-level control at decoding time to reduce harm while preserving capabilities.
paper_title: SAFEINFER: Context Adaptive Decoding Time Safety Alignment for Large Language Models

problem: Existing safety evaluations lack a comprehensive, policy-grounded benchmark spanning diverse misuse categories to stress-test LLM harmfulness across prompting regimes.
method: HARMEVAL: a benchmark of ~550 harmful queries across 11 categories derived from OpenAI and Meta usage policies. Queries are vetted in two steps—GPT-4 for harmful/non-harmful labeling and Perspective API for toxicity (≥0.7)—and used to evaluate safety under simple, instruction-centric, CoT, and jailbreak prompting setups.
paper_title: SAFEINFER: Context Adaptive Decoding Time Safety Alignment for Large Language Models

problem: Open-domain LLM chats are long, multi-intent, and multi-topic, making traditional turn-level DST in narrow domains inadequate; we need a zero-shot way to jointly detect topical segments and track intent/state per segment.
method: S3-DST introduces a structured prompting framework that performs joint segmentation and state tracking with no training. It uses hierarchical XML for both inputs and outputs, encoding each turn inside <T{id}> with nested tags for user/agent and labels. A per-turn preceding_topical_relation (YES/NO) marks segment boundaries and enforces that all turns within a segment share the same intent and domain. Valid categories are provided in-advance in XML, constraining generation and enabling reliable parsing; empirically this yields higher intent/domain JGA and segmentation accuracy than zero-shot baselines.
paper_title: S3-DST: Structured Open-Domain Dialogue Segmentation and State Tracking in the Era of LLMs

problem: LLMs struggle to maintain and ground long conversational context, leading to errors and degradation as dialogues get longer.
method: Pre-Analytical Recollection (PAR) is a grounding strategy that first requires the model to summarize each turn in ≤3 sentences within a <summary> tag before emitting segmentation and state labels. PAR is akin to chain-of-thought but explicitly grounds predictions to the referenced turn content, improving long-context tracking. Ablations show removing PAR notably hurts JGA, especially in longer conversations, confirming its role in mitigating performance drop with increasing dialogue length.
paper_title: S3-DST: Structured Open-Domain Dialogue Segmentation and State Tracking in the Era of LLMs

problem: On standard task-oriented DST benchmarks, zero-shot LLMs inconsistently extract and update slot values over turns without explicit structure or conflict resolution.
method: S3-DST adapts its structured prompting to MWOZ by prompting an XML-structured, per-turn template that echoes the agent/user utterances and outputs an updated list of ['SLOT-value'] tags drawn from a predefined <slots> list (with <valid_values> when applicable). The prompt instructs deduplication and conflict resolution by keeping the most recent user-provided value and to use dontcare only when explicitly stated. This zero-shot, structured extraction achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot JGA on MWOZ 2.1/2.4 compared to strong LLM baselines.
paper_title: S3-DST: Structured Open-Domain Dialogue Segmentation and State Tracking in the Era of LLMs

problem: In RAG, dense retrievers are a bottleneck and cannot reason step-by-step; prompt-based iterative RAG uses fixed, human-designed workflows that fail to fully exploit LLM reasoning. Moreover, outcome-only RL gives sparse signals and does not teach how to retrieve relevant documents at each step.
method: R3-RAG trains the LLM itself to interleave reasoning and retrieval via reinforcement learning with carefully designed rewards. It uses PPO with a composite reward: (a) an outcome reward for answer correctness that combines exact-match and an LLM-based judgment to reduce noise; (b) a process reward via relevance-based document verification, where an LLM scores each retrieved document’s relevance to the original question at every step; and (c) a format-correctness reward. The overall reward includes a trajectory-dependent scaling factor that up-weights process rewards on successful trajectories and down-weights them on failures, and a KL penalty stabilizes updates. This teaches the policy to plan queries, retrieve comprehensive evidence, and produce correct answers through multi-step reasoning-retrieval trajectories.
paper_title: R3-RAG: Learning Step-by-Step Reasoning and Retrieval for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

problem: Base LLMs do not natively know how to interleave reasoning with retrieval, making direct RL exploration in external retrieval environments unstable and inefficient.
method: A cold-start supervised stage synthesizes interleaved reasoning–retrieval trajectories using a strong LLM prompted with structured templates. For each question, the teacher LLM alternates between producing a reasoning step with a retrieval query or a final answer; retrieved documents are fed back iteratively up to a cap, and rejection sampling filters for format-correct, answer-correct trajectories. These trajectories are converted into stepwise SFT pairs by conditioning on prior steps (analyses, queries, retrieved docs) and predicting the current step’s reasoning and action (query or answer). MLE fine-tuning yields R3-RAG-CS, which already performs iterative reasoning and invoking of the retriever, providing stronger positive trajectories and more effective exploration for the subsequent PPO stage.
paper_title: R3-RAG: Learning Step-by-Step Reasoning and Retrieval for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
method: Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.
paper_title: MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation

problem: LLMs exhibit confirmation bias and weak self-correction, so initial ideas are rarely challenged or substantially improved, leaving core flaws unaddressed.
method: Develop the Q-Driven Socratic Ideator: a dual-agent loop where a mentor only probes along three axes—innovation, feasibility, and rationality—without giving solutions, while a researcher defends and revises ideas by querying MotivGraph (fuzzy search, relation retrieval), consulting Semantic Scholar, and injecting cross-domain novelty via a random-nodes tool. The process runs for multiple rounds and ends with an ACCEPT/REJECT gate to enforce quality control. Ablations show the mentor loop is essential—removing it substantially reduces quality (e.g., Novelty −0.86, Experiment −0.88, Motivation −0.62).
paper_title: MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation

problem: Training data attribution (TDA) for autoregressive GPT models has focused mostly on test loss and often ties simulator parameters to seen training indices, limiting generalization to unseen data and overlooking richer training dynamics.
method: GPTfluence introduces a featurized simulator that models training as an n-th order Markov process with multiplicative and additive influence factors. It computes per-batch effects via α and β, which are aggregated from per-example factors A_i,j and B_i derived from pairwise interactions between frozen pre-trained text encodings of training and test examples. Using a pre-trained encoder (e.g., BERT/GPT) for both training and test inputs enables parameter sharing over features rather than indices, allowing autoregressive prediction of full performance trajectories and robust generalization to unseen training/test data.
paper_title: On Training Data Influence of GPT Models

problem: Practitioners need to estimate how individual training examples affect downstream task performance metrics (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE) for GPTs of varying sizes, but prior TDA methods mainly target loss and some cannot predict such metrics.
method: GPTfluence extends trajectory simulation beyond loss to predict BLEU and ROUGE-L, leveraging the same featurized α/β simulator. The approach consistently outperforms Simfluence across instruction-tuning and fine-tuning, including larger GPTs (14M–2.8B). For fine-tuning, it reduces all-steps MSE and MAE by 42% and 28% on average versus Simfluence, and achieves higher final-step Spearman correlations; TracIn and Grad-Dot are not applicable to these metric predictions.
paper_title: On Training Data Influence of GPT Models

problem: There is a shortage of standardized, large-scale training-dynamics data needed to develop and benchmark TDA for GPT models across tasks and model scales.
method: The authors release GPTDynamics, a dataset of 350+ training runs spanning six GPT sizes and five NLP tasks, recording curricula and sequential test metrics (loss, BLEU, ROUGE-L) for both instruction-tuning and fine-tuning scenarios to support training and evaluation of simulators like GPTfluence.
paper_title: On Training Data Influence of GPT Models

problem: LLMs need a way to remove the influence of specific, user-requested training data without retraining the entire model, while preserving task performance and making the forgotten information hard to extract.
method: Introduce lightweight unlearning layers (adapters) inserted after the feed-forward networks in transformer layers, and update only these small modules instead of the full LLM. Train them with a selective teacher–student objective: match the original model on retained data while deliberately deviating on data to forget by minimizing KL on retained examples and maximizing KL on forgotten ones. Add a task loss on retained data to maintain performance and a negated pretraining objective (e.g., masked LM loss for T5 with a 'Predict the masked word' prefix) on forgotten data to erase pretrained knowledge traces. Alternate optimization over retained and forgotten sets for stability, tuning only ~0.5% of parameters.
paper_title: Unlearn What You Want to Forget: Efficient Unlearning for LLMs

problem: In real deployments, models face a sequence of deletion requests and need to combine multiple unlearning operations efficiently without storing sensitive data or retraining.
method: Learn separate unlearning layers for different forget sets, then merge them into a single unified layer via a fusion mechanism. The fusion solves a linear regression objective to make the merged layer’s outputs match those of each individual unlearning layer on its forgotten data, yielding a closed-form solution for the merged weights. It requires only the precomputed inner-product matrices of hidden representations (X_f^T X_f) for forgotten data, enabling efficient, training-free fusion and improved privacy by avoiding storage of the raw data.
paper_title: Unlearn What You Want to Forget: Efficient Unlearning for LLMs

problem: Safety-enhanced LLMs like ChatGPT largely refuse direct prompts for personally identifiable information (PII), making prior extraction attacks ineffective in a black-box API setting.
method: Introduce a Multi-step Jailbreaking Prompt (MJP) that uses a three-utterance context to undermine the model’s moral safeguards: (1) a jailbreak role-play instruction (e.g., Developer Mode), (2) an assistant turn that explicitly acknowledges jailbreak mode is enabled, and (3) a final user query that asks for the PII while encouraging a ‘random guess’ if unsure. To further boost recovery, generate multiple responses and apply response verification via a multiple-choice re-prompt or majority voting. This multi-step setup coaxes the model to improvise based on learned distributions, surfacing memorized PII. Empirically, on frequent Enron emails, MJP raises accuracy from 0% (DP) and 29.55% (JP) to 42.04%, and to 57.95–59.09% with multiple-choice/majority voting; it also yields more parsed PII across datasets (Tables 1–3; Sec. 3.3.3, 4.2.2).
paper_title: Multi-step Jailbreaking Privacy Attacks on ChatGPT

problem: Application-integrated LLMs (e.g., New Bing with web search) may amplify privacy risks by collating and disseminating PII at scale, beyond training-data memorization.
method: Evaluate two simple attacks enabled by search augmentation: (a) partially identified extraction via direct prompts that provide a name and domain (e.g., institution), and (b) free-form extraction that requests (name, PII) lists given only domain knowledge. The integrated search allows the model to locate personal webpages and public sources, then extract and normalize PII. Results show high success with minimal effort: 94% accuracy for institutional emails and 48% for phones via direct prompts; free-form extraction attains up to 100% accuracy on Enron-domain emails (Tables 4–5; Sec. 3.4, 4.3), indicating elevated risks of unintended PII dissemination.
paper_title: Multi-step Jailbreaking Privacy Attacks on ChatGPT

problem: Beyond fields like email/phone, can ChatGPT reproduce full private email contents from its training data without requiring preceding context?
method: Design a zero-shot content recovery prompt that supplies identifiers (sender, receiver, date, subject, Message-ID) and uses the MJP setup to elicit the entire email text. Evaluate with ROUGE/BLEU and NER-based F1 metrics, and report successful verbatim and near-verbatim recoveries on Enron emails despite low average scores (Table 6; Figures 8–9). An ablation on identifiers shows recovery sensitivity to which fields are provided (Table 7), collectively suggesting memorization-driven leakage of longer-form content.
paper_title: Multi-step Jailbreaking Privacy Attacks on ChatGPT

problem: Instance-level detection of LLM-written text is brittle across models, hard to validate, and computationally expensive; stakeholders need a reliable way to quantify how much of a large corpus has been substantially AI-generated or AI-modified without classifying each item.
method: Proposes distributional GPT quantification: estimate human (P) and AI (Q) text distributions from historical human reviews and AI reviews generated by prompting an LLM with the same review instructions and papers. Represent each sentence/document as a set of adjective occurrences (technical keywords removed) and model likelihood with a Bernoulli product over this vocabulary; then perform MLE on a P–Q mixture to infer the corpus-level AI proportion α. Validated on semi-synthetic mixes with low error and robustness to OOD shifts, and shown to reduce in-distribution error 3.4× and OOD error 4.6× versus state-of-the-art detectors while being ~7 orders of magnitude more efficient at inference.
paper_title: Monitoring AI-Modified Content at Scale: A Case Study on the Impact of ChatGPT on AI Conference Peer Reviews

problem: After the release of ChatGPT, the prevalence and effects of LLM use in scientific peer review are unclear at scale, and prior tools cannot robustly measure real-world usage or its corpus-level implications.
method: Applies the estimator to reviews from ICLR 2024, NeurIPS 2023, CoRL 2023, EMNLP 2023, and Nature portfolio journals. Finds a sharp post-ChatGPT rise in α for ML conferences (e.g., 10.6% for ICLR 2024; 9.1% for NeurIPS 2023; 6.5% for CoRL 2023; 16.9% for EMNLP 2023) but no significant change for Nature journals. Uses α to uncover correlates and potential impacts: higher α near deadlines, lower α when scholarly citations (e.g., 'et al.') appear, negative correlation between α and reviewer reply counts, higher α for embedding-convergent (homogenized) reviews, and higher α associated with lower self-reported confidence.
paper_title: Monitoring AI-Modified Content at Scale: A Case Study on the Impact of ChatGPT on AI Conference Peer Reviews

problem: Organizations need methods that distinguish substantial LLM-assisted writing from simple proofreading and that reflect realistic usage such as expanding human outlines.
method: Runs controlled studies showing the estimator is robust to minor grammar-only proofreading (producing only a small, non-significant α increase) but sensitive to substantive LLM involvement: when LLMs expand human-written skeletons into full reviews, the estimated α closely tracks the ground truth mixture. This indicates the approach captures substantial AI modifications rather than mere surface edits.
paper_title: Monitoring AI-Modified Content at Scale: A Case Study on the Impact of ChatGPT on AI Conference Peer Reviews

problem: LLMs exhibit high sensitivity to small changes in prompt wording, but most analyses aggregate at the dataset level and miss instance-level variability, hindering fair assessment and user reliability.
method: Introduce ProSA with a new instance-level metric, PromptSensiScore (PSS), which computes the average absolute performance difference across all pairs of semantically equivalent prompt variants for each instance, then averages over instances. Implement 12 high-quality prompt templates across four aspects and evaluate 8 LLMs on CommonsenseQA, ARC-Challenge, MATH (4-shot), and HumanEval. Use PSS to characterize sensitivity per task/model (e.g., Llama3-70B-Instruct shows strong robustness), and provide practical guidance such as interpreting PSS (<0.1 as high robustness) and observing that few-shot examples reduce sensitivity, especially from 0-shot to 1-shot.
paper_title: ProSA: Assessing and Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity of LLMs

problem: Prompt sensitivity in subjective (open-ended) evaluations is underexplored, despite its relevance to real user experience and LLM alignment.
method: Extend ProSA to subjective evaluation by rewriting prompts for LC AlpacaEval 2.0 and Arena Hard Auto using GPT-4o and GPT-4-0409, verifying semantic equivalence via BERTScore (~0.93–0.95) and human annotation (~0.88–0.91). Compute PSS from GPT-4-1106-preview judge scores across original and rewritten prompts, finding low sensitivity on LC AlpacaEval 2.0 but higher sensitivity on Arena Hard Auto. Further, cluster 500 prompts into 20 categories using sentence embeddings and k-means to reveal category-specific robustness patterns (e.g., IT troubleshooting more robust; coding/creative/data visualization more sensitive).
paper_title: ProSA: Assessing and Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity of LLMs

problem: The root cause of prompt sensitivity is unclear, limiting principled approaches to predict or mitigate it.
method: Use decoding confidence as an explanatory mechanism on CommonsenseQA by computing token-probability-based confidence: for each instance, average the maximum next-token probability over the prompt set. Analyze Mistral-7B-Instruct, InternLM2-7B-Chat, and InternLM2-20B-Chat and show that higher decoding confidence correlates with lower PSS (greater robustness), indicating that prompt sensitivity is an external manifestation of the model’s confidence in its outputs.
paper_title: ProSA: Assessing and Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity of LLMs

problem: LLM-generated text detectors are brittle: simple paraphrasing and adaptive attackers can sharply reduce accuracy, and prior work rarely models an attacker that conditions on a detector’s outputs. Detectors also typically require training/fine-tuning, limiting responsiveness to new attacks.
method: OUTFOX is a training-free, in-context learning framework where a detector and an attacker co-adapt using each other’s outputs. The detector (ChatGPT) retrieves top-k semantically similar problem statements via TF-IDF and constructs in-context examples mixing human-written, non-attacked LLM, and adversarially attacked essays, labeling attacker texts as LM to teach robustness. It predicts labels with a fixed prompt and zero randomness. This design improves robustness to adaptive attacks, yielding up to +41.3 F1 on attacked texts while maintaining strong non-attacked performance (state-of-the-art up to 96.9 F1).
paper_title: OUTFOX: LLM-Generated Essay Detection Through In-Context Learning with Adversarially Generated Examples

problem: Existing attack evaluations mostly rely on paraphrasing, which may not adequately capture adaptive, stronger adversaries that directly target detector weaknesses.
method: OUTFOX introduces an adaptive, generation-based attacker that uses the detector’s predicted labels on retrieved, similar LLM essays as in-context examples. Given a problem statement, it conditions on tuples (problem, detector label, essay) and is instructed to write an essay ‘detected as Human’ with matched word count, using ChatGPT at higher temperature. This produces essays more semantically similar to human writing than non-attacked LLM outputs and degrades detectors far more than paraphrasing (e.g., up to −57.0 F1 on the proposed detector; stronger than DIPPER).
paper_title: OUTFOX: LLM-Generated Essay Detection Through In-Context Learning with Adversarially Generated Examples

problem: There is a lack of large-scale, realistic datasets of native-student essays paired with problem statements and matched LLM outputs to study detection and adaptive attacks.
method: The authors construct a dataset from the Feedback Prize corpus by generating pseudo problem statements and corresponding essays from multiple LMs (ChatGPT, GPT-3.5, FLAN-T5-XXL), yielding 15,400 triplets (problem, human essay, LLM essay) per LM with a 14400/500/500 split. They also provide attacked essays for the test set per attacker and release code/data to support reproducible detection and attack research.
paper_title: OUTFOX: LLM-Generated Essay Detection Through In-Context Learning with Adversarially Generated Examples

problem: Autoregressive pre-training for images uses causal attention, but most downstream ViT usage relies on bidirectional attention; this mismatch degrades transfer if the causal mask is removed or retained.
method: Adopt a PrefixLM-style attention for images: uniformly sample a prefix length S, allow bidirectional self-attention within the first S patches, and compute the autoregressive loss only on subsequent patches. This trains the backbone to operate without a causal mask at inference, enabling bidirectional attention during downstream adaptation. Ablations show prefix attention during pre-training combined with bidirectional attention at transfer yields the best accuracy, whereas purely causal pre-training transfers poorly when unmasking attention.
paper_title: Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models

problem: Next-patch pixel prediction can force the backbone to specialize in low-level details, hurting the quality of transferable features.
method: Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.
paper_title: Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models

problem: It is unclear whether simple autoregressive pre-training on images scales like LLMs and whether its training objective value predicts downstream performance.
method: Train ViT backbones autoregressively over raster-ordered patches using an MSE loss on per-patch normalized pixels, at scales up to 7B parameters and billions of images (DFN-2B+ mixture: 80% DFN-2B, 20% ImageNet-1k), with a straightforward AdamW setup and no stabilization tricks (e.g., LayerScale, QK-Norm). The pre-training loss on a validation set tightly correlates with frozen-trunk accuracy across training and model sizes, improvements persist with more data and longer schedules, and the 7B model reaches 84.0% ImageNet-1k top-1 with a frozen trunk (using attentive pooling), indicating robust, compute/data-driven scaling.
paper_title: Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models

problem: LLMs/MLLMs struggle with zero-shot document understanding because they do not effectively learn or utilize document layout; plain or coordinate-augmented text fed to LLMs loses structure or isn’t well understood, and typical MLLM pre-training/SFT under-emphasizes layout signals.
method: LayoutLLM integrates a document pre-trained model (LayoutLMv3) as the encoder and maps its visual and text+layout features into the LLM via two MLP projectors, then performs layout instruction tuning with multi-level, layout-aware pre-training tasks. At the document level, it uses Document Dense Description (long, detailed captions; ~373.25 words vs 36.27 in LLaVAR) and Text & Layout Reconstruction (outputting '<{box},{text}>' to align text/layout embeddings with the LLM). At the region level, it performs Document Layout Analysis (region localization/type) and Table Understanding (rows/columns counts, logical coordinates, and cell content) to capture 2D structure. At the segment level, it converts classic tasks into instruction format—masked VLM, position masking to recover zeroed coordinates, and geometric relation queries—while freezing the LLM and updating the encoder+projectors during pre-training.
paper_title: LayoutLLM: Layout Instruction Tuning with Large Language Models for Document Understanding

problem: Direct answer-only supervised fine-tuning in document MLLMs lacks explicit layout reasoning and focus, limiting accuracy and interpretability.
method: LayoutLLM introduces LayoutCoT, a layout-aware chain-of-thought with three steps: Question Analysis (identify task type and key layout cues), Relevant Area Concentration (predict bounding boxes of answer-relevant regions to narrow search), and Answer Formation (reason using region-specific layout features, e.g., table rows/columns). A manual-labeling-free pipeline constructs LayoutCoT data by prompting GPT-3.5 on layout-aware representations (HTML or layout text) to produce QA and text CoT, then converting to LayoutCoT by reusing steps 1&3 and taking the union of boxes of sentences referenced in the CoT for step 2. Used in SFT, this yields measurable gains over pre-training alone (+1.96% DocVQA, +5.96% FUNSD) and achieves strong zero-shot results, outperforming trained MLLMs like mPLUG-DocOWL and Qwen-VL by around 10% on DocVQA.
paper_title: LayoutLLM: Layout Instruction Tuning with Large Language Models for Document Understanding

problem: Practitioners lack a principled way to predict when chain-of-thought (CoT) will reduce model performance, despite its widespread default use.
method: Draw a heuristic from cognitive psychology: identify task archetypes where human verbal thinking impairs performance, then test whether CoT similarly harms LLM/LMMs. The authors scale six representative tasks into large evaluations (released as a benchmark) and compare zero-shot vs. CoT across many state-of-the-art models, finding large CoT-induced drops in three archetypes (implicit statistical learning, stimuli ill-suited to language such as faces, and rules-with-exceptions). A bootstrap analysis against 378 prior CoT comparisons shows the heuristic disproportionately surfaces failures (p<1e-4 by magnitude; p<1.1e-4 by frequency), and the benchmark is available at https://github.com/JiayiGeng/CoT_overthinking.
paper_title: Mind Your Step (by Step): Chain-of-Thought can Reduce Performance on Tasks where Thinking Makes Humans Worse

problem: In implicit statistical learning (e.g., artificial grammar learning), patterns are often recognized implicitly and are hard to verbalize; CoT may force suboptimal verbal reasoning and reduce accuracy.
method: Replicate and massively scale artificial grammar learning to 100 finite-state grammars (4400 problems), evaluating nine SOTA models with zero-shot vs. CoT. CoT consistently lowers accuracy (e.g., GPT-4o: −23.1% absolute; Claude 3 Opus: −8.0%; Gemini 1.5 Pro: −6.05%; Llama 3.1 70B: −8.8%), and o1-preview (with built-in reasoning) underperforms GPT-4o zero-shot by 36.3% on a subset. The effect persists across grammar complexities; tree-of-thought yields only small gains (64.55% vs 62.52% CoT) and remains far below zero-shot (94.00%), and results are robust to temperature and in-context steering toward bigram strategies.
paper_title: Mind Your Step (by Step): Chain-of-Thought can Reduce Performance on Tasks where Thinking Makes Humans Worse

problem: When classifying data governed by a simple rule with exceptions, CoT may bias models toward overgeneralizing the rule, slowing learning despite explicit in-context feedback.
method: Adapt a multi-turn vehicle classification task with feedback and long-context memory (240 lists × 10 items) where a highly correlated feature has 20% exceptions and a unique identifier feature allows perfect mapping. CoT greatly increases rounds needed to master labels (GPT-4o: +331% to 12.5 rounds; Claude 3.5 Sonnet: +178%; Claude 3 Opus: +129%), with GPT-4o’s CoT accuracy saturating near the non-exception rate (~80%) after 15 iterations. Steering prompts that explicitly focus on the exception-free identifier (e.g., license plates) can mitigate the deficit, but default CoT remains substantially worse than direct prompting.
paper_title: Mind Your Step (by Step): Chain-of-Thought can Reduce Performance on Tasks where Thinking Makes Humans Worse

problem: Zero-shot visual language navigation (VLN) agents struggle with single-round self-thinking: they must simultaneously parse instructions, perceive scenes, and plan actions, which degrades performance and hinders real-world deployment.
method: Introduce DiscussNav, a zero-shot VLN agent that actively consults multiple prompted 'domain experts' before each move to decompose the task into tractable subtasks. The agent uses a question-template corpus to query experts across four roles: instruction analysis (GPT-4 for action decomposition and landmark extraction), vision perception (InstructBLIP for scene-level queries via targeted prompts; RAM for object tags), completion estimation (ChatGPT for trajectory summarization and GPT-4 for executed/in-progress/pending action estimation with chain-of-thought), and decision testing (ChatGPT for thought fusion and GPT-4 for final selection). This multi-expert discussion reduces the reasoning burden and improves zero-shot VLN performance, outperforming prior zero-shot NavGPT on R2R (e.g., SR 43 vs 34; SPL 40 vs 29) and increasing real-robot success (25% vs 10%).
paper_title: Discuss Before Moving: Visual Language Navigation via Multi-expert Discussions

problem: Natural language instructions often contain complex grammar, causing errors in action order and landmark identification; additionally, actions can span multiple steps, creating misalignment between step-wise moves and task progress.
method: Create specialized instruction analysis and progress-tracking experts with built-in verification. A GPT-4 action decomposition expert detects all actions and an error-correction step explicitly checks action order before landmark extraction; a GPT-4 landmark expert extracts complete landmarks while ensuring integrity. For execution tracking, a ChatGPT trajectory summary expert formats history, and a GPT-4 completion estimation expert performs chain-of-thought reasoning to check passed landmarks and direction changes, then outputs 'Executed Actions', 'In-progress Actions', and 'Actions Waiting to be Executed'. This verification-centric design corrects parsing mistakes and keeps execution aligned with multi-step actions.
paper_title: Discuss Before Moving: Visual Language Navigation via Multi-expert Discussions

problem: LLM-based planners can generate multiple plausible movement decisions per step, leading to inconsistent choices without a principled mechanism to select a reliable final action.
method: Adopt beam search to sample N candidate thought–prediction pairs per step, then introduce decision testing experts to arbitrate. A ChatGPT thought-fusion expert groups identical predictions and fuses their rationales; a GPT-4 decision testing expert evaluates the feasibility of each fused candidate against current observations via chain-of-thought and selects the most reliable final action. Ablations show removing decision testing degrades performance on R2R (e.g., SR and SPL drop versus full DiscussNav), validating the arbitration mechanism.
paper_title: Discuss Before Moving: Visual Language Navigation via Multi-expert Discussions

problem: Automated multi-agent systems for LLMs typically search for a single, static workflow, which fails to adapt inference resources to query difficulty or domain and leads to unnecessary token/tool/LLM-call costs.
method: Introduce an agentic supernet—a probabilistic, continuous distribution over multi-layer agentic operators—optimized to generate query-dependent architectures. A controller Q_ϕ samples architectures conditioned on the query and previously selected operators using an MoE-style gating: it computes activation scores from lightweight embeddings (e.g., MiniLM/SentenceBERT) and sequentially activates operators per layer until a cumulative threshold is met; an explicit early-exit operator allows stopping at variable depths. Training optimizes a cost-constrained objective U(G) − λ·C(G) and updates the supernet distribution via an empirical Bayes Monte Carlo estimator with cost-aware importance weights, biasing toward high-utility, token-efficient systems.
paper_title: Multi-agent Architecture Search via Agentic Supernet

problem: Agentic operators (LLM calls, tool invocations, prompts) are non-differentiable, making it hard to automatically improve prompts, temperatures, and operator graph structure within multi-agent workflows.
method: Use agent-based textual gradients to approximate backpropagation for operator optimization. The system generates targeted textual analyses to propose updates for (i) prompts (T_P), (ii) model temperatures (T_T), and (iii) operator node structure (T_N; e.g., merging/splitting/altering nodes), then applies these to evolve operators jointly with the supernet. This enables end-to-end, self-evolving multi-agent systems; ablations show removing textual gradients yields the largest performance drop.
paper_title: Multi-agent Architecture Search via Agentic Supernet

problem: LLMs trained in a continual learning regime catastrophically forget earlier tasks, and prior-stage real training data may be unavailable (e.g., when starting from a public checkpoint).
method: Self-Synthesized Rehearsal (SSR): at each stage, synthesize rehearsal instances via in-context learning with the base LLM θ(0) using a few demonstrations, then query the latest LLM θ(t−1) to regenerate/refine the outputs for those synthetic inputs so the pairs reflect current knowledge, and finally select diverse, high-quality rehearsal items via K-means clustering around cluster centroids. This avoids needing prior real data, leverages the base model’s stronger ICL ability, and uses only a tiny demo set (e.g., 1%) to guide synthesis while matching or surpassing real-data rehearsal baselines.
paper_title: Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting in Large Language Models with Self-Synthesized Rehearsal

problem: Synthetic instances can contain low-quality or outdated outputs, causing noisy rehearsal and exacerbating forgetting.
method: Synthetic Output Refinement (SOR): for each synthetic input x̂ produced via ICL, replace its output with ȳ generated by the latest LLM θ(t−1), aligning rehearsal pairs with the model’s current predictive distribution. Ablations show removing SOR lowers AR and worsens BWT, while even using synthetic outputs for real inputs improves forgetting over original real outputs, confirming that refined outputs reduce noise and better preserve prior capabilities.
paper_title: Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting in Large Language Models with Self-Synthesized Rehearsal

problem: In practical settings, only limited prior data or even no base checkpoint/demonstration outputs may be available, and general instruction-following and language understanding should be preserved during continual learning.
method: Demonstration-efficient, flexible synthesis: SSR uses only 1% of prior-stage data as few-shot demos (or human-constructed/input-only demos) to drive ICL, then rehearses exclusively on synthetic data. Experiments show SSR achieves superior or comparable AR/BWT to rehearsal with real data, preserves general abilities on AlpacaEval and MMLU without needing Alpaca-52k for rehearsal, and remains robust when the original base LLM is unavailable (using Llama-7b or Alpaca-7b for ICL) or when only input-only demos are provided.
paper_title: Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting in Large Language Models with Self-Synthesized Rehearsal

problem: LLMs struggle to reason over large or noisy tables due to limited context windows and irrelevant content, making it hard to extract only the rows/columns needed for a query.
method: TAP4LLM introduces a Table Sampling module that selects a sub-table by ranking rows/columns using embedding-based semantic similarity to the query, with optional column grounding to consider both axes. It further adds diversity via centroid-based KMeans clustering and a hybrid scorer h(r,c,u) that combines distance to cluster centroids and query similarity (α=0.3, β=0.7). Across multiple datasets, semantic sampling with column grounding and the hybrid method outperform rule-based, LLM-based, and no-sampling baselines (Table 1; §2.1.2, §3.2).
paper_title: TAP4LLM: Table Provider on Sampling, Augmenting, and Packing Semi-structured Data for Large Language Model Reasoning

problem: Raw tables often lack metadata and external context, leading LLMs to misinterpret fields and hallucinate on ambiguous terms.
method: TAP4LLM’s Table Augmentation module enriches inputs with three complementary sources: (i) metadata inferred via LLMs (Dimension/Measure, Semantic Field Type, Table Size, Statistics Features, Header Hierarchy), (ii) retrieval-based context using a vector DB and LangChain to fetch Wikipedia document references and targeted term explanations, with an LLM-based cell-selection strategy outperforming heuristics, and (iii) self-consistency prompting that first extracts critical values/ranges then re-queries. This unified augmentation consistently boosts performance (e.g., D/M+SF up to +2.87% on TabFact; Docs References +5.13% on SQA; LLM-based term explanations up to +4.07% on ToTTo) and reduces factual errors (§2.2; Table 2; Appendix B,C).
paper_title: TAP4LLM: Table Provider on Sampling, Augmenting, and Packing Semi-structured Data for Large Language Model Reasoning

problem: With fixed prompt budgets, practitioners must decide how to serialize tables and allocate tokens between sampled table content and augmentation to maximize LLM reasoning quality.
method: The Table Packing module enforces a token budget between sub-table and augmentation and supports multiple serializations (HTML, XML, JSON, CSV, NL+Sep, Markdown), acknowledging that markup formats can improve model understanding at higher token cost. An empirical study finds a balanced token split (about 5:5 or 4:6 between table and augmentation) generally yields the best accuracy, with diminishing returns when over-allocating to augmentation (e.g., 3:7). Ablations confirm packing’s contribution to overall gains (§2.3; §3.5; Fig. 3; Table 3).
paper_title: TAP4LLM: Table Provider on Sampling, Augmenting, and Packing Semi-structured Data for Large Language Model Reasoning

problem: Existing instruction-tuning data selection for vision-language models either relies on unreliable single metrics or predefined downstream tasks, which is time-consuming and risks overfitting, making it hard to generalize and scale.
method: SELF-FILTER co-trains a lightweight score net with the target VLM to learn per-sample weights that reflect instruction difficulty directly from the VLM’s training dynamics. The score net (implemented as a linear layer) takes frozen multimodal features as input and outputs a weight used to reweight the VLM loss; inter-batch softmax normalizes weights for comparability, and final difficulty is defined as d_i = -w_i. After training, the hardest samples (lowest weights) are selected for instruction tuning, avoiding any external evaluation datasets or surrogate models. The approach outperforms full-data training with ~15% of data on LLaVA and MiniGPT-4.
paper_title: Your Vision-Language Model Itself Is a Strong Filter: Towards High-Quality Instruction Tuning with Data Selection

problem: Filtering purely by difficulty can select redundant, topic-biased samples, reducing generalization across tasks.
method: SELF-FILTER adds a diversity-aware selection stage that penalizes neighbors of selected samples in feature space to reduce redundancy. After choosing a sample, it lowers the difficulty scores of its k-nearest neighbors (k=10) proportionally to cosine similarity of CLIP features with a penalty strength γ=1, encouraging a broader topic spread. Ablations show this diversity module improves performance on benchmarks where redundancy hurts (e.g., MMBench and VisDial).
paper_title: Your Vision-Language Model Itself Is a Strong Filter: Towards High-Quality Instruction Tuning with Data Selection

problem: Assessing multimodal instruction quality with a single indicator is unreliable, and computing robust per-sample metrics can be lossy or expensive.
method: The method constructs ‘score embeddings’ per instruction by fusing outputs from multiple feature extractors (e.g., CLIP Score, ImageReward, ChatGPT/GPT-4V) or directly using CLIP-encoded image/text features. Feeding these coarse yet rich features into the score net allows it to learn the mapping from features to difficulty without task-specific supervision. Empirically, using only CLIP embeddings (a 1536-dim concatenation) yields the strongest results on LLaVA, outperforming precomputed scores that suffer from inaccuracies and information loss.
paper_title: Your Vision-Language Model Itself Is a Strong Filter: Towards High-Quality Instruction Tuning with Data Selection

problem: Video inpainting methods typically rely on manual binary masks, which are tedious and impractical for long videos, and there is no dataset pairing videos with language instructions and inpainted results to study language-driven video inpainting (including interactive, conversational inputs).
method: They introduce the ROVI dataset with triplets of original videos, removal expressions, and inpainted results, plus chat-style interactive dialogues. Videos and masks come from referring video segmentation datasets (Refer-YouTube-VOS and A2D-Sentences), inpainting ground truths are generated with the E2FGVI video inpainting model, and human annotators select the best result across multiple mask dilations to ensure quality. Interactive requests and responses are synthesized via an LLM/MLLM pipeline to simulate realistic conversational guidance.
paper_title: Towards Language-Driven Video Inpainting via Multimodal Large Language Models

problem: There is no end-to-end model that performs language-driven video inpainting without mask inputs while maintaining temporal coherence and accurately localizing text-referred objects.
method: They propose LGVI, a diffusion-based one-stage baseline extending Stable Diffusion to videos via temporal inflation and a parameter-efficient Temporal Attention module inserted between cross-attention and FFN to enforce temporal consistency. The original video is encoded and concatenated with the noised target latent (with a zero-initialized Conv_x for stable conditioning), and a mask decoder supervised by ground-truth masks provides explicit region guidance during training. Joint training on images and videos improves robustness, and LGVI outperforms strong image-editing baselines and is on par with a multi-stage mask-based pipeline on ROVI.
paper_title: Towards Language-Driven Video Inpainting via Multimodal Large Language Models

problem: Handling implicit, conversational user requests for video inpainting requires understanding complex dialogue and translating it into effective generative control.
method: They extend LGVI to LGVI-I by integrating an MLLM (LLaVA-7B) that ingests a video frame and chat-style request, outputs a language response, and brackets prompt tokens with <PROMPT> and </PROMPT>. Hidden states between these markers are extracted and passed through a two-layer MM head to produce vision-aware prompt embeddings that condition the U-Net cross-attention. The model is trained end-to-end with diffusion, mask, and language modeling losses, yielding significant gains over both non-MLLM and two-stage MLLM-enhanced baselines on interactive video inpainting.
paper_title: Towards Language-Driven Video Inpainting via Multimodal Large Language Models

problem: Existing 3D human–object interaction (HOI) datasets provide only coarse, goal-level labels over long sequences and lack state-level, fine-grained descriptions of human parts, objects, and their transitions, hindering semantic alignment and learning of intermediate HOI states.
method: Construct Semantic-HOI by unifying GRAB, CHAIRS, and BEHAVE into 20,441 paired HOI states, each with images, 3D HOI poses and object mesh, plus fine-grained state and transformation annotations. Use GPT-4V with carefully designed prompts to produce part-level human (whole body, head, arms, hands, legs, feet), object, and interaction descriptions for each state and the movement between consecutive states, followed by manual verification. Define three new state-level tasks—Understanding (caption an HOI state), Reasoning (describe the next state given a goal), and Generation (produce the next state from movement text)—to learn and evaluate fine-grained semantic alignment.
paper_title: F-HOI: Toward Fine-grained Semantic-Aligned 3D Human-Object Interactions

problem: Learning fine-grained alignment across text, 2D images, and 3D HOI poses/objects requires a unified model that can handle flexible multimodal inputs/outputs and operate effectively with limited data.
method: Propose F-HOI, a unified MLLM framework that maps multiple modalities into the language space of Vicuna-7B via modality-specific encoders and projection layers: SentencePiece for text, frozen CLIP + projector for images, frozen Uni3D + projector for 3D object meshes, and dedicated projectors for human/object poses. Decode either text or 3D HOI-Pose via special tokens (<Human>, <Object>) with trainable deprojection layers. Train with (i) pretraining for alignments using COCO (image→pose) and PoseScript (text→pose) and (ii) multi-task instruction tuning over Understanding, Reasoning, Generation, and Reconstruction. For Generation/Reconstruction, use an offset regression loss on pose deltas (∆θ, ∆O) to better tie movement text to pose changes, yielding consistent gains over a LLaVA-based baseline on BLEU/ROUGE and Chamfer metrics.
paper_title: F-HOI: Toward Fine-grained Semantic-Aligned 3D Human-Object Interactions

problem: In LoRA-based parameter-efficient finetuning for transformers, it is unclear whether dropout methods designed for full finetuning remain effective and how different transformer-specific dropouts relate; with few trainable parameters, dropout’s role in mitigating overfitting appears contradictory and underexplored.
method: The paper proves that DropKey and DropAttention have equivalent forward computations (when using normalized rescaling), but shows that the NoGrad operator in DropAttention injects gradient noise during backpropagation, harming stability and performance. Building on this, it proposes a unified framework with three axes—dropping position (attention logits/weights/hidden reps), structural pattern (element/column/span), and compensation for the train–inference gap (e.g., bidirectional KL vs. JS)—to systematically instantiate and compare methods under LoRA. Empirically, column-wise DropKey performs best, element-wise HiddenCut surpasses span-wise under LoRA, DropAttention performs worst due to gradient noise, and bidirectional KL consistently outperforms JS for compensation.
paper_title: LoRA Meets Dropout under a Unified Framework

problem: LoRA finetuning remains overfitting-prone and lacks a dropout method tailored to its constraints that is effective across NLU/NLG tasks and LLM scales.
method: The paper introduces HiddenKey, which combines column-wise dropout of attention logits (DropKey-style) in self-attention with element-wise dropout of hidden representations in the feed-forward module, and adds a bidirectional KL divergence between two stochastic forward passes to reduce the train–inference gap. Across RoBERTa-large on GLUE, GPT-2 Medium on E2E/WebNLG, and LLaMA2-7B, HiddenKey consistently outperforms LoRA baselines and prior dropouts; adding input/output dropout yields no consistent additional gains, indicating the method’s sufficiency.
paper_title: LoRA Meets Dropout under a Unified Framework

problem: Knowledge distillation is typically used for model compression; it is unclear whether a student with identical capacity can surpass its teacher and how to best exploit the teacher’s outputs to improve generalization.
method: Train a student with the same architecture as its teacher using cross-entropy to match the teacher’s softmax outputs (no temperature), optionally combined with the label loss, and iterate this self-distillation over multiple generations (BAN). Ensembling consecutive generations (BANE) further boosts accuracy. This simple, temperature-free self-distillation consistently yields students that outperform their teachers on CIFAR-10/100 and also improves LSTM language models, achieving state-of-the-art CIFAR-100 results for a single model (15.5% error) and an ensemble (14.9%).
paper_title: Born-Again Neural Networks

problem: What aspects of the teacher’s output distribution drive the gains in knowledge distillation: non-argmax ‘dark knowledge’ about class similarities, or importance weighting based on teacher confidence?
method: Decompose the KD gradient into a dark-knowledge term (from non-argmax outputs) and a ground-truth term weighted by the teacher’s confidence, then design two interventions: (i) Confidence-Weighted by Teacher Max (CWTM), which trains with standard label loss but weights each sample by the teacher’s max probability; and (ii) Dark Knowledge with Permuted Predictions (DKPP), which matches the teacher distribution after randomly permuting all non-argmax logits. On CIFAR-100, both CWTM and DKPP still improve over baselines, showing that KD benefits are not solely from specific non-argmax class similarities but also from confidence-based reweighting and higher-order moments of the teacher distribution.
paper_title: Born-Again Neural Networks

problem: Can knowledge learned by a DenseNet be effectively transferred to different architectures (e.g., ResNets) of similar capacity to surpass both their baselines and sometimes the teacher?
method: Construct ResNet students that match the DenseNet teacher’s stage-wise output shapes and train them to match the teacher’s softmax outputs, sharing and freezing the teacher’s first (conv1) and last (fc) layers. Replacing dense blocks with residual blocks under KD supervision yields BAN-ResNets that outperform their ResNet baselines and can even beat the DenseNet teacher on CIFAR-100 (e.g., BAN-Wide-ResNet variants reaching ~17.1–17.4% error).
paper_title: Born-Again Neural Networks

problem: Robots need to follow concise natural-language commands to find and precisely identify remote objects in previously unseen real indoor environments, a capability not covered by step-by-step VLN tasks or single-image referring expression benchmarks.
method: Introduce the REVERIE task and dataset on top of the Matterport3D simulator, extending it with object-level annotations and a projection pipeline that renders 2D bounding boxes from 3D object geometry at any viewpoint, handling occlusion via depth ordering and adjusting box size/aspect with camera pose. Curate 21,702 high-level instructions (avg 18 words) over 4,140 target objects across 90 buildings, exposing candidates from panoramas and preserving objects within 3 m of viewpoints. Define evaluation with REVERIE success (IoU ≥ 0.5 within 3 m) plus navigation metrics, and host an evaluation server.
paper_title: REVERIE: Remote Embodied Visual Referring Expression in Real Indoor Environments

problem: Naively chaining a navigator with a referring-expression module yields compounding errors; navigation needs grounding feedback and grounding improves only when navigation reaches good views.
method: Propose an Interactive Navigator-Pointer model: a FAST-short navigator (seq2seq with attention and backtracking) and a MAttNet pointer (subject/location/relationship modules) are coupled by an interaction module that, per candidate view, selects the top-3 matched objects, encodes their category labels with a bi-LSTM and averages their ResNet FC7 region features to augment the view features, acting as hard attention for action selection. Train jointly with a navigation loss (action cross-entropy + progress monitor) and a ranking loss for grounding. This interaction improves unseen test REVERIE success from 7.07% (FAST-Short+MAttNet) to 11.28%.
paper_title: REVERIE: Remote Embodied Visual Referring Expression in Real Indoor Environments

problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
method: Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.
paper_title: VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation

problem: Attention mechanisms in VQA are typically learned as latent variables from indirect signals (text answers), resulting in poor alignment with human attention and suboptimal answer accuracy.
method: Supervise attention directly using VQS masks: downsample the segmentation linked to each question–image pair to region grids (e.g., 14×14), L1-normalize to a probability distribution, and train an attention network f(Q, {r_i}) (architecture from stacked attention networks) with a cross-entropy loss to match these distributions. Concatenate the learned attention features x_att with image (ResNet or attribute) and text features in a simple MLP baseline. This yields +1.5% absolute over MLP+ResNet on VQA Real MC Test-Dev (67.4→68.9), and with attribute features reaches 69.5 (Dev) / 69.8 (Test-Std); VQS-based supervision outperforms HAT maps and tight boxes (All: 68.94 vs 68.42 and 68.65).
paper_title: VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation

problem: There is no established method to produce pixel-wise segmentations that directly answer natural-language questions (question-focused semantic segmentation), bridging the gap between VQA (text outputs) and visual grounding (limited phrase scope).
method: Propose mask aggregation for QFSS: generate N=25 SharpMask proposals and form a convex combination E=∑ s_i e_i, where s_i=softmax(x_q^T A z_i) uses question embeddings and proposal features; threshold E and train with an L2 loss to VQS masks. Compare against a DeconvNet baseline (VGG-16 + deconvolution with question-guided feature gating) and an oracle upper bound that selects among ground-truth COCO instance masks via a binary classifier. Mask aggregation outperforms DeconvNet (IOU 0.3256 vs 0.2687) but remains below the oracle (0.5709), highlighting both effectiveness and remaining headroom.
paper_title: VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation

problem: End-to-end neural VQA systems are opaque and struggle on questions that require extra background knowledge and explicit multi-step reasoning, especially for what/which questions over unconstrained images.
method: Add an explicit probabilistic reasoning layer atop neural VQA by formulating VQA as PSL inference over semantic graphs from both the image and the question. Image semantics are obtained by parsing Dense Captioning outputs into triplets has_img(X, R, Y); question semantics into has_q(X, R, Y). The PSL program is augmented with phrasal similarity (≈p) derived from word2vec and optionally ConceptNet 5.5 to allow fuzzy matching between open-ended phrases, models a potential has_img_ans(Z, X, R, Y) linking answers to image triplets, includes rules that soft-match question and image structures (including missing-node matches), and imposes a summation constraint over ans(Z) to prioritize the most supported answers. Rule weights are learned by maximum likelihood. The system outputs both answers and key evidential predicates, and improves accuracy particularly on specific what/which categories in VQA.
paper_title: Explicit Reasoning over End-to-End Neural Architectures for Visual Question Answering

problem: VQA needs structured semantics from images and questions, but open-ended relations and paraphrases make supervised semantic parsing brittle and data-hungry.
method: Develop a lightweight, heuristic semantic parsing pipeline that extracts relationship triplets from Dense Captioning sentences and questions using Stanford dependency parses, shortest dependency paths between noun/adjective pairs, and phrase similarity via aggregated word embeddings (cosine over word2vec, optionally fused with ConceptNet). The approach is validated by annotating 4,500 Visual Genome region captions with word-pairs and open-ended relations, achieving 64% exact-match accuracy over a large target set of 20,102 relations; this annotated subset serves as seed data for semi-supervised caption parsing.
paper_title: Explicit Reasoning over End-to-End Neural Architectures for Visual Question Answering

problem: Deep networks typically assume fixed-size, ordered outputs, making them ill-suited for predicting variable-size, permutation-invariant sets. Prior deep set methods learn set cardinality and element states with separate networks and sequentially, which can be suboptimal.
method: Formulate set prediction with finite set statistics and an i.i.d.-cluster point process so the set density factors into a cardinality distribution and permutation-invariant element-state terms. Parameterize both the cardinality and state distributions with a single deep network sharing weights w, learning them jointly end-to-end. Model cardinality with a categorical distribution whose probabilities are given a Dirichlet prior, yielding a Dirichlet–Categorical (DC) term in the objective, and model element presence with binary logistic outputs; optimize a joint MAP loss combining the DC cardinality likelihood and binary cross-entropy for labels with weight decay.
