This reduces parameters and improves both cardinality and label prediction compared to training them independently.
paper_title: Joint Learning of Set Cardinality and State Distribution

problem: Generating the most likely output set under a learned model should be joint and optimal; sequential or order-dependent inference (e.g., RNNs) can yield invalid or heuristic solutions.
method: Derive a joint MAP inference over binary label indicators Z∈{0,1}^M that decomposes into a higher-order term from the learned cardinality distribution p(m|x,w*) and a linear term C^T Z from the classifier scores. Show that for any fixed cardinality m the optimal set is obtained by selecting the m largest entries of C, and the globally optimal m* is found efficiently by sorting C and maximizing a scalar objective f(m) over m. The final prediction is the top-m* labels, yielding an efficient, permutation-invariant, and valid set without beam search or post-hoc heuristics.
paper_title: Joint Learning of Set Cardinality and State Distribution

problem: Sampled softmax training for large-class models is biased when using simple, non-adaptive negative sampling (e.g., uniform or popularity), requiring many negatives to approach full-softmax quality. There is a need for an adaptive sampler that closely tracks the model’s output distribution but remains efficient.
method: Introduce kernel-based adaptive sampling, defining q_i ∝ K(h, w_i) where K is a non-negative kernel over the query embedding h and class embedding w_i, with feature map φ such that K(a,b)=⟨φ(a),φ(b)⟩. This isolates the partition function via a precomputable summary z=∑_j φ(w_j), enabling fast probability computation as ⟨φ(h),φ(w_i)⟩ (lines 95–100). A divide-and-conquer sampling algorithm samples subsets C′|C with probabilities based on ⟨φ(h), z(C′)⟩, yielding O(D log n) per sample and O(D log n) updates when embeddings change (lines 101–109, 119). A practical tree variant stops splitting at O(D·d)-sized leaves, reducing storage to O(n·d) while preserving O(D log n)+O(D) sampling (lines 121–128). The sampler depends on the current example, model structure, and parameters, making it adaptive (lines 79–86, 89–99).
paper_title: Adaptive Sampled Softmax with Kernel Based Sampling

problem: Designing a sampling distribution that approximates softmax well yet is cheap to compute is challenging; softmax-proportional sampling is unbiased but as expensive as full softmax, while naive kernels can misalign with negative logits.
method: Propose a quadratic kernel K(h,w_i)=α⟨h,w_i⟩^2+1 as a positive, simple approximation to exp near zero (lines 131–137). To align a symmetric kernel with the prediction distribution, use “absolute softmax” over |o_i|, preserving expressiveness via shift-invariance and matching the unbiasedness condition under the modified output (lines 137–144). Empirically, quadratic-kernel sampling achieves much lower bias than uniform, reaching near full-softmax quality with only tens to hundreds of negatives—one to two orders of magnitude fewer than uniform (lines 171–180)—and once bias is removed, adding more samples has little effect on convergence speed (lines 187–199).
paper_title: Adaptive Sampled Softmax with Kernel Based Sampling

problem: Two-stage open-vocabulary segmentation pipelines rely on CLIP to classify masked region crops, but pre-trained CLIP is trained on natural, unmasked images and performs poorly on masked crops; additionally, finetuning CLIP on closed-set segmentation labels harms generalization to unseen classes.
method: Adapt CLIP by finetuning its image encoder on masked image–text pairs mined from image captions, while freezing the text encoder. For each image–caption pair, generate class-agnostic region proposals with a pre-trained MaskFormer, extract nouns from captions via an NLP parser, and assign each noun to its best-matching proposal using CLIP similarity. This weakly supervised self-labeling yields large, diverse training sets (e.g., 440K pairs with ~12K nouns using 1 caption, or up to 1.3M pairs with ~27K nouns using 5 captions) that better preserve open-vocabulary generalization than using 965K GT masks from COCO-Stuff (171 classes). Ablations show caption-mined pairs substantially improve zero-shot mIoU over GT-mask finetuning, which overfits to the limited vocabulary.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Mask-adapted CLIP

problem: Masked crops fed to CLIP contain large blank regions that tokenize to zero tokens, introducing a distribution shift absent in CLIP pretraining and degrading classification on masked inputs.
method: Introduce Mask Prompt Tuning (MPT), replacing zero tokens from fully masked patches with learnable prompt tokens so the transformer input is T ⊗ M_p + P ⊗ (1 − M_p), optionally inserted as deep prompts across layers. MPT can be trained with CLIP weights frozen, providing a parameter-efficient adaptation specific to masked inputs, or combined with full finetuning. Experiments show MPT alone yields large gains (+4.7 mIoU on ADE-150 and +4.0 on PC-59 over baseline) without changing CLIP’s weights, and further improves over a fully finetuned model by an additional +0.8 mIoU on ADE-150.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Mask-adapted CLIP

problem: It is unclear whether the primary bottleneck in two-stage open-vocabulary segmentation is the quality of region proposals or CLIP’s ability to classify masked regions.
method: Conduct controlled ‘oracle’ analyses: (1) use GT masks (oracle proposals) with a pre-trained CLIP classifier on masked crops, achieving only 20.1% mIoU on ADE20K-150; (2) use MaskFormer proposals with oracle label assignment (matching to GT objects), achieving 66.5% mIoU. These results isolate CLIP-on-masked-images as the key bottleneck, motivating mask-adaptive CLIP training and MPT.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Mask-adapted CLIP

problem: Unified vision-language (encoder-decoder) models incur high inference cost and existing early-exit methods either target encoder-only models or only the decoder, since estimating encoder-side confidence is hard; this leads to suboptimal compute savings.
method: MuE introduces dynamic early exiting in both encoder and decoder by using layer-wise input similarity as the exit signal. It measures cosine similarity of hidden states across layers to detect saturation; when similarity exceeds a threshold, subsequent layers are skipped. This multiple-exit mechanism applies per decoding step (with a decayed threshold for autoregressive generation) and achieves large speedups (e.g., up to ~50% expected time reduction on SNLI-VE and ~40% on COCO) with minimal performance drop.
paper_title: You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model

problem: Early-fusion encoders process image and text jointly, preventing modality-specific compute allocation even though modalities contribute unevenly across tasks (e.g., text is more critical for visual entailment, image for captioning).
method: MuE decomposes the early-fusion encoder into modality-specific image and text encoders with tied weights (no extra parameters), processing modalities independently and allowing separate early exits per modality. The resulting representations are concatenated before decoding, enabling selective skipping of unnecessary modality layers and improving efficiency while preserving accuracy (e.g., fewer image layers suffice for visual entailment, whereas captioning needs more image layers).
paper_title: You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model

problem: Early exiting can degrade performance because intermediate decoder states are not optimized to be predictive, and in generation tasks errors accumulate across timesteps.
method: MuE adds a layer-wise task loss on every decoder layer during fine-tuning so that each layer is directly optimized for the end task, making intermediate representations more predictive and robust to early exit. Combined with a threshold decay schedule at early decoding steps, this mitigates error accumulation and narrows the performance gap versus full-depth inference, as validated by ablations and captioning metrics.
paper_title: You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model

problem: Designing multimodal DNNs typically requires manual selection of which unimodal features to fuse (including intra- and inter-modal pairs) and how to fuse them, which is task-specific, inflexible, and labor-intensive.
method: BM-NAS introduces a bilevel, fully differentiable NAS framework that jointly searches (i) an upper-level DAG of fusion Cells to select intra-/inter-modal feature pairs from pretrained backbones via learnable edge weights α (identity/zero), and (ii) a lower-level Cell structure that learns the fusion operation sequence via learnable β and γ over a pool of two-input primitive ops (Zero, Sum, scaled dot-product Attention with guided queries, LinearGLU, ConcatFC). The primitive pool composes into widely used fusion modules such as multi-head attention and Attention-on-Attention, with repeated inner-step inputs enabling multi-head structures. This end-to-end DARTS-style relaxation yields a unified, task-agnostic search over both feature selection and fusion strategy.
paper_title: BM-NAS: Bilevel Multimodal Neural Architecture Search

problem: Existing generalized multimodal NAS approaches are slow (black-box SMBO or constrained search) and often produce large fusion heads, limiting practicality across diverse modalities and tasks.
method: BM-NAS applies DARTS-style continuous relaxation at both levels and a Cell-by-Cell selection scheme to drastically shrink the combinatorial space (e.g., 2(NA+NB) vs. pairwise C^2 search), while unifying heterogeneous backbone features to a common N×C×L format for cross-modal operations. The hypernet alternates weight and architecture updates and discretizes to the best genotype for evaluation, yielding efficient search and compact fusion networks. Empirically, BM-NAS reaches state-of-the-art or competitive results on MM-IMDB, NTU RGB-D, and EgoGesture with fewer fusion parameters (e.g., 0.98M vs. 2.16M/8.61M) and far lower search cost (e.g., 0.89 vs. 9.24 GPU·h on MM-IMDB; 38.6 vs. 603.64 GPU·h on NTU).
paper_title: BM-NAS: Bilevel Multimodal Neural Architecture Search

problem: Vision-language instruction fine-tuning makes VLLMs vulnerable to harmful outputs and simple jailbreaks, while existing LLM-only safety tuning does not address multimodal risks without hurting helpfulness.
method: Introduce two lightweight safety-alignment strategies using a small vision-language safety set (VLGuard): (1) post-hoc fine-tuning that mixes VLGuard with a small amount of helpfulness data, and (2) mixed fine-tuning that appends VLGuard into standard VLLM training. With ~2,000 images (≈0.3% of LLaVA stage-2 data) plus 5k helpfulness samples, these methods cut attack success rates to near zero on suffix-injection and FigStep while drastically reducing unsafe responses on VLGuard, and maintain or slightly improve helpfulness. The fine-tuning is efficient (<1 hour on 2×A100 for LLaVA-7B full FT) and works with both full FT and LoRA.
paper_title: Safety Fine-Tuning at (Almost) No Cost: A Baseline for Vision Large Language Models

problem: There is no dedicated multimodal safety instruction-following dataset to train and evaluate VLLMs across both language- and vision-originated harms.
method: Build VLGuard, the first public VLLM safety dataset spanning 4 main categories and 9 subcategories, sourced from diverse image datasets and curated via GPT-4V prompts. For safe images, generate paired safe and unsafe instructions with corresponding responses; for unsafe images, generate an instruction and a refusal-style rationale. Provide a train split (~3k instruction–response pairs from 2k images) and a test split partitioned into Safe–Safe (helpfulness), Safe–Unsafe (language-side refusal), and Unsafe (vision-side refusal), enabling comprehensive safety and helpfulness evaluation.
paper_title: Safety Fine-Tuning at (Almost) No Cost: A Baseline for Vision Large Language Models

problem: Why do VLLMs become less safe than their underlying LLMs, and which training choices exacerbate or mitigate this?
method: Diagnose alignment forgetting by probing the VLLM’s LLM component with text-only safety benchmarks (AdvBench, XSTest), revealing higher attack success than base LLMs. Audit popular VLLM instruction data with Llama-Guard to uncover harmful samples, then show LoRA-tuned models are consistently less safe than full FT given the same data. Retraining with identified unsafe samples removed improves but does not fully recover LLM-level safety, motivating explicit multimodal safety fine-tuning beyond data cleaning.
paper_title: Safety Fine-Tuning at (Almost) No Cost: A Baseline for Vision Large Language Models

problem: Zero-shot VLN agents driven by GPT typically make decisions from only local observations and lack a global representation of the environment, causing aimless exploration and poor global planning.
method: Introduce map-guided prompting: the agent builds an online topological map and converts it into linguistic prompts that GPT can understand. The map maintains explored nodes, currently accessible nodes, and unexplored-but-inaccessible nodes, encodes trajectory and connectivity as natural-language statements, and annotates nodes within the action space plus supplementary info for inaccessible nodes. Precise GPS coordinates are discarded in favor of topological relationships, which GPT-4/GPT-4V handles better, enabling global reasoning, systematic exploration, and backtracking.
paper_title: MapGPT: Map-Guided Prompting with Adaptive Path Planning for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Prior GPT-based VLN agents rely on per-step local decisions or verbose chain-of-thought logging, lacking explicit multi-step path planning across multiple candidate sub-goals and limited ability to correct errors via backtracking.
method: Adopt an adaptive path planning mechanism that requires GPT to output and iteratively update a multi-step plan at each step, conditioned on current thought, the map, and the previous plan. This planning explicitly considers multiple potential nodes or sub-goals, supports strategic exploration and targeted backtracking, and is updated as new map information arrives. Experiments show improved upper-bound navigation ability (OSR) and higher rates of successful backtracking corrections.
paper_title: MapGPT: Map-Guided Prompting with Adaptive Path Planning for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Existing GPT-based VLN systems often use complex multi-expert, language-only pipelines that are token-expensive and hard to adapt to multimodal inputs or different instruction styles.
method: Design a single-expert navigation prompt system that flexibly operates either as a one-stage GPT-4V agent (directly consuming images) or as a two-stage GPT-4 pipeline (with vision-to-text via BLIP-2 and Faster R-CNN). The unified prompts integrate instruction, history, observations, action space, and the map, substantially reducing token cost (e.g., 672 input/115 output tokens vs. NavGPT’s 2,465/317 per step) and transferring effortlessly between fine-grained (R2R) and high-level (REVERIE) instructions, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on both benchmarks.
paper_title: MapGPT: Map-Guided Prompting with Adaptive Path Planning for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Estimating 3D human–object contact from a single in-the-wild image is difficult due to occlusions, depth ambiguity, diverse object shapes, and scarcity of 3D contact annotations. Foundation models largely reason in 2D and are not multi-view/3D aware.
method: InteractVLM introduces a Render-Localize-Lift (RLL) pipeline and a Multi-View Localization model (MV-Loc) guided by a fine-tuned VLM. It renders canonical SMPL+H bodies and retrieved object shapes from multiple views, uses a LoRA-tuned LLaVA to produce contact tokens (<HCON>, <OCON>) whose embeddings guide a SAM-based MV-Loc to segment 2D contact masks, lifts VLM features to 3D via a FeatLift network that incorporates camera parameters, enforces multi-view consistency with 3D lifting and losses, and back-projects 2D masks to 3D contact on meshes/point clouds. This reduces reliance on full 3D supervision and outperforms prior work (e.g., F1 75.6% vs 55.0 on DAMON) while remaining data-efficient (e.g., strong performance with 1–5% of data).
paper_title: InteractVLM: 3D Interaction Reasoning from 2D Foundational Models

problem: Existing human-contact predictors are typically binary and do not capture which specific object the body is contacting, limiting modeling of multi-object interactions.
method: The paper introduces the Semantic Human Contact task: predict body contact conditioned on an object’s semantic label. InteractVLM prompts the VLM with the image and object label; the VLM outputs specialized contact tokens whose embeddings condition MV-Loc to localize contact regions on the body specific to the named object. This yields large gains over a Semantic-DECO baseline across categories on DAMON (e.g., higher F1 and lower geodesic error).
paper_title: InteractVLM: 3D Interaction Reasoning from 2D Foundational Models

problem: Recovering joint 3D human–object arrangements from a single image is ill-posed; prior methods often assume known or handcrafted contacts, struggling under occlusions and depth ambiguity.
method: Using InteractVLM’s inferred 3D contacts on both body and object, the method retrieves an object mesh via OpenShape, initializes object pose by ICP aligning object and body contact points with normal compatibility, and then optimizes object rotation/translation/scale with differentiable rendering, combining mask and contact anchoring losses. This produces more realistic 3D HOI reconstructions and is preferred over PHOSA in a user study (62% preference).
paper_title: InteractVLM: 3D Interaction Reasoning from 2D Foundational Models

problem: Open-vocabulary dense prediction benchmarks are not tailored to imperceptible, camouflaged objects, making it hard to evaluate models’ ability to generalize to unseen classes in complex scenes.
method: They define a new task, Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation (OVCOS), and release OVCamo: 11,483 hand-selected images with fine masks over 75 relabeled, semantically consistent classes. The split uses 14 seen classes for training and 61 unseen for testing (7713/3770 images), with dataset attributes (e.g., object concentration, color ratio, size) analyzed to capture camouflage difficulty. They adapt evaluation to jointly assess classification and segmentation (e.g., cSm, cFωβ, cMAE, cIoU).
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation

problem: CLIP-based open-vocabulary segmentation struggles on camouflaged objects because appearance cues alone are weak; models need stronger class-semantic guidance and structural cues within an efficient, single-stage pipeline.
method: They propose OVCoser, a single-stage, CLIP-driven encoder–decoder baseline with frozen CLIP text and visual encoders. A Semantic Guidance (SG) module injects normalized text embeddings into attention (SGA) to spatially emphasize class-relevant features, while a Structure Enhancement (SE) module adds auxiliary edge and depth branches whose stem features modulate visual features via attention (SEA). An iterative refinement loop feeds back a correlation matrix between visual/text embeddings (M_cor) and an object-aware representation from the previous mask (f_obj) to reweight attention (top-down). Edge/depth are used only as training-time supervision; inference needs only RGB. Final class assignment uses masked average pooling over high-level features and similarity to text embeddings, yielding strong gains on OVCamo with low overhead (<2% trainable params, ~0.2T FLOPs).
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation

problem: Generic CLIP prompts provide suboptimal semantic context for camouflaged objects, limiting the effectiveness of text-guided segmentation.
method: They design CamoPrompts, a set of camouflage-aware textual templates (e.g., “A photo of the <class> camouflaged to blend in with its surroundings.”) and average their embeddings per class. On OVCamo, masked-CLIP classification accuracy improves to 0.704 versus typical generic templates (~0.686–0.700), and analysis shows these templates reduce the Hausdorff distance between seen and unseen class embeddings, indicating better transfer of semantic priors.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation

problem: Pre-trained vision, language, and vision-language models can fail under complex, stochastic combinations of factors, but exhaustively testing all conditions is infeasible. There is a need for a scalable, post-hoc method to discover where and how these failures occur across continuous and discrete spaces.
method: Frame failure discovery as a deep RL problem over an interpretable concept space C (e.g., image transforms, text edits, prompt attributes) and learn a DQN policy to select actions that increase a discrepancy-based reward (accuracy drop in classifiers, BLEU/length penalties in summarization, human or CLIP-based bias/quality in generation). Two exploration regimes are introduced: macroscopic (sporadic actions over all combinations) to quickly locate disparate failure regions, and microscopic (small, incremental actions) to zoom into neighborhoods and reach failures in fewer steps. The approach is instantiated in Gym environments for image classification (ImageNet models), text summarization (BART/T5), and Stable Diffusion v1-4. Empirically, the DQN finds substantially more failures with lower entropy than baselines like random/greedy/threshold searches (Table 1), and CLIP-based rewards approximate human feedback, yielding similar learned policies.
paper_title: Failures Are Fated, But Can Be Faded: Characterizing and Mitigating Unwanted Behaviors in Large-Scale Vision and Language Models

problem: Even after discovering many potential failures, practitioners need ways to interpret, prioritize, and communicate these regions—especially in high-dimensional action spaces—so they can decide which failure modes to address.
method: Summarize the discovered failure landscape qualitatively by converting Q-values into action-selection probabilities via a softmax over Q(s,a) and visualizing the top actions in 3D; color encodes mean probability of failure-inducing actions and point size reflects confidence (inverse standard deviation), enabling interactive inspection of sample failures. Quantitatively, compute Wasserstein barycenters over regions of interest to obtain sliced, aggregated views that help compare and prioritize failure pockets. These summaries guide users to select specific failure modes for mitigation.
paper_title: Failures Are Fated, But Can Be Faded: Characterizing and Mitigating Unwanted Behaviors in Large-Scale Vision and Language Models

problem: Once high-impact failure modes are identified, reducing them without retraining large models from scratch and without degrading overall performance remains challenging.
method: Restructure the failure landscape via targeted fine-tuning driven by user-selected failure modes. For classifiers, fine-tune only the final layer with a small learning rate to preserve overall accuracy while reducing failures at selected actions; for summarization models, fine-tune BART/T5 on paired data; for Stable Diffusion v1-4, apply LoRA with a small, balanced dataset (e.g., DALL·E3-generated images) to correct gender bias. Documented shifts include reduced failure peaks and Wasserstein distances in classifiers, BLEU-based reward drops indicating improved summaries, and a male-to-female bias ratio drop from 1.65 to 1.16 with a 43% reduction in ambiguous generations for SD v1-4, demonstrating effective, localized mitigation.
paper_title: Failures Are Fated, But Can Be Faded: Characterizing and Mitigating Unwanted Behaviors in Large-Scale Vision and Language Models

problem: Existing vision-language models struggle to generate frame-specific, temporally fine-grained captions and often hallucinate action progression across adjacent frames.
method: Introduce ProgressCaptioner, a two-stage VLM fine-tuning pipeline that moves from frame-pair captioning to full sequence captioning. Stage I pseudo-labels frame pairs with an ensemble of captioners, then automatically filters them using two critics: (1) progression detection via LLM majority vote to check if captions reflect visual change, and (2) caption matching via a VLM multi-choice task with an 'unsure' option to verify frame–caption alignment. Accepted pairs supervise SFT while accepted/rejected pairs create preference tuples for DPO to curb hallucination. Stage II extends to T-frame sequences using a two-frame sliding window (leveraging the Stage-I model plus other VLMs) and repeats the same critics to curate sequence-level SFT and DPO data. The final model (SigLIP vision encoder + Qwen2 LLM, initialized from LLaVA-OV-7B) accepts 2–T frames and balances local detail with broader temporal context.
paper_title: Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning

problem: There is no scalable dataset or robust metrics to train and evaluate frame-level, progress-aware video captions that capture subtle temporal changes without hallucination.
method: Build FrameCap, a large-scale training set from HowToChange and COIN by pseudo-labeling frame pairs and short sequences with multiple VLMs, then filtering using progression detection (LLM judge) and caption matching (VLM judge), yielding extensive SFT data and preference tuples for DPO. Establish FrameCapEval from four public action datasets (HowToChange, COIN, Penn Action, Kinetics) with clustered, manually verified sequences to ensure fine-grained progression. Propose two automatic, reference-free evaluation metrics: progression detection accuracy (LLama-3.1-70B-Instruct) and sequence-level caption matching accuracy (Gemini-1.5-Pro), jointly assessing temporal granularity and hallucination.
paper_title: Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning

problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
method: Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.
paper_title: Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning

problem: Existing LVLM benchmarks largely focus on object hallucination and lack control-group designs that disentangle errors from language priors versus failures in visual perception. This makes it hard to diagnose when models hallucinate answers from parametric memory versus misinterpret actual visual inputs.
method: The paper introduces HALLUSIONBENCH, a human-crafted benchmark of 1,129 yes/no visual question pairs over 346 figures (165 original and 181 human-edited) spanning diverse formats (images, videos, charts, tables, maps, OCR, illusions, math). It defines two question categories—Visual Dependent (cannot be answered without the image) and Visual Supplement (answerable from knowledge but image may correct or override)—and structures each sample as a control pair (original/no-visual vs edited/original) to expose the impact of parametric memory and visual reasoning. Human edits (e.g., flipping, order reversing, masking, OCR/object/color edits) are used to systematically perturb context and measure robustness. This design explicitly targets and quantifies both language hallucination and visual illusion, a gap not addressed by prior benchmarks.
paper_title: HALLUSIONBENCH: An Advanced Diagnostic Suite for Entangled Language Hallucination and Visual Illusion in Large Vision-Language Models

problem: There is no standardized, quantitative methodology to diagnose LVLM failure modes beyond raw accuracy, including yes/no bias, logical consistency across related questions, and the root cause of errors (language hallucination vs visual illusion). Human grading is costly and inconsistent for free-form outputs.
method: The authors propose a GPT-4-assisted evaluation pipeline that maps LVLM outputs to Correct/Incorrect/Uncertain using a fixed prompt, runs the judge three times to reduce variance, and shows close alignment with human evaluation. They define correctness metrics (All Accuracy, Figure Accuracy, Question Pair Accuracy) with an acceptance rule for 'uncertain' on Visual Supplement questions without visual input. They further introduce analytical tests: a Yes/No Bias Test (Yes Percentage Difference and False Positive Ratio), a Consistency Test over logically correlated question sets per figure, and a decision-tree-based Diagnostic Test on control pairs that categorizes each failure as Language Hallucination, Visual Illusion, or Mixed/Uncertain. This suite provides fine-grained, quantitative diagnosis of LVLM weaknesses.
paper_title: HALLUSIONBENCH: An Advanced Diagnostic Suite for Entangled Language Hallucination and Visual Illusion in Large Vision-Language Models

problem: Modern Transformers rely on normalization layers (LN/RMSNorm) for stability and performance, making them seemingly indispensable. Can we remove them without degrading training stability or accuracy and without extensive recipe changes?
method: Introduce Dynamic Tanh (DyT), a drop-in, element-wise replacement for normalization: y = γ · tanh(α · x) + β, where α is a learnable scalar and γ, β are per-channel affine parameters. DyT squashes extreme activations via tanh while α learns an appropriate global scale—achieving the core effects of LN/RMSNorm without computing statistics. Simply replace each normalization layer in attention/FFN/final norm with DyT (Algorithm 1), keep most original hyperparameters, and train as usual. Across diverse settings—supervised and self-supervised ViT/ConvNeXt, Diffusion Transformers, speech (wav2vec 2.0), DNA sequence models, and LLaMA 7B–70B—DyT matches or improves performance and trains stably (Tables 1–6, Figure 6; Sections 5, A).
paper_title: Transformers without Normalization

problem: The mechanism by which LN improves training is not fully understood beyond linear re-centering/rescaling. What nonlinearity does LN effectively impose, and can we emulate it directly to avoid statistic-based normalization?
method: Empirically analyze LN input–output mappings in trained Transformers and observe tanh-like S-shaped curves that linearly pass central activations while squashing outliers (Figures 2–4). Per-token mappings are linear, but token-wise variances produce a collective nonlinearity; a few channels exhibit extreme values that are disproportionately squashed. This motivates DyT’s tanh(αx) form. Further, α learns values that track ≈1/std of activations during and after training (Figure 8; Section 6.2), providing global scaling akin to normalization. Ablations show squashing is essential—replacing tanh with identity leads to divergence; tanh outperforms hardtanh/sigmoid; and a learnable α significantly boosts accuracy (Tables 7–8; Section 6.1).
paper_title: Transformers without Normalization

problem: Applying a normalization-free Transformer at LLM scale is challenging due to extreme width and sensitivity to scaling, risking instability or performance loss.
method: For LLaMA pretraining with DyT, add a learnable scalar immediately after embeddings (initialized to √d) to correct initial activation magnitudes and tune α initialization by block type and width: use higher α0 in attention blocks and lower α0 in FFN/final layers, with larger widths requiring smaller α0 (Table 10, Table 11; Appendix A). With these choices, DyT achieves training-loss and zero-shot parity with RMSNorm from 7B to 70B (Table 4; Figure 6), and heatmaps show improved losses with higher attention α0 (Figure 11).
paper_title: Transformers without Normalization

problem: Document parsing methods lack a fair, comprehensive benchmark: existing datasets cover few real-world page types, rely on mismatched metrics, and provide little fine-grained analysis.
method: Introduce OmniDocBench, a high-quality evaluation set spanning 9 PDF page types (e.g., books, slides, reports, textbooks, exams, magazines, academic papers, notes, newspapers) with 981 pages. It provides 19 region/layout categories, content recognition for text, formulas (LaTeX), and tables (HTML/LaTeX), plus reading-order and affiliation annotations. Pages and boxes are richly labeled with attributes (e.g., 6 page-level and 9 bbox-level attributes including language, text background, rotation; table frame types, merged cells, formulas, rotation). Annotations are produced via semi-automatic pre-annotation (LayoutLMv3, PaddleOCR, UniMERNet, GPT-4o), manual correction, and expert QC (including CDM-based rendering checks) to ensure accuracy.
paper_title: OmniDocBench: Benchmarking Diverse PDF Document Parsing with Comprehensive Annotations

problem: End-to-end parsers and VLMs output heterogeneous Markdown and formula/table formats, making fair, structure-aware evaluation and reading-order assessment challenging.
method: Provide a unified end-to-end evaluation pipeline with preprocessing and ordered element extraction (LaTeX/HTML tables, display formulas, markdown tables, code blocks), pure-text paragraphing, inline formula canonicalization to Unicode, and reading-order tagging. Propose Adjacency Search Match to merge/split paragraphs using normalized edit distance and fuzzy matching, mitigating segmentation differences. Apply ignore handling (headers/footers/page numbers, ambiguous captions/footnotes) to avoid penalizing format conventions. Compute component-appropriate metrics: Normalized Edit Distance for text and reading order, TEDS and Edit Distance for tables (with LaTeX→HTML conversion), and CDM/BLEU/Edit Distance for formulas; the repo also upgrades to a hybrid matching mode to reduce biases from Unicode vs LaTeX outputs.
paper_title: OmniDocBench: Benchmarking Diverse PDF Document Parsing with Comprehensive Annotations

problem: Practitioners lack clear, apples-to-apples comparisons of pipeline tools, expert VLMs, and general VLMs across diverse document types and attributes.
method: Systematically benchmark representative systems (e.g., MinerU, Marker, Mathpix; GOT-OCR, Nougat; GPT-4o, Qwen2-VL, InternVL2) using OmniDocBench at end-to-end and single-task levels. Report per-type and attribute-based results showing: pipelines excel on standard/dense layouts (e.g., newspapers) and component tasks (text/formula/table), while VLMs generalize better to notes/slides and are more robust under visual degradations (fuzzy scans, watermarks, colorful backgrounds). All models degrade on multi-column/complex layouts, and English pages generally score higher than Chinese—highlighting design trade-offs and future directions.
paper_title: OmniDocBench: Benchmarking Diverse PDF Document Parsing with Comprehensive Annotations

problem: 3D object detectors on point clouds often fail to explicitly model relationships among local point sets and to integrate global context, limiting accuracy when using sparse LiDAR data.
method: SVGA-Net introduces a voxel-graph network that partitions space via iterative farthest point sampling into fixed-radius spherical voxels, then constructs a local complete graph within each sphere and a global KNN graph over voxel centers. Local attention scores are softmax-normalized to aggregate features across all nodes in a sphere, while global features are obtained by a 3-layer MLP on voxel centers and refined via KNN attention (with K=3 found effective). The local and global attention factors jointly update point features through stacked point-attention layers, followed by per-voxel max pooling to produce compact features for detection.
paper_title: SVGA-Net: Sparse Voxel-Graph Attention Network for 3D Object Detection from Point Clouds

problem: Regressing accurate 3D bounding boxes from sparse point features requires effective multi-scale feature fusion, which many pipelines lack.
method: The sparse-to-dense regression (SDR) module builds three downsampling conv blocks (b1–b3), upsamples higher-level features and concatenates them with lower-level features to align resolutions, producing dense maps F1–F3. It then fuses each original sparse map bi with its corresponding dense Fi via element-wise addition, and concatenates the fused maps followed by a 3×3 conv to obtain a final feature Fs for classification and 3D box regression. Ablations show SDR outperforms variants that remove the cross-scale concatenation (SR) or the element-wise fusion (DR).
paper_title: SVGA-Net: Sparse Voxel-Graph Attention Network for 3D Object Detection from Point Clouds

problem: Extreme single-image super-resolution (×16) must recover fine, perceptually convincing textures while keeping model complexity manageable.
method: Embed multi-scale Receptive Field Blocks (RFB) into an ESRGAN-based generator by stacking Residual of Receptive Field Dense Blocks (RRFDBs)—8 RRFDBs, each containing 5 RFBs—in a dedicated Trunk-RFB. RFB uses multi-branch, small asymmetric kernels (1×1, 1×3, 3×1) with dilated convolutions to simulate varying receptive field sizes, capturing detailed line-like features while reducing parameters and time complexity. To better fit SR, all BN layers in RFB are removed and Leaky ReLU is used (with ReLU in branches). Ablations show RFB yields finer, smoother hair/fur textures compared to removing it.
paper_title: Perceptual Extreme Super Resolution Network with Receptive Field Block

problem: Common upsampling choices either blur details (nearest neighbor) or introduce oversharp artifacts (sub-pixel), hindering perceptual quality-efficiency balance.
method: Alternate Nearest Neighbor Interpolation (NNI) and Sub-pixel Convolution (SPC) in the upsampling module, placing an RFB after each to propagate spatial-to-depth and depth-to-spatial transformations, improving information communication between space and depth. This design reduces parameters/time (via SPC) while preserving natural detail (via NNI+RFB). Ablation results show only NNI is blurry and only SPC is oversharp with artifacts, while the alternating scheme produces the most realistic textures.
paper_title: Perceptual Extreme Super Resolution Network with Receptive Field Block

problem: GAN-based SR models can be noisy and unstable across diverse images; improving robustness without sacrificing perceptual quality is challenging.
method: Fuse parameters of the top 10 GAN-based checkpoints (selected by perceptual performance) into a single ensemble model by averaging, explicitly avoiding any PSNR-oriented model blending (unlike ESRGAN’s PSNR+GAN fusion). This ensemble reduces reconstruction noise and improves robustness across test images; using many more models slightly reduces noise but harms perceptual quality, making 10 the chosen balance.
paper_title: Perceptual Extreme Super Resolution Network with Receptive Field Block

problem: One-shot keypoint extraction methods typically assume one-to-one correspondences and thus fail to recover duplicated keypoints across multiple object instances in a query image, lacking instance awareness.
method: Learn from a single support image (one-shot) both keypoint prototypes (per designated keypoint) and edge prototypes (feature distributions along inter-keypoint edges) using off-the-shelf ViT features. In a query image, compute a support–query similarity matrix and extract Best-Prototype Pairs (BPP) to allow one-to-many correspondences, gathering candidate keypoints per identity (including adjacent support pixels and NMS with a weak threshold). Build a graph over candidates and compute edge similarity to edge prototypes by averaging ViT features over NSEG=8 segments; prune edges via a weak threshold (e.g., τ_E≈0.3) and pairwise comparisons among edges sharing a vertex and identity, splitting the graph into subgraphs that correspond to object instances, yielding instance-aware keypoint groups.
paper_title: AnyOKP: One-Shot and Instance-Aware Object Keypoint Extraction with Pretrained ViT

problem: Off-the-shelf pretrained ViT features, while transferable, need adaptation for robust keypoint description under domain shift and viewpoint changes without task-specific training, as ViTs lack strong local 2D pattern encoding.
method: Introduce training-free feature enhancement: (1) Objectness attention by computing pixel-wise mean absolute activation, min-max normalizing, then applying sigmoid scaling with α=5 to suppress low-activation background features—leveraging DINO-ViT’s emergent foreground activation; (2) Neighborhood binning that concatenates each pixel’s features with those of 8 immediate neighbors and 8 spaced neighbors from an average-pooled map, expanding channels to 17×D to inject local structure. Empirically, NB boosts recall/precision across backbones; for DINO-ViTb-p8, adding OA increases mean keypoint recall by 0.013 with a negligible precision drop of 0.002, and the full pipeline attains mean KP P=0.984 and INS R/P=0.964/0.985 on eight robot-collected categories.
paper_title: AnyOKP: One-Shot and Instance-Aware Object Keypoint Extraction with Pretrained ViT

problem: Evaluating how different camera and LiDAR placements/parameters affect 3D object detection is costly and slow, and existing works lack a unified criterion that applies to both sensors in fusion settings.
method: Introduce a unified, information-theoretic surrogate metric that predicts perception performance from ground-truth 3D bounding boxes without re-collecting data or retraining models. First, construct a Probabilistic Occupancy Grid (POG) over ROI voxels from dataset bounding boxes. Model both sensors via ray casting (LiDAR as rotating, vertically distributed rays; camera as rays from resized image pixels) and use Bresenham’s line algorithm to identify voxels traversed by each sensor’s rays. Compute conditional POG and entropies to obtain per-sensor information gain (S-MIG) and combine camera/LiDAR via a λ-weighted unified metric (S-MS). This metric correlates with mAP across fusion detectors and object classes, enabling rapid configuration evaluation.
paper_title: Influence of Camera-LiDAR Configuration on 3D Object Detection for Autonomous Driving

problem: There is no practical, systematic pipeline to quantify the impact of realistic camera–LiDAR configuration choices on multi-sensor 3D detection performance across modern fusion algorithms.
method: Build an automated CARLA-based framework to generate nuScenes-style datasets under multiple realistic LiDAR placements (Center, Pyramid, Line, Trapezoid) and camera FOV setups (Wide vs. Narrow), then train/evaluate representative fusion methods (Transfusion, BEVFusion, PointAugmenting) with nuScenes mAP. Experiments show configuration alone can change mAP by up to 30%; Wide cameras generally outperform Narrow by up to 10%, and Trapezoid LiDAR often yields the best results. Detection performance increases with the proposed S-MS metric, validating its predictive power for configuration assessment.
paper_title: Influence of Camera-LiDAR Configuration on 3D Object Detection for Autonomous Driving

problem: Practitioners need to select an ensemble of pre-trained source models that will transfer well to a target vision task without exhaustively fine-tuning all combinations. Prior ensemble transferability metrics largely rely on classifier outputs, ignore latent-space domain mismatch, and neglect inter-model agreement, leading to unreliable ensemble selection.
method: Proposes OSBORN, a transferability metric for ensembles that jointly accounts for (i) domain difference via 1-Wasserstein (Earth Mover’s) distance between source and target latent feature distributions computed with Sinkhorn OT, (ii) task difference via conditional entropy of predicted source labels given target labels, where the joint and marginal distributions are derived from the OT coupling, and (iii) inter-model cohesiveness via a conditional-entropy-based disagreement term across models’ predictions. The three terms are combined (unweighted) into a single score where lower is better; target samples are forward-propagated through source models to compute all quantities, enabling domain- and task-agnostic ensemble selection and outperforming MS-LEEP/E-LEEP across classification, segmentation, and domain adaptation.
paper_title: Building a Winning Team: Selecting Source Model Ensembles using a Submodular Transferability Estimation Approach

problem: Finding the optimal subset of source models for an ensemble is combinatorial and NP-hard, and naïvely increasing ensemble size can hurt transfer performance due to negative transfer from weak models. A scalable, principled selection procedure with guarantees is needed.
method: Shows the OSBORN-derived scoring function is a submodular set function, enabling greedy maximization under a cardinality constraint. The algorithm precomputes pairwise domain (WD) and task (WT) differences per source-target pair, then iteratively adds the model with the largest marginal gain while updating the cohesion (WC) term, with computations parallelizable across models. This yields the classic 1−1/e approximation guarantee and empirically attains near-optimal ensemble accuracy (e.g., ~95% of exhaustive-optimal on tested datasets).
paper_title: Building a Winning Team: Selecting Source Model Ensembles using a Submodular Transferability Estimation Approach

problem: Long-range LiDAR 3D object detection needs to be both accurate and highly efficient; voxel/grid methods become computationally prohibitive over large areas and range-image-only methods struggle with occlusions, precise localization, and size estimation.
method: RSN introduces a two-stage, multi-view fusion pipeline. A lightweight U-Net operates directly on the high-resolution range image to learn semantic features and perform high-recall foreground segmentation using focal loss; points with score above a threshold γ are selected and their learned range-image features are carried forward. Only these selected points are dynamically voxelized and passed to a Sparse Point Feature Extraction (SPFE) backbone using 2D/3D sparse and submanifold convolutions, greatly reducing compute while improving feature quality. A modified CenterNet-style head on sparse voxels regresses boxes with a centerness heatmap, smooth L1/IoU losses, and heading bin loss, and selects outputs via sparse submanifold max pooling, eliminating NMS. The whole model is trained end-to-end, yielding state-of-the-art accuracy on WOD with low latency (e.g., CarS 3f at 15.5 ms) and >60 FPS over a 150 m × 150 m region.
paper_title: RSN: Range Sparse Net for Efficient, Accurate LiDAR 3D Object Detection

problem: Leveraging temporal context for range image–based LiDAR detection is difficult because naive frame stacking is degraded by ego-motion and quantization errors, and prior range-image methods are not temporal-friendly.
method: Temporal RSN processes each frame with the same RIFE branch to segment foreground and extract range-image features, then transforms the selected points from all past frames into the latest frame to remove ego-motion. During SPFE, it augments each point with per-frame voxel statistics and a time delta to avoid mixing frames during voxelization, and merges all points for the same sparse backbone and detection head. At inference, past range features and segmentation outputs are reused so temporal models incur negligible extra cost versus single-frame. This strategy consistently boosts accuracy (e.g., CarS 1f 3D AP L1 70.5 → 74.8; PedS 1f 69.6 → 75.2) with similar latency (e.g., 15.5 ms, 14.4 ms).
paper_title: RSN: Range Sparse Net for Efficient, Accurate LiDAR 3D Object Detection

problem: Monocular 3D object detection suffers from inaccurate depth due to the ill-posed 2D–3D projection and the indirect link between predicted 2D cues and final depth, which leads to localization errors especially under occlusion and textureless regions.
method: MonoJSG reformulates depth estimation as proposal refinement using a joint semantic-and-geometric cost. It learns dense normalized object coordinates (per-pixel NOCS) and extracts object-level semantic features via RoIAlign. For each proposal and candidate depth, it reprojects the NOCS to image space to obtain projected locations and samples features at both source and projected points, forming a 4D cost volume by concatenating semantic features and 2D coordinates for source vs. projected points (W×H×D×2(C+2)). A refinement network with 2D and 3D convolutions aggregates the volume along spatial and depth dimensions, produces a softmax distribution over sampled depths, and computes the expectation to output the refined depth under direct depth supervision.
paper_title: MonoJSG: Joint Semantic and Geometric Cost Volume for Monocular 3D Object Detection

problem: A fixed, uniformly sampled depth grid is either too small to cover the true depth under varying uncertainty or too large and computationally expensive, hurting both accuracy and runtime.
method: MonoJSG builds an adaptive cost volume whose depth sampling range is determined per proposal by the predicted depth uncertainty σz from the proposal stage. With a fixed number of candidates D, the grid size Δz is set proportional to σz, balancing coverage and efficiency. This adaptive sampling consistently outperforms uniform sampling at the same D (e.g., on KITTI validation, adaptive > uniform for D=8 and D=32) while maintaining real-time inference.
paper_title: MonoJSG: Joint Semantic and Geometric Cost Volume for Monocular 3D Object Detection

problem: Sparse geometric constraints (e.g., edges or a few keypoints) provide weak, indirectly optimized cues for depth, leading to failures when keypoints are occluded or poorly localized.
method: The detector adds a branch to estimate per-pixel normalized object coordinates, supervised by LiDAR correspondences where available and by an unsupervised reprojection loss otherwise. These dense NOCS estimates enable pixel-level 2D–3D constraints: for each candidate depth, NOCS are transformed to camera and projected to image space, yielding geometric discrepancies (pi vs. p̂i) and enabling robust semantic comparisons (F(pi) vs. F(p̂i)) within the cost volume used by the refinement network.
paper_title: MonoJSG: Joint Semantic and Geometric Cost Volume for Monocular 3D Object Detection

problem: Many self-supervised pretext tasks (e.g., jigsaw, rotation) encourage representations that covary with the applied image transformation, which is undesirable for semantic recognition where invariance is preferred.
method: PIRL learns invariance to pretext transformations by maximizing the similarity between an image representation and the representation of its transformed counterpart, while contrasting against other images. It uses a contrastive NCE objective with cosine similarity and a large set of negatives maintained via a memory bank that stores moving-average features for all images, enabling many negatives without huge batches. Two projection heads are used: f(·) on the original image features and g(·) on transformed-image features; the final loss is a convex combination of two NCE terms controlled by λ, where λ=0 recovers NPID, and λ>0 explicitly enforces pretext invariance and stabilizes updates via similarity to the memory representation. (See Sections 2.1–2.3, Fig. 3, and Eq. 5.)
paper_title: Self-Supervised Learning of Pretext-Invariant Representations

problem: Jigsaw-style pretext learning traditionally predicts the patch permutation, which forces covariance to permutations and limits the number of permutations due to a growing output space.
method: PIRL adapts Jigsaw by computing a patchwise representation g(v_{I_t}) for the transformed image (nine res5 patch features → 128-D per patch → concatenation → linear projection) and aligning it with a holistic image representation f(v_I) via the contrastive loss, instead of predicting the permutation. Because PIRL never outputs permutation labels, it can leverage all 9! possible permutations, strengthening invariance. This PIRL-Jigsaw setup yields state-of-the-art transfer: it substantially improves linear ImageNet accuracy over Jigsaw/NPID++ and even surpasses supervised ImageNet pretraining for VOC detection (e.g., AP_all 54.0 vs 52.6). (See Sec. 2.3, lines 85–86; Sec. 4.2 on |T|; Tables 1–2.)
paper_title: Self-Supervised Learning of Pretext-Invariant Representations

problem: It is unclear whether invariance to pretext transformations generalizes beyond a single task and whether combining transformations helps.
method: PIRL applies the same invariance principle to Rotation (T = {0°, 90°, 180°, 270°}) and to a combined Rotation+Jigsaw pipeline by aligning original and transformed representations with the contrastive loss. PIRL (Rotation) markedly outperforms rotation prediction (e.g., +11.3% ImageNet top-1), and combining Rotation+Jigsaw further improves transfer performance compared to either alone, demonstrating PIRL’s generality and composability across pretext transformations. (See Sec. 4.3 and Table 5.)
paper_title: Self-Supervised Learning of Pretext-Invariant Representations

problem: Instance segmentation methods that process objects independently produce inconsistent masks under mutual occlusion, and fully supervised pixel annotations are costly. There is a need to jointly model multiple objects and occluders while training with only bounding boxes.
method: Extend Compositional Networks to a multi-object generative model that includes N object models plus an outlier (occluder) model, with a per-pixel visibility constraint that only one object (or outlier) can be active (∑_n z_i,n = 1). The model produces per-pixel foreground, context, and occlusion likelihood maps and amodal masks, and is trained weakly using bounding-box-based segmentation to learn priors and mixture components. This generalization enables consistent, occlusion-aware instance segmentation from bounding box supervision only.
paper_title: Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion

problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
method: Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.
paper_title: Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion

problem: Evaluating robustness to occlusion requires precise amodal annotations and controlled, diverse occlusion scenarios, including cases with unknown occluders, which are hard to obtain from human-labeled datasets.
method: Construct an Occlusion Challenge dataset by superimposing segmented, fully visible KITTI objects onto random backgrounds to create photo-realistic occlusions with pixel-accurate amodal masks. It provides three controlled scenarios: pairwise occlusion (2 objects), complex multi-object occlusion (4 objects with varied inter-occlusions), and mixed occlusions including previously unseen occluder classes. This dataset enables targeted evaluation across occlusion levels and scenarios.
paper_title: Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion

problem: Vanilla self-attention has quadratic cost in the number of tokens and uses fixed Q/K/V dimensions tied to the input, which limits context-oriented generalization and produces dispersed, non-object-centric activations.
method: Scalable Self-Attention (SSA) introduces two scaling factors to decouple and resize Q, K, and V: a spatial factor rn that reduces the number of tokens (aggregating redundant tokens) and a channel factor rc that expands channel capacity for richer representations. Concretely, f_q scales Q’s channels from C to C·rc, f_k scales K’s spatial dimension from N to N·rn and channels to C·rc, and f_v scales V’s spatial dimension from N to N·rn; these are implemented via convolutions plus linear projections, and output dimensions are restored for residuals. This reduces unnecessary intermediate multiplications and yields a complexity O(N·N·rn·C + N·N·rn·C·rc) that is linear in input size as used, while achieving contiguous, object-sensitive features and maintaining contextual cues.
paper_title: ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer

problem: Window-based self-attention (WSA) limits interaction across non-overlapping windows, leading to isolated activations and a lack of global receptive field within a single layer.
method: Interactive Window-based Self-Attention (IWSA) augments WSA with a Local Interactive Module (LIM) that re-merges the discrete value tensors from each window into a full-resolution map and applies a depth-wise 3×3 convolution (with zero padding) to aggregate information across adjacent windows. The interacted feature is added to the WSA output, enabling inter-window communication and a global receptive field in one layer with negligible extra cost; the depth-wise conv also implicitly encodes positional information. Ablations show consistent gains over plain WSA and shifted-window alternatives on ImageNet and ADE20K.
paper_title: ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer

problem: Designing a general-purpose vision backbone that balances accuracy and efficiency across classification, detection, and segmentation while modeling long-range dependencies without deep stacking.
method: ScalableViT is a hierarchical ViT backbone that alternates IW-MSA and SSA blocks within each stage and inserts a positional encoding generator (PEG) at the front of stages. This topology leverages SSA’s context-preserving scaling and IWSA’s inter-window interaction to capture long-range dependencies in single layers, improving the accuracy–cost trade-off. Empirically, ScalableViT-S improves ImageNet-1K top-1 by +1.4% over Twins-SVT-S and +1.8% over Swin-T at similar FLOPs, and yields substantial gains on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation.
paper_title: ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer

problem: Diffusion-pretrained backbones have strong generative priors, but diffusion-based perception lacks a principled prompting strategy; unaligned prompts (e.g., averaged EOS embeddings) misalign text and image features via cross-attention, hurting tasks like semantic segmentation and depth estimation.
method: Text-Aligned Diffusion Perception (TADP) conditions Stable Diffusion with automated per-image captions generated by BLIP-2, aligning text to each input image. It runs a single reverse-diffusion step ϵθ(z0, 0, C), concatenates cross-attention maps and multi-scale U-Net features, and feeds them to a task-specific head, with latent scaling applied to image latents. Compared to prior averaging of EOS tokens, this per-image caption conditioning yields sharper, targeted cross-attention and improves performance across benchmarks (e.g., VOC mIoU increases by ~4 over VPD(LS), ADE20K improves, and NYUv2 RMSE drops from 0.235 to 0.225). Ablations show longer captions help and that nouns are largely sufficient, indicating the mechanism benefits from precise, image-aligned semantics.
paper_title: Text-image Alignment for Diffusion-based Perception

problem: Cross-domain generalization (e.g., day→night, photo→watercolor/comic) remains challenging for diffusion-based perception when only source-domain labels are available and target labels are absent.
method: TADP extends alignment to the cross-domain setting by augmenting source captions with target-aware modifiers: (i) null (no modifier), (ii) simple style strings, (iii) learned tokens via Textual Inversion, and (iv) DreamBooth personalization of the diffusion backbone. At test time, GPT-3.5 is used to strip target-style leakage from BLIP captions before re-applying the same modifier, ensuring train–test caption format consistency. Better text–target alignment and personalization consistently improve transfer, achieving new SOTA on cross-domain segmentation (Cityscapes→Dark Zurich-val and Nighttime Driving) and cross-domain detection (VOC→Watercolor2K), with Textual Inversion yielding the strongest average gains and off-target/nearby-domain modifiers degrading performance.
paper_title: Text-image Alignment for Diffusion-based Perception

problem: It is unclear how specific prompt design choices affect diffusion-based perception features and outputs, leading to suboptimal use of the text interface.
method: The paper systematically studies prompting: averaged EOS-token embeddings (used in prior work) produce diffuse, off-target cross-attention and underperform; replacing them with actual class-name tokens helps, while appending off-target classes or omitting present classes degrades segmentation. An oracle with exact present-class captions provides a much higher upper bound, and ablations show recall of present classes matters more than precision for segmentation quality. The study also confirms that proper latent scaling of image latents is crucial, and that BLIP-2 captions approximate high-precision/medium-recall behavior that improves downstream performance.
paper_title: Text-image Alignment for Diffusion-based Perception

problem: Lightweight models deployed on devices degrade under continual distribution shifts and cannot be frequently updated due to limited on-device compute and uplink bandwidth.
method: Introduce Cloud-Device Collaborative Continual Adaptation with Uncertainty Guided Sampling (UGS) that selects and uploads only the most environment-specific/OOD samples. UGS estimates uncertainty via Monte Carlo Dropout by computing the variance of repeated detection class probabilities and transmits samples whose uncertainty exceeds a threshold. The cloud updates a teacher–student detector on these challenging samples and returns adapted parameters, achieving similar or better performance with far less data (e.g., outperforming full-data training using only ~42–50% of samples) and beating random selection while reducing bandwidth.
paper_title: Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world

problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
method: Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.
paper_title: Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world

problem: Existing adversarial attacks on object detectors largely target accuracy, or when targeting latency, rely on complex IoU-based objectives that are computationally and memory intensive—especially problematic on edge devices. The key open question is which model factors most effectively increase inference time with minimal attack cost.
method: Overload analyzes NMS time complexity and shows latency is dominated by the number of objects fed into NMS rather than the number of surviving boxes. Based on this, it formulates a simple white-box objective that maximizes per-detection objectness confidence and class probabilities via a monotonic increasing function, inflating the number of pre-NMS objects without tracking pairwise IoUs. The attack is optimized with PGD-style updates under a small perturbation budget and is NMS-agnostic. Empirically, it generates >20k ghost objects and increases end-to-end latency by ~11–13× on Jetson NX, while using less memory/time than Daedalus and Phantom Sponge (e.g., ~3 min for 16 images at 7.5 GB vs. ~145 min for 10 images at 8.5 GB).
paper_title: Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices

problem: Even with a strong objective, adversarial optimization can over-focus on regions already dense with detections, limiting the total number of ghost objects and thus the latency impact.
method: Introduce spatial attention: tile the image into an m×m grid and adaptively weight each grid inversely to its current object density, so optimization emphasizes low-density regions. The weighted gradient step (PGD) is applied each iteration, continually updating grid weights from the detector’s intermediate outputs. This simple, gradient-free weighting boosts object counts by ~2,000+ over vanilla PGD and works best around a 5×5 grid, further increasing NMS load and latency.
paper_title: Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices

problem: Visual Prompt Tuning (VPT) underperforms with self-supervised ViTs because prompt tokens interact with all blocks uniformly, while the blocks carrying task-relevant information vary by SSL pretraining and downstream task; exhaustively choosing optimal insertion blocks is costly.
method: Gated Prompt Tuning introduces a learnable scalar gate per ViT block that convexly combines the prompt representation before and after each block (g_l * prompt_after + (1−g_l) * prompt_before), with a single prompt set prepended once like VPT-shallow. This learns to selectively carry forward only the influence of task-relevant blocks, yielding a selective aggregation of block outputs at the last layer and enabling sample-specific, task-relevant instructions. The method keeps the backbone frozen and trains only prompts, gates, and head, outperforming VPT baselines on FGVC (e.g., MAE 73.39% vs. 72.02% VPT-deep), VTAB-1K (MoCo v3 65.80% vs. 61.22% VPT-deep), and ADE20K (MAE 38.44 mIoU vs. 37.76 VPT-deep).
paper_title: Improving Visual Prompt Tuning for Self-supervised Vision Transformers

problem: Prompt tuning needs stronger control over how pretrained self-attention distributes focus so that prompts can better encode task-specific instructions in SSL ViTs.
method: Adaptive Attention Shaping adds a learnable temperature per block that rescales the attention softmax (sharpening or smoothing) to directly modulate each block’s self-attention during transfer. Combined with Gated Prompt Tuning, this further improves adaptation: attention maps shift toward more discriminative regions, and ablations show consistent gains when adding learnable temperatures on top of gating.
paper_title: Improving Visual Prompt Tuning for Self-supervised Vision Transformers

problem: Medical image segmentation methods typically use discrete pixel-wise decoders that are spatially inflexible, can introduce artifacts at new resolutions, and struggle with precise boundary delineation. Prior implicit methods often rely on weaker encoders and ignore frequency-domain cues that are critical for boundary accuracy, limiting cross-domain generalization.
method: I-MedSAM adapts SAM to medical images with parameter-efficient fine-tuning using two adapters: (1) a novel frequency adapter that extracts and injects high-frequency information (amplitude spectrum from FFT) across ViT blocks to sharpen boundaries, and (2) LoRA applied to SAM’s attention projections (Q and V) to efficiently adapt the image encoder. The model takes an image and a coarse bounding-box prompt, freezes most of SAM, and trains only the adapters, prompt encoder, and INRs, achieving strong performance with just 1.6M trainable parameters. Ablations show amplitude > phase for the frequency adapter and overall boundary quality gains (Tab. 6), while overall results outperform both discrete and prior implicit baselines (Tab. 1).
paper_title: I-MedSAM: Implicit Medical Image Segmentation with Segment Anything

problem: Existing implicit segmentation approaches often use one-stage decoders and random/naive coordinate sampling, which fail to focus refinement on hard pixels and limit efficiency and accuracy, especially under cross-resolution or cross-domain shifts.
method: I-MedSAM employs a two-stage, coarse-to-fine implicit neural representation (INR) decoder. It concatenates interpolated SAM image/prompt features with high-frequency positional encodings of grid coordinates, then: (i) a shallow coarse INR predicts an initial map and latent features; (ii) uncertainty-guided sampling computes per-pixel variance via MC-Dropout over T stochastic passes and selects the Top-K most uncertain points; (iii) a deeper fine INR refines only these uncertain points and merges with the coarse predictions. This design improves boundary accuracy and robustness to resolution/domain shifts, with concrete gains across cross-resolution and cross-domain tests (Sec. 3.4; Impl: Top-K=12.5%, dropout=0.5; Tabs. 2–4, 7).
paper_title: I-MedSAM: Implicit Medical Image Segmentation with Segment Anything

problem: There is no large-scale, publicly available benchmark to develop and evaluate crowd density estimation, localization, and tracking algorithms specifically for drone-captured videos, which involve small object scales, viewpoint/scale variations, and background clutter.
method: The authors release DroneCrowd, a drone-captured video benchmark with 112 HD video clips (33,600 frames) across 70 scenarios, annotated with 4.8M head points and 20,800 trajectories plus video-level attributes (illumination, scale, density). They define unified evaluation protocols: MAE/MSE for density estimation, L-mAP at multiple distance thresholds for localization, and T-mAP at multiple matching thresholds for tracking. This is, to their knowledge, the largest and most thoroughly annotated dataset for drone-based density estimation, localization, and tracking, enabling standardized, challenging evaluation.
paper_title: Detection, Tracking, and Counting Meets Drones in Crowds: A Benchmark

problem: Jointly counting, localizing, and temporally associating people in dense, drone-captured crowds is difficult due to tiny targets and inconsistent motion cues, where exploiting spatio-temporal relationships among neighboring objects is crucial.
method: They propose STNNet, a Space-Time Neighbor-Aware Network that takes pairs of frames and jointly predicts density maps, point localizations, and motion offsets. A Siamese VGG-16 backbone with U-Net-style multi-scale fusion and a correlation operation across frames provides temporally coherent features; a localization subnet uses per-pixel proposals with classification+regression and multi-scale channel/spatial attention, followed by NMS. An association subnet selects the top-M proposals and, together with multi-scale correlation features, applies stacked PointConv/MLPs over nearest neighbors to regress motion offsets bidirectionally (cycle). A neighboring context loss, composed of a temporal prediction constraint and a relation constraint, penalizes changes in relative positions among nearby objects across frames to guide offset learning. The model is trained end-to-end with a multi-task loss, and predicted offsets are fed to standard association (min-cost flow or Social-LSTM) to form long trajectories.
paper_title: Detection, Tracking, and Counting Meets Drones in Crowds: A Benchmark

problem: Beekeepers need a non-invasive, timely way to monitor hive activity, pollen foraging, and mite infestation; existing manual methods are labor-intensive, sometimes destructive, and not real-time.
method: Deploy a low-cost, edge vision pipeline on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano using two YOLOv7-Tiny models: one for honey bee detection/tracking on 720p video, and a second for small-object attributes (pollen, mite) on cropped bee images. The pipeline saves full-body bee crops when crossing predefined triggers to improve downstream attribute detection, and converts models to ONNX then TensorRT to halve per-frame latency from ~56 ms to ~27 ms, enabling near real-time 5-minute segments processed concurrently with capture. The bee detector attains F1=0.95 with precision/recall of 0.981, and the pollen classifier achieves F1≈0.832 on five 5-minute videos; the attribute model is trained at 64×64 input to better capture small features. (Evidence: model choice and training/resizing [121, 125, 131], trigger-based cropping and two-stage flow [185–186], ONNX/TensorRT speedup 56→27 ms and throughput timing [127–129, 137], metrics [303–315, 474]).
paper_title: IntelliBeeHive: An Automated Honey Bee, Pollen, and Varroa Destructor Monitoring System

problem: Counting bidirectional bee traffic at the hive entrance under occlusions and limited compute requires a robust, lightweight tracker that maps detections to behavioral states.
method: Use YOLOv7-Tiny detections to compute per-bee midpoints, associate across frames via nearest-neighbor within a 50-pixel tolerance, and classify motion using two fixed Y-thresholds on 640×420 video (140 px ‘Arriving’ and 280 px ‘Leaving’) with four statuses: New, Deck, Arriving, Leaving. The algorithm logs size once at trigger crossing and saves crops for later attribute inference; it runs on 5-minute clips with processing overlapping capture for near-live updates. Evaluated on five 1-minute videos, the tracker reaches 96.28% accuracy vs. manual counts; the authors diagnose ‘Deck’ mispairings from fast motion and note higher frame rate as a remedy. (Evidence: midpoint and 50-pixel association [141–147], thresholds/status logic and resolution [149–151], overlapping processing [137], evaluation protocol and accuracy [367–399, 474], failure analysis [415–416]).
paper_title: IntelliBeeHive: An Automated Honey Bee, Pollen, and Varroa Destructor Monitoring System

problem: Feature- or prediction-level knowledge distillation methods designed for images transfer poorly to point-cloud 3D detectors because point clouds are sparse, irregular, and lack explicit topology. Students need to inherit a teacher’s understanding of local geometric structure rather than only global backbone features.
method: Introduce local distillation that explicitly distills local geometric structure. For the top-N sampled voxels/points, build KNN neighborhoods and encode them with dynamic graph convolution (DGCNN-style) after fusing the neighborhood with the center feature, producing graph features for teacher and student. Train with a feature-matching loss on these graph features; the graph conv adapters are learned jointly with the student and discarded at inference, making the approach lightweight and architecture-agnostic. (Supported by: Section 1 ‘Local Distillation’; Sec. 3.2 ‘Extracting Local Geometric Information’ incl. KNN graphs and dynamic graph conv; note on discarding layers at inference.)
paper_title: PointDistiller: Structured Knowledge Distillation Towards Efficient and Compact 3D Detection

problem: In point clouds, most voxels contain very few (often single, noisy) points, and many points are uninformative. Uniformly distilling all locations wastes student capacity and reduces KD effectiveness on 3D detection.
method: Use importance-aware distillation to focus on informative regions. Define per-voxel importance as the number of points inside the voxel and per-point importance as the teacher feature’s channel-wise maximum (a saliency score). Sample the top-N voxels/points by these scores for distillation, then reweight the KD loss with a softmax-temperature weighting derived from importance (for voxels) or from the teacher’s graph features/saliency (for points), so higher-importance neighborhoods contribute more to the loss. This sampling-and-reweighting works for both voxel- and point-based detectors and plugs directly into standard training. (Supported by: Sec. 3.2 ‘Sampling TopN To-be-distilled Voxels (Points)’; Sec. 3.2 ‘Reweighting Knowledge Distillation Loss’ with φ=softmax(·/τ); Fig. 2 and description on sparsity.)
paper_title: PointDistiller: Structured Knowledge Distillation Towards Efficient and Compact 3D Detection

problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
method: Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)
paper_title: S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging

problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
method: Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.
paper_title: Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty
