problem: Naively computing a pooled target across a large stack of images per mask is computationally impractical, limiting the feasibility of learning from a denoised semantic target at scale.
method: Provide a theoretical analysis that upper-bounds the distance between a sample’s semantic feature and the (intractable) pooled estimate by a weighted sum of pairwise distances within the stack. This yields a practical objective that only requires two images per iteration: enforcing pairwise feature consistency suffices to approximate learning from the pooled, denoised semantic representation. In S2S2, this results in an efficient training loss term computed on two images per mask (typically the original plus one generated sample). (Paper: Sec. 3 “Practical Objective for Semantic Stacking”; lines 75–93; Appendix Fig. 7 and lines 416, 426)
paper_title: S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging

problem: Latent feature collapse and prototype collapse reduce separability between in-distribution and out-of-distribution samples and degrade uncertainty estimation.
method: Augment training with two regularizers: (1) a prototype dissimilarity loss that pushes learned prototypes apart to prevent collapse, and (2) an entropy-like loss over the softmax of prototype similarities that encourages each sample to relate to multiple prototypes rather than a single one. Additionally, train g_unc to predict the model’s normalized task loss using a binary cross-entropy objective, linking the prototype-based embedding to uncertainty. This combination yields more discriminative latent representations (as shown on the two-moons toy example) and improves OOD/uncertainty quality; an optional second-stage training on synthesized outliers can further bias g_unc toward epistemic uncertainty.
paper_title: Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty

problem: Constructing meaningful stacks that capture clinically relevant variability without brittle, domain-specific heuristics is challenging, especially across heterogeneous modalities.
method: Construct semantic stacks by generating multiple images per ground-truth mask using a conditional generative model: fine-tune Stable Diffusion 2.x with ControlNet conditioned on segmentation maps, then sample n images (n=16 in experiments) per mask. This data-driven synthesis captures organ-level variations beyond simple photometric transforms, aligning the mean semantic feature of the generated set with the ground-truth and reducing variance as n grows. Empirically, photometric-only variants with the same consistency loss underperform, underscoring the importance of generative semantic stacks. (Paper: Sec. 3 “Constructing Semantic Stack”; lines 97–104; Appendix C lines 404–414, 408; Table 14 lines 533–541)
paper_title: S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging

problem: On-device training of CNNs is bottlenecked by the high compute of backpropagating through convolution layers and the large memory needed to store full-resolution activations for gradient computation.
method: Introduce gradient filtering that approximates the output gradient gy by partitioning it into r×r patches and replacing each patch with its mean, yielding a structured gradient ˜gy with far fewer unique spatial elements. Replace the rotated kernel and activations with their spatial sums (˜θ′ and ˜x). This turns the gx computation from a convolution into element-wise multiplication and channel-wise addition with ˜θ′, and simplifies gθ via Frobenius products between ˜x and ˜gy. The approach reduces FLOPs roughly by 1/r^2 from fewer unique elements and by an additional factor of (WθHθ) from avoiding convolution, and cuts activation memory by ≈(1−1/r^2) by storing only patch-summed ˜x; boundary effects are handled via sufficient padding. (Supported in Sec. 3.3–3.4, 4.2–4.3: lines 90–115, 182–196, 204.)
paper_title: Efficient On-device Training via Gradient Filtering

problem: Filtering gradients can introduce approximation noise that may harm convergence and accuracy; a guarantee is needed that errors will not accumulate during backpropagation.
method: Provide a frequency-domain analysis (Proposition 1) showing that if the DC component dominates the kernel spectrum, the SNR of the input gradient after filtering (˜gx) is at least as high as the SNR of the filtered output gradient (˜gy), implying the injected noise weakens as gradients propagate. Empirically verify the DC-dominance assumption across popular CNNs and observe accuracy correlates positively with SNR, explaining the limited accuracy loss under filtering. (Supported in Sec. 4.1 and 5.6: lines 122–176, 310–313; and SNR–accuracy link in lines 270–277.)
paper_title: Efficient On-device Training via Gradient Filtering

problem: Devising LLM agents that minimize computation/API cost while meeting a user-specified reliability target is challenging, especially when a small base model is inaccurate and poorly calibrated about its own uncertainty.
method: Formulate an agentic orchestration of a cheap base LLM and a stronger guide LLM: each round the base produces a reasoning trace and answer; the guide consumes the question + base trace to output a binary judgment (yes/no) and a corrected answer at low output-token cost. Define a conformal policy C(o) that maps observations to an action set {use base answer, use guide answer, next round}, inducing a prediction set over final answers by branching only on 'next round' (max 2^T rollouts). Impose a conformal coverage constraint: with high probability, if any action sequence can produce the true answer, then the prediction set contains it, and optimize expected API cost with an optional set-size penalty.
paper_title: Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents

problem: Directly learning set-valued (conformal) policies is combinatorial, and the distribution shift between behavior and target policies complicates reliable coverage calibration in sequential settings.
method: Introduce CCPO, which parameterizes a score policy π(a|o) and a threshold κ, and defines a stochastic surrogate S_{π,κ} that places uniform probability over the conformal action set C_{π,κ}(o). Train π with an actor–critic using V-trace off-policy corrections from π to S_{π,κ} with clipped importance weights, and perform a trust-region CPO update that optimizes advantages under a KL constraint while enforcing an upper-bounded coverage constraint; replace the non-differentiable indicator in C with a sigmoid softmask for stable gradients. Update κ online via decaying-step-size conformal prediction to track the target 1−α coverage, followed by a final batch calibration step; under mild assumptions this yields asymptotic 1−α coverage (and O(T^{-1/2+ξ}) coverage regret in adversarial streams) while minimizing cost.
paper_title: Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents

problem: Deep RL from pixels for long-horizon robotic manipulation suffers from sparse rewards and poor exploration, while behavior cloning typically needs many demonstrations and can overfit.
method: Introduce Adaptive Curriculum Generation from Demonstrations (ACGD), which bootstraps learning via reverse-trajectory sampling from a small set of demos (initially near the goal, then progressively earlier) and mixes these with regular randomized resets to avoid demo bias. Two difficulty coefficients for demo and regular resets (dd, dr) are adjusted online to keep observed success rates within a target interval [a,b], thereby automatically controlling start-state distance and task difficulty. The update rule (Algorithm 1) increments/decrements d based on whether the success rate is above/below [a,b], and selects reset type with probability p=0.5(srr + i/N), yielding faster, stable learning on sparse-reward tasks where PPO with sparse or shaped rewards fails.
paper_title: Adaptive Curriculum Generation from Demonstrations for Sim-to-Real Visuomotor Control

problem: Applying strong domain (visual/dynamics) randomization from the start can hinder convergence in sim training, yet sufficient randomization is needed for sim-to-real transfer of visuomotor policies.
method: Treat domain randomization and other environment/task parameters H as curriculum-controlled difficulty. Start with minimal randomization and linearly increase the variance of appearance and dynamics parameters as the adaptively tuned difficulty coefficients (dd, dr) rise to keep success rates within [a,b]. This unified control of demo start-state sampling and randomization levels learns when to increase randomization, improving training speed and enabling zero-shot transfer, achieving 85% (pick-and-stow) and 60% (block stacking) success on a real robot.
paper_title: Adaptive Curriculum Generation from Demonstrations for Sim-to-Real Visuomotor Control

problem: Multi-object navigation methods typically assume a fixed, pre-sequenced order of target classes, which is impractical in dynamic environments. The agent needs to adaptively decide which target to pursue next while minimizing travel and time.
method: Proposes SAM, a modular deep RL framework that selects long-term goals without computing a global sequence. It builds an allocentric semantic map from RGB-D using Mask R-CNN semantics and differentiable geometric mapping, then an encoder fuses the map, agent history, and a multi-goal one-hot target encoding. An actor-critic outputs a long-term goal every 25 timesteps to greedily reduce distance to all remaining targets, while a deterministic local planner (Fast Marching Method) executes low-level actions. Training uses clipped double Q-learning for the critic, shared conv features updated via the critic, replay buffer, and random shift augmentation of the semantic map.
paper_title: Sequence-Agnostic Multi-Object Navigation

problem: Naive extensions of ON rewards to MultiON either focus on a single goal or simply sum distance reductions, failing to encourage concurrent progress toward multiple targets and to penalize dithering.
method: Introduces a reward that drives sequence-agnostic behavior: (i) a sub-goal reward granted only when any target instance is localized (r_sub-goal = 2), (ii) a process reward scaled by α_process = 0.1 that, during training, uses ground-truth nearest-instance distances to each remaining class and adds reward proportional to the fraction of classes whose distance decreased at a timestep, and (iii) a small per-step penalty CNR = −0.01. This encourages greedy, concurrent advancement toward multiple targets without global sequence optimization and applies irrespective of the number of targets.
paper_title: Sequence-Agnostic Multi-Object Navigation

problem: Existing MultiON setups often use single-instance, artificial objects and fixed sequences, limiting realism and generalization.
method: Formulates and evaluates Sequence-Agnostic MultiON in photo-realistic Gibson scenes with naturally occurring objects and potentially multiple instances per class, training on 2–3 target classes and testing on held-out scenes. Defines Global-SPL (G-SPL) for MultiON as the ratio of the global shortest tour visiting one instance of each target to the actual path, and shows SAM significantly outperforms PSM and an extended SemExp baseline, e.g., ~50% reductions in timesteps and path length versus PSM.
paper_title: Sequence-Agnostic Multi-Object Navigation

problem: Single RL agents struggle in non-stationary, highly volatile crypto markets; prior hierarchical methods typically rely on trend alone and select a single agent per step, ignoring volatility and leading to biased decisions.
method: Decompose sequential market data into fixed-length chunks and label each chunk by two macro indicators—trend (slope from a low-pass-filtered linear fit) and volatility (average volatility)—then partition by quantiles to form six regimes (bull/flat/bear and high/medium/low volatility). Train a separate DDQN-based sub-agent on each labeled subset and select models by validation performance on the corresponding regime, yielding a pool of six specialized policies. This market decomposition enhances specialization and mitigates overfitting by exposing each agent to more homogeneous dynamics and explicit context during training.
paper_title: MacroHFT: Memory Augmented Context-aware Reinforcement Learning On High Frequency Trading

problem: HFT policies tend to overfit a few features and fail to adapt to recent context and the trader’s current position, hurting responsiveness and risk control at minute granularity.
method: Equip each sub-agent with a conditional adapter: embed recent context features and current position into a condition vector, then use adaptive LayerNorm (scale-and-shift from the condition) to modulate the single-state embedding before the dueling DDQN head. Optimize with TD error plus a KL term to dynamic-programming-derived optimal Q-values (Optimal Value Supervisor), stabilizing learning and anchoring action-values. This conditioning lets policies adjust to market context and holdings in real time, improving robustness and adaptability.
paper_title: MacroHFT: Memory Augmented Context-aware Reinforcement Learning On High Frequency Trading

problem: At the meta level, rewards for similar states can vary sharply and rare market shocks are hard to capture with standard replay, while selecting a single sub-agent yields one-sided behavior.
method: Train a hyper-agent that produces softmax weights over all sub-agents and forms a mixed action-value Q_hyper = Σ_i w_i Q_sub_i, with its own conditional adapter driven by slope and volatility indicators. Augment training with an episodic memory that stores recent hidden states, actions, and one-step Q targets; at each step, retrieve the top-m similar experiences (via L2-distance/kernel weighting) to compute an auxiliary value target added to the loss alongside TD and KL-to-demonstration terms. This memory-augmented mixture stabilizes Q estimates across similar contexts and enables rapid adaptation to transient, extreme market movements.
paper_title: MacroHFT: Memory Augmented Context-aware Reinforcement Learning On High Frequency Trading

problem: Outcome-only rewards for QA are sparse and noisy (e.g., string matching misses semantically correct variants), providing weak guidance to learn high-quality multi-step retrieval decisions.
method: R3-RAG designs a composite reward with (1) an outcome reward for answer correctness that combines string-match and model-based judgments to reduce noise (§3.3.1), and (2) a process reward via relevance-based document verification that, at each retrieval step, prompts an LLM to score the relevance between the question and each retrieved document on a 0–1 scale, leveraging full token-level interaction to shape retrieval quality (§3.3.1). A format-correctness reward and a trajectory-dependent scaling factor λ_T upweight process rewards when the final answer is correct and downweight them otherwise (§3.3.1). Training with PPO and a KL penalty (§3.3.2) yields measurable gains; ablations show removing the process reward drops average accuracy by ~1.4 points and removing all RL rewards by an additional ~3.6 points (Table 2).
paper_title: R3-RAG: Learning Step-by-Step Reasoning and Retrieval for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

problem: Diversity-driven RL ensembles can generalize to dynamics shifts but often produce unsafe or impractical behaviors because diversity is unconstrained and not focused on task-relevant patterns.
method: Diversity in Regulation (DiR) introduces a diversity objective based on disagreement between inverse dynamics models under partial state information. A customizable state filtration function f(s) removes selected state variables, and for each new policy an inverse dynamics model T_phi(a | f(s), f(s')) is learned. Training adds an intrinsic reward that increases the cross-entropy between the new policy’s actions and the inverse dynamics predictions of previously discovered policies on filtered state pairs, discouraging agreement on retained state aspects. Applying the filtration only to IDMs (not to the policy inputs) regulates which behavior dimensions diversify (e.g., leg motions), yielding feasible, pattern-targeted policies.
paper_title: Open-Ended Diverse Solution Discovery with Regulated Behavior Patterns for Cross-Domain Adaptation

problem: Simultaneously training diverse policy populations with pairwise constraints is computationally expensive and induces non-stationary diversity signals, hindering scalability and stability.
method: DiR adopts an open-ended, iterative procedure that trains a single policy at a time against the fixed set of previously trained policies via a soft-constrained Lagrangian objective with equal multipliers β=1/(k−1). It approximates policy-conditioned inverse dynamics with learned IDMs and computes a tractable diversity term as cross-entropy on collected transitions, converted into an intrinsic reward for PPO. This avoids O(M^2) pairwise computations, allows unbounded solution discovery, and yields more stationary intrinsic rewards from converged IDMs, improving stability and adaptation performance compared to methods like DvD and SMERL.
paper_title: Open-Ended Diverse Solution Discovery with Regulated Behavior Patterns for Cross-Domain Adaptation

problem: There is no principled control over how much and what type of diversity to induce, making it hard to balance exploration with practical feasibility.
method: DiR’s objective admits a conditional mutual-information view, maximizing I(a; z | f(s), f(s')) where z indexes policies. By designing f(s), users directly control the strength and locus of diversity—from no diversity (f(s)=s) to maximal diversity (f(s)=∅). In practice, filtering out leg-state variables focuses diversity on locomotion patterns, yielding distinct, consistent gaits and improved few-shot transfer under component damage, dynamics parameter shifts, and sensor failures across Ant, Walker, Hopper, and Minitaur.
paper_title: Open-Ended Diverse Solution Discovery with Regulated Behavior Patterns for Cross-Domain Adaptation

problem: Existing complexity measures for model-based RL in Mean-Field Games (MFGs), such as MBED, can be exponentially large even in tabular settings, leading to pessimistic sample-complexity bounds. A tighter notion is needed that reflects the effective single-agent nature once the mean-field density is fixed.
method: Introduce the Partial Model-Based Eluder Dimension (P-MBED), defined via partial ε-independence after adversarially fixing the density through ν_h^π(M)=µ_h^{π,M}, and take the worst case over steps and policies. This converts the mean-field model class into a family of single-agent model classes and measures their eluder complexity. The paper proves dim_PE(M,ε)≤|S||A| in tabular MFGs (while MBED can be Ω(exp(|S|))) and shows in linear decomposable MFGs that P-MBED scales with the state-action feature dimension (˜O(ẑd)).
paper_title: Model-Based RL for Mean-Field Games is not Statistically Harder than Single-Agent RL

problem: Design a sample-efficient exploration algorithm to find a Nash Equilibrium in unknown MFGs under basic realizability and Lipschitz assumptions, without relying on strong structural conditions (e.g., contractivity or monotonicity).
method: Propose MEBP, a model elimination algorithm that iteratively selects reference policies and invokes a single-agent ModelElim subroutine conditioned on induced densities to prune inconsistent models. A novel Bridge Policy is constructed via Policy-Aware Models (PAM) and a cover of the policy space to guarantee that each iteration either returns an ε-NE or halves the hypothesis class. The resulting sample complexity is polynomial in P-MBED and logarithmic in |M|, implying that learning NE in MFGs is no harder than solving O(log|M|) single-agent model elimination problems; for tabular MFGs, the bound is polynomial in |S|, |A|, and H and avoids the exponential (1+L_T)^H term seen in prior work.
paper_title: Model-Based RL for Mean-Field Games is not Statistically Harder than Single-Agent RL

problem: For heterogeneous populations (Multi-Type MFGs), fundamental sample complexity guarantees for learning Nash Equilibria with function approximation are largely unknown.
method: Reduce finding an NE in a Multi-Type MFG to finding a constrained NE in a lifted single-type MFG with a restricted policy space Π†, and define a Multi-Type P-MBED capturing the summed complexity across types. Using essentially the same elimination framework with constrained policies, the paper establishes the first sample-complexity upper bounds for MT-MFGs that are polynomial in the Multi-Type P-MBED (e.g., ˜O(∑w|S_w||A_w|) in tabular and ˜O(∑w d_w) in linear decomposable settings) and logarithmic in |M|.
paper_title: Model-Based RL for Mean-Field Games is not Statistically Harder than Single-Agent RL

problem: Multi-trait automated essay scoring is evaluated with quadratic weighted kappa (QWK), but QWK is non-differentiable and batch-wise QWK assigns the same reward to all samples, leading to unstable training. Generation-based AES also lacks direct optimization for score-level accuracy.
method: Introduce Scoring-aware Multi-reward Reinforcement Learning (SaMRL) that directly optimizes scoring metrics via two rewards: a bidirectional QWK reward that combines batch-wise QWK with sample-level, trait-wise QWK to stabilize and align with the rating schema, and a mean trait-wise RMSE reward to enforce precise score-level accuracy. The policy (score generator) is updated with PPO, using a frozen anchor model with token-wise KL regularization to preserve the [trait, score] format, and dynamic, trainable weights to balance QWK and MSE objectives. This removes the need for packed-evaluation randomness and integrates evaluation-aware learning into training.
paper_title: Autoregressive Multi-trait Essay Scoring via Reinforcement Learning with Scoring-aware Multiple Rewards

problem: Prior RL approaches to essay scoring rely on classification to obtain action probabilities, which degrades performance—especially with wide score ranges—while regression lacks a probability distribution for policy gradients.
method: Reformulate multi-trait AES as autoregressive score generation with a T5 sequence-to-sequence model that outputs a sequence of [trait, score] tokens. Treat token generation probabilities as the policy over actions, and deliver rewards after the full sequence to optimize with policy gradients. This leverages generation probabilities without the class explosion of classification, enabling robust multi-trait predictions and improved performance on prompts with broader score ranges.
paper_title: Autoregressive Multi-trait Essay Scoring via Reinforcement Learning with Scoring-aware Multiple Rewards

problem: Scheduling dataflow workloads on heterogeneous clusters (CPUs/GPUs/FPGAs) is NP-hard; black-box/model-free RL needs many trajectories and fails to capture hidden resource contention, making training and deployment costly.
method: Symphony encodes domain knowledge as a Bayesian-network-driven POMDP that infers latent system state (resource utilizations, including hidden resources like PCIe links) from noisy performance counters using architectural invariants. The inferred belief state feeds neural policy and value networks trained end-to-end; graph networks embed the machine topology and the application DFG, and an RNN (A3C) learns the policy. This inductive bias reduces data needs and focuses learning on relevant resources, improving scheduling quality; Symphony cuts job completion time vs hand-tuned/ML baselines by up to 32% and performs within 6% of an oracle at the tail.
paper_title: Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters

problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
method: The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.
paper_title: Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters

problem: A practical scheduler must plan across CPUs/GPUs/FPGAs under dynamic workloads, honor DFG dependencies, minimize makespan, and keep scheduler latency manageable, all while accounting for hidden resource contention.
method: Symphony integrates the BN-inferred belief state with graph-network embeddings of system topology and the application DFG, and uses an LSTM-based actor-critic (A3C) to select execution, FPGA reconfiguration, or no-op actions with a makespan-oriented reward. It captures hidden contention via the BN and mitigates tail scheduler latency using task batching and accelerator-assisted inference. Empirically it outperforms Paragon, Graphene, and Sparrow: up to 60% of runs match oracle performance and the rest are within 20%, with batching reducing tail latency by up to 12×.
paper_title: Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters

problem: Imitation-based RL like AMP automates style learning but cannot intentionally select among multiple overlapping styles, often blending them, and requires tedious reward tuning to achieve desired motion styles on real robots.
method: Multi-AMP extends AMP to support multiple, discretely switchable styles by training one discriminator per style on state-transition descriptors from motion datasets and style-specific policy rollouts, using a least-squares objective. A one-hot style selector augments the command so only one style is active at a time; the selected discriminator’s score yields a nonnegative style reward that is added to the task reward for policy optimization (e.g., with PPO). Descriptors use pairs of consecutive states (no action dependence), enabling motion priors from heterogeneous sources such as existing RL controllers, trajectory optimization, and reversed motion recordings, and allowing intentional style switching during execution.
paper_title: Advanced Skills through Multiple Adversarial Motion Priors in Reinforcement Learning

problem: Some desired skills lack motion data but still need to be learned jointly with motion-driven styles in one policy without interference or special handling.
method: Treat each motion-data-free skill as a valid style in the selector but set its style reward to zero during training. The skill is optimized purely via the task reward while coexisting with AMP-guided styles, using the same buffers and training loop, enabling simultaneous multi-skill learning without requiring motion priors.
paper_title: Advanced Skills through Multiple Adversarial Motion Priors in Reinforcement Learning

problem: Naively learning a sit-down behavior with task rewards leads to high-impact motions that exceed hardware safety limits, and tuning rewards to fix this is tedious and can conflict with desired style.
method: Record a successful stand-up motion, reverse it to form a sit-down motion prior, and include it as a style in Multi-AMP while training stand-up with task rewards. Introduce a brief zero task-reward window after the sit-down command to avoid conflicts with the style reward, then re-enable task rewards. This encourages low-impact, feasible sit-down motions on the real robot, reducing impulsive knee loads compared to task-reward-only training.
paper_title: Advanced Skills through Multiple Adversarial Motion Priors in Reinforcement Learning

problem: RL controllers for legged manipulators typically achieve forceful interaction only implicitly and cannot be directly commanded to regulate end-effector contact forces, especially without force/torque sensors. This limits compliance control and safe, precise force application during loco-manipulation.
method: Train a unified whole-body RL policy that tracks commanded end-effector forces while locomoting using only proprioceptive sensing. In simulation, create a training signal for force regulation by placing the gripper in a soft-contact potential field—a spring-damper to a moving setpoint in the body frame—and optimize a force-tracking reward. Alternate within episodes between position-tracking and force-tracking modes via a binary command that also toggles the corresponding reward terms. Concurrently learn a state estimator to infer privileged variables (body velocity, gripper position in the body frame, and external force on the gripper) from observation history, and condition the actor on this estimate. The learned policy achieves 5–10 N force-tracking error across a large workspace in both simulation and on a real quadruped manipulator, without any wrist force sensor.
paper_title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation

problem: Deploying compliant, intuitive teleoperation (e.g., gravity compensation and safe physical interaction) on legged manipulators typically requires specialized force sensors or complex model-based controllers.
method: Leverage the learned force controller to realize impedance control with no additional training by closing a simple spring-damper loop around the policy: compute F_cmd = −Kp(x−x_des) − Kd·ẋ using the learned state estimates of the gripper and feed this commanded force to the policy. Zero-force commands produce fully compliant behavior and gravity compensation; modulating the vertical component enables lifting while remaining compliant. The system supports teleoperated tasks such as grasping/dragging furniture and facilitates kinesthetic demonstrations. In tests, compliance is restored for payloads up to 4 kg by matching the commanded vertical force to payload weight.
paper_title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation

problem: Quadruped manipulators struggle to reach across a large 3D workspace and to apply large forces stably using whole-body coordination.
method: Learn a unified whole-body policy that takes end-effector position and base-velocity commands and outputs joint targets for all legs and arm, with locomotion shaped by a trotting contact schedule and smoothness/safety terms. This policy expands the manipulation workspace beyond that of the fixed arm and enables leveraging the body to apply higher forces. In evaluation, the reachable workspace volume increases by 59% over the fixed-arm baseline (1.29 vs 0.81 m^3), with mean position errors around 4.6–5.5 cm in simulation and 4.4–6.9 cm on hardware. The robot can exert up to 90 N pulling force by coordinating the body, exceeding the arm’s nominal payload.
paper_title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation

problem: Recommender systems struggle to capture the rich hierarchical relations in textual and semantic data; Euclidean LLM-augmented methods miss hierarchy, and hyperbolic CF lacks explicit structural signals to guide item/user representations.
method: Use LLMs to generate 3-level hierarchical parent–child tags for each item, then build tag–tag and tag–item graphs to encode taxonomy-like structure. Jointly train a hyperbolic recommender on user–item and tag graphs with task-specific margin ranking losses, and align the two item views via contrastive learning in hyperbolic space using squared hyperbolic distances with temperature weighting. This explicitly injects structural hierarchy and aligns it with collaborative signals in hyperbolic geometry.
paper_title: Large Language Models Enhanced Hyperbolic Space Recommender Systems

problem: Semantic embeddings derived from item/user texts are misaligned with collaborative representations—especially in hyperbolic geometry—leading to unstable or ineffective fusion.
method: Introduce a meta-optimized two-phase strategy with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) adaptor. First, summarize preference-centric texts for items and users with LLMs and encode them into semantic embeddings. Phase 1 freezes both the semantic embeddings and a hyperbolic recommender, training only the MoE to map semantic embeddings into the hyperbolic collaborative space using a small-margin hyperbolic margin ranking loss; the transformed embeddings are saved. Phase 2 initializes user/item ID embeddings with these MoE outputs and trains the hyperbolic recommender on interactions (while integrating tag structure), yielding better alignment, faster convergence, and improved training stability.
paper_title: Large Language Models Enhanced Hyperbolic Space Recommender Systems

problem: Reward models (RMs) trained with the Bradley–Terry (BT) loss overfit and lose generalizability to unseen prompts and response styles, a form of over-optimization that degrades alignment under distribution shift.
method: The paper diagnoses that over-optimization stems from excessive dispersion of hidden-state norms: while the projection head norm stays near its initialization, BT training drives growth in ||h(x,y_w)−h(x,y_l)|| and Var(||h(x,y)||), causing over-confidence and poor OOD performance (Figures 1–2). It proposes batch-wise sum-to-zero regularization (BSR), adding λ·(mean_batch(rewards))^2 to the BT objective to enforce zero-centered rewards per batch and penalize reward outliers with symmetric gradients. This reduces hidden-state norm dispersion across splits (Figure 3) and yields consistent robustness gains over plain BT and baselines (hinge, logit normalization, margin-boosted loss) across four generalization scenarios (ID, prompt-OOD, response-OOD, mutual-OOD), improving accuracy and Kendall’s τ against a gold RM (ArmoRM) (Figure 4, Table 5).
paper_title: On the Robustness of Reward Models for Language Model Alignment

problem: In RLHF, proxy misalignment from over-optimized RMs can stall true-preference improvement and encourage reward gaming such as verbosity, undermining stable policy optimization.
method: Using an RM trained with BSR as the proxy in RLOO leads to better alignment with the gold reward r* throughout training and a more stable reward–KL trajectory, whereas the plain BT-based RM plateaus on r* despite continuing to maximize its own proxy reward (Figures 5a–5d). In AlpacaEval 2.0 (length-controlled), the policy trained with the BSR RM produces substantially shorter outputs and higher win rates: average length drops ~40% vs SFT (2247→1337 tokens) while LC win rate increases (2.59%→9.02%) and exceeds the plain BT-RM run (8.41%) (Table 3), indicating reduced verbosity bias and more reliable downstream alignment.
paper_title: On the Robustness of Reward Models for Language Model Alignment

problem: Robustifying RMs should scale to larger models and high-quality preference data and be measurable beyond in-domain accuracy; overfitting often manifests as representation mismatch between train and evaluation corpora.
method: Scaling BSR to Llama-3.1-8B trained on Skywork-Reward-Preference-80K improves RM-Bench performance on challenging cases: hard accuracy rises by over 5% absolute (e.g., 47.8→55.7 with λ=1e−2), surpassing 8B state-of-the-art hard-task prediction (Table 2). Parametric analysis via effective rank shows BSR maintains nearly identical train/eval ranks (+0.14) while plain BT exhibits a large eval increase (+10.42) (Table 1), indicating reduced overfitting and stronger distributional robustness.
paper_title: On the Robustness of Reward Models for Language Model Alignment

problem: Learning-based local feature descriptors are typically trained with pairwise/triplet surrogate losses that do not directly optimize the nearest-neighbor matching stage, leading to suboptimal ranking performance and reliance on hard-negative mining heuristics.
method: Model descriptor matching as nearest-neighbor retrieval and train deep networks to directly optimize the listwise metric Average Precision (AP). Implement differentiable AP optimization via histogram-binning approximations: for binary descriptors, use smoothed Hamming-distance histograms with tanh-thresholded bits; for real-valued descriptors, introduce a quantization-based approximation by binning Euclidean distances of L2-normalized descriptors over [0,2], enabling end-to-end gradients. This listwise optimization is position-sensitive, removes the need for complex mining heuristics, and achieves state-of-the-art on UBC Phototour, HPatches, RomePatches, and Oxford image matching.
paper_title: Local Descriptors Optimized for Average Precision

problem: Geometric noise (e.g., rotation, scale, affine distortions) in local patches degrades descriptor robustness and matching accuracy.
method: Augment the descriptor network with a Spatial Transformer that predicts a 6-DOF affine transform to align input patches without extra supervision. Mitigate out-of-boundary sampling artifacts by replicate boundary padding instead of zero padding, stabilizing alignment for small patch inputs. The resulting DOAP-ST variant consistently improves over the base descriptor across benchmarks.
paper_title: Local Descriptors Optimized for Average Precision

problem: In HPatches, image matching involves in-sequence distractors that are not modeled when training on patch retrieval with only out-of-sequence negatives, causing a train-test mismatch and reduced matching performance.
method: Mine additional in-sequence distractor labels via clustering within each sequence using HOG+raw-pixel features and K-means; treat cluster pairs with inter-cluster distance above a threshold (with 3D verification) as distractors. Modify minibatch sampling to draw two sequences per batch to include many in-sequence negatives. This label-mined DOAP-ST-LM significantly boosts image matching mAP on HPatches (e.g., ≈+6% over DOAP-ST and ≈+10% over L2Net), including tough cases.
paper_title: Local Descriptors Optimized for Average Precision

problem: Unbiased learning-to-rank from clicks requires accurate examination-bias (propensity) estimates, but existing methods are restricted to the Position-Based Model (PBM) that ignores query/user context and often rely on intrusive randomized swaps or difficult document-level relevance modeling.
method: Introduce the Contextual Position-Based Model (CPBM), which models examination as Pr(E=1 | k, x) depending on rank k and context x, and learn it via a neural network h(k, x). Develop an AllPairs estimator that harvests implicit interventions from logs of at least two historic rankers (e.g., A/B tests or stationary deployments): define interventional sets S_{k,k'} of (x, d) that appear at both positions under different rankers, compute assignment weights q_k(x, d), form IPS-weighted click labels, and jointly fit h and an average-relevance model g(k, k', x) by maximizing a weighted cross-entropy objective. This controls for unobserved relevance without explicit swaps and yields context-specific propensities.
paper_title: Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation

problem: Learning a context-dependent propensity model must aggregate sparse interventional evidence across many position pairs and scale to rich context features while remaining trainable and robust.
method: Instantiate CPBM with two MLPs: a propensity MLP outputs the position-wise propensity vector p(x) via a sigmoid layer, and an average-relevance MLP outputs a symmetric matrix r(x) using 3D weights and symmetrization; r(x) can be simplified to context-independent r_{k,k'} with little loss. Show the AllPairs objective is equivalent to a weighted cross-entropy, enabling efficient backprop training and effective information aggregation across position pairs. Empirically, CPBM learned from harvested interventions matches swap-based gold standards on ArXiv with tighter error bars and, on Yahoo’s semi-synthetic data, reduces relative error vs. PBM by ~65% and improves downstream LTR performance.
paper_title: Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation

problem: Graph-based collaborative filtering suffers from noisy and sparse user–item interactions. Existing self-supervised GCL methods rely on hand-crafted random augmentations that may drop informative edges, retain noise, and risk model collapse when two views share the same distribution.
method: AdaGCL introduces two trainable, heterogeneous view generators to create adaptive contrastive views. (1) A VGAE-based graph generative model reconstructs diverse views by sampling latent node embeddings with KL-regularized Gaussian noise, yielding challenging yet informative augmentations. (2) A graph denoising generator learns per-edge Bernoulli masks via an MLP over endpoint features at each GNN layer, using the concrete (hard) reparameterization and an L0-style sparsity regularizer to filter noisy edges and renormalize adjacency. These complementary views are contrasted with a node-level InfoNCE objective, providing richer signals and mitigating model collapse.
paper_title: Adaptive Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Generative or denoising view models trained only with generic reconstruction objectives may produce augmentations misaligned with the ranking goal of recommendation.
method: AdaGCL learns task-aware view generators via a bilevel scheme: the upper level jointly optimizes the main CF encoder (LightGCN) with BPR and the cross-view contrastive loss, while the lower level updates the VGAE and denoising generators using BPR-based objectives alongside their respective reconstruction/KL and L0 penalties. This aligns generated views with the recommendation objective; ablations show removing task-aware training or the denoising view degrades performance.
paper_title: Adaptive Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Collaborative filtering models commonly optimize BPR without a clear understanding of what representation properties it encourages, limiting principled objective design.
method: The paper formalizes two desired properties—alignment (positive user–item pairs are close) and uniformity (embeddings are well spread on the unit hypersphere)—and proves that perfectly aligned and perfectly uniform encoders are exact minimizers of the BPR loss when realizable. It then quantifies these metrics and empirically tracks learning dynamics for representative CF methods (BPRMF, BPR-DS, LGCN, ENMF), showing distinct trajectories and that improving either alignment or uniformity correlates with better recommendation performance.
paper_title: Towards Representation Alignment and Uniformity in Collaborative Filtering

problem: Existing CF objectives rely on negative sampling and do not explicitly optimize both alignment and uniformity, leading to suboptimal representations and sensitivity to sampling choices.
method: DirectAU is a negative-sample-free learning objective that directly optimizes representation quality: it minimizes the squared L2 distance between L2-normalized embeddings of positive user–item pairs (alignment) and the log of the average exp(−t·distance^2) over in-batch pairwise distances for users and items separately (uniformity), combined with a single trade-off weight γ. Uniformity is computed in-batch to better match the underlying user/item distributions, and only one hyperparameter (γ) is tuned; the approach works with simple MF or GNN encoders and uses dot-product scoring. Across Beauty, Gowalla, and Yelp2018, DirectAU achieves consistent SOTA results with improvements up to about 14% over strong baselines, while remaining training-efficient.
paper_title: Towards Representation Alignment and Uniformity in Collaborative Filtering

problem: User behavior histories contain many interactions irrelevant to a given target item, which act as noise and degrade time-cycle modeling and CTR prediction.
method: Introduce a gated filter unit that measures relevance between the target item embedding and each behavior’s item embedding via a cosine-based score, then applies a thresholded, piece-wise gating to produce weights f_k, setting irrelevant behaviors to zero. This denoised sequence is then used for time-cycle modeling, ensuring only target-related behaviors contribute. (Supported: gated filter unit with cos activation and threshold; zeroing out noise behaviors.)
paper_title: Denoising Time Cycle Modeling for Recommendation

problem: Discrete semantic time features (e.g., hour, weekday, month) break temporal continuity, hindering learning of absolute time cycle patterns (ATC).
method: Propose an absolute time convolution module that aggregates surrounding time-slot embeddings at multiple granularities. It performs 1D convolution with ReLU over a window (j=0..J, default J=2) of neighboring time slots followed by max-pooling, then concatenates across granularities to form Λ(t). This preserves local continuity and captures absolute cyclic patterns. (Supported: surrounding time embeddings, conv+pooling, multi-granularity, diminishing impact with larger j.)
paper_title: Denoising Time Cycle Modeling for Recommendation

problem: Modeling relative time cycles (RTC)—how influence decays or repeats over time intervals—and fusing them with absolute cycles is crucial for timely recommendation.
method: Learn a continuous, translation-invariant temporal embedding Φ(t) inspired by Bochner’s theorem, with trainable frequency parameters, so that the inner product ⟨Φ(t1),Φ(t2)⟩ depends only on the interval |t2−t1|. Combine this RTC representation with the ATC Λ(t) and apply a time cycle attention over the denoised behavior sequence to extract cycle patterns relevant to the target; concatenate with a user-interest module and predict CTR via an MLP. This fusion yields consistent gains over strong baselines on four datasets. (Supported: translation-invariant kernel via Bochner, trainable ω, time cycle attention combining Λ and Φ, concatenation with user-interest module, improved results across datasets.)
paper_title: Denoising Time Cycle Modeling for Recommendation

problem: Sparse retrieval-based speculative decoding drafts next tokens by exact string matching over short contexts, which lowers precision/recall and limits acceptance rates and speed gains.
method: DReSD replaces sparse matching with dense, semantic retrieval: it queries a static key–value datastore using the LLM’s current state (last hidden state before the LM head) as a contextualised token embedding, then retrieves next-token sequences via approximate nearest neighbour search (ScaNN) with cosine similarity. To make this efficient and accurate, DReSD applies dual normalisation (z-score on full embeddings and Lp magnitude normalisation post-PCA) and dimensionality reduction (PCA to 64 dims) before search, and filters candidates to those beginning with the LLM’s own next-token prediction. Drafts are verified with exact, batched SD verification, preserving the LLM’s outputs. Across experiments, DReSD achieves on average 87% higher acceptance rates, 65% longer accepted token runs, and 19% faster generation than sparse REST, with up to 4.64x speedup over autoregressive decoding.
paper_title: DReSD: Dense Retrieval for Speculative Decoding

problem: Naively deploying dense retrieval for SD can be slow or ineffective without the right design choices; it is unclear which factors most impact acceptance and throughput.
method: The paper identifies three critical factors and provides concrete configurations: (1) Embedding preprocessing—z-score normalisation, PCA to 64 dimensions (capturing ~30–40% variance), and post-PCA normalisation—yields high retrieval quality (MRR ~93–94) and better SD than higher dims, while avoiding large-vector overheads. (2) Datastore alignment—prompt, response, and sampling alignment—matters multiplicatively: using in-distribution (ID) datastores built from the target LLM’s own responses raises MAR by ~70% on CodeAlpaca despite ~40% fewer tokens; matching sampling (e.g., greedy datastore with greedy decoding) further improves speedups. (3) Draft shape—tuning the number vs. length of drafts—is key: narrow-and-deep drafts for greedy (e.g., 3×20) and more, shorter drafts for nucleus (e.g., 10×10) outperform REST’s fixed shallow–wide trees, yielding 10–23% (nucleus) and 42–218% (greedy) faster speeds over REST and enabling up to 4.64x average speedups over autoregressive generation.
paper_title: DReSD: Dense Retrieval for Speculative Decoding

problem: Listwise LLM rerankers are inefficient because they generate full sequences of passage IDs, and standard LM training treats all ranking errors uniformly, under-emphasizing top relevant items.
method: FIRST derives the entire ranking from only the output logits at the first generated identifier position, mapping passages to single-token alphabetic IDs (A–Z) so logits are directly comparable. It trains the LLM with a joint objective that combines the LM loss with a weighted RankNet learning-to-rank loss applied to the first-token logits, using inverse mean-rank weights 1/(i+j) to prioritize higher ranks. At inference, it performs single-token decoding and sorts candidates by those first-position logits, avoiding sequence generation. Experiments on BEIR show maintained/improved ranking (e.g., higher avg nDCG@10 than RankZephyr) with about 50% lower latency.
paper_title: FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding

problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
method: Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).
paper_title: FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding

problem: Knowledge graphs contain many noisy or task-irrelevant triplets, making it hard for KG-enhanced recommenders to focus on the knowledge that truly explains user-item interactions.
method: KGRec learns rationale scores for KG triplets via an attentive rationale weighting function inspired by heterogeneous graph transformers. It computes attention over triplets using relation-aware keys (element-wise product of relation and tail embeddings) and normalizes scores across a head’s neighbors to yield comparable importance. These rationale scores are then injected to weight heterogeneous knowledge aggregation, emphasizing high-importance neighbors during item (and user) representation learning.
paper_title: Knowledge Graph Self-Supervised Rationalization for Recommendation

problem: Generative self-supervision on KGs often relies on random masking, which fails to highlight task-relevant knowledge and can be sensitive to KG noise.
method: KGRec introduces a rationale-aware masked autoencoder: it derives globally comparable importance γ(h,r,t) by scaling softmax rationale scores with the head’s degree and adds Gumbel noise for robust top‑k selection. High‑γ triplets are masked to form an augmented KG, embeddings are computed on the remaining graph, and a relation-aware dot‑product decoder reconstructs the masked edges. This masking‑reconstruction forces the model to rebuild crucial connections and distill informative KG rationales for recommendation.
paper_title: Knowledge Graph Self-Supervised Rationalization for Recommendation

problem: Cross-view contrastive learning between the KG and user–item interaction graph can be degraded by noisy edges and false negatives, weakening alignment between knowledge and collaborative signals.
method: KGRec performs rationale-aware augmentation for both views: it prunes low‑score KG triplets and down-weights/drops user–item edges based on the mean rationale scores of items’ linked triplets via multinomial sampling. It then computes view-specific embeddings (LightGCN for the interaction view; rationale-aware aggregation for the KG view), maps them into a shared space with two MLPs, and optimizes a modified InfoNCE-style loss with one random negative per view and temperature τ, yielding noise-resistant cross-view alignment.
paper_title: Knowledge Graph Self-Supervised Rationalization for Recommendation

problem: Online recommender systems face non-stationary data and test-time distribution shift, while gradient-based incremental updates adapt too slowly for large CTR models.
method: ReLoop2 introduces a slow–fast learning paradigm with a training-free, non-parametric error memory that stores recent error samples (hidden state, base prediction, and ground-truth) observed during serving. At inference, it retrieves similar samples to the current hidden representation and uses attention with a temperature to estimate the prediction error; this error is then used to compensate the base model’s output with a tunable weight and clamping to [0,1]. The approach continually refreshes the memory online, creating a self-correcting loop for fast test-time adaptation, and is model-agnostic and orthogonal to incremental training.
paper_title: ReLoop2: Building Self-Adaptive Recommendation Models via Responsive Error Compensation Loop

problem: Building an error memory for web-scale recommendation must meet tight latency and memory constraints; storing raw samples or building heavy ANN indices is too slow and memory-intensive.
method: ReLoop2 designs a fast-access, LSH-based sketch memory using Signed Random Projections (SimHash) to hash hidden vectors into 2^L buckets across K independent arrays. Instead of raw samples, each bucket stores aggregate sums (e.g., of labels and base outputs), enabling O(1) streaming writes and O(1) reads with a constant memory footprint (2^L × K × 3). Parallel updates and simple averaging across arrays yield similarity-weighted readouts for error estimation, avoiding k-means/HNSW construction and large RAM usage while retaining theoretical guarantees.
paper_title: ReLoop2: Building Self-Adaptive Recommendation Models via Responsive Error Compensation Loop

problem: Linguistic field recordings in low-resource and endangered languages rarely get fully annotated as Interlinear Glossed Text (IGT), and there is no standardized speech-to-IGT dataset spanning many languages.
method: Define the WAV2GLOSS task: generate IGT directly from speech with four aligned outputs—transcription (wd), underlying forms (ur), glosses (gl), and free translation (tr). Build FIELDWORK, a 37-language corpus by unifying ELAN/EXMARaLDA/Pangloss sources (DoReCo, Multi-CAST, COCOON, INEL, NINJAL), converting audio to 16 kHz mono WAV, normalizing and cleaning annotations, and packaging them into a machine-readable format with document-level train/dev/test splits optimized via a knapsack solver and released as a Hugging Face dataset.
paper_title: WAV2GLOSS: Generating Interlinear Glossed Text from Speech

problem: Automatically predicting multiple IGT components from raw speech across many under-resourced languages is challenging; it is unclear whether end-to-end or supervised pre-trained decoders, and single-task vs multi-task or monolingual vs multilingual training, work best.
method: Provide strong end-to-end speech baselines by fine-tuning frozen self-supervised encoders (WavLM Large and XLS-R-300M) with an added conformer encoder + transformer decoder and CTC-Attention training; use character-level tokenization with language and task tokens. Also fine-tune an open Whisper-style supervised model (OWSM v3.1-base) with its BPE tokenizer, adding language and task tokens and fully updating encoder–decoder with CTC-Attention. Compare single-task vs multi-task and monolingual vs multilingual setups. Findings: single-task generally outperforms multi-task except for glossing; OWSM excels at glossing and translation (benefiting from a pre-trained decoder vocabulary in high-resource target languages), while XLS-R leads on transcription and underlying forms on seen languages; multilingual training helps the smallest datasets but degrades as per-language data grow.
paper_title: WAV2GLOSS: Generating Interlinear Glossed Text from Speech

problem: Directly generating glosses and translations from speech is substantially harder than ASR; leveraging text-based models could help but may suffer from error propagation in cascades.
method: Evaluate a cascaded pipeline that feeds the best ASR outputs (XLS-R E2E) into ByT5 text-to-IGT models, including a variant first fine-tuned on noisy IGT (ODIN) before FIELDWORK. This byte-level model supports multilingual, unsegmented inputs. Results show the cascade improves translation over end-to-end speech models, but not underlying forms or glossing; ODIN pretraining yields small gains. With ground-truth text, ByT5 performance is much higher, highlighting both the promise of text-based modeling and the impact of ASR error propagation.
paper_title: WAV2GLOSS: Generating Interlinear Glossed Text from Speech

problem: Diffusion-based text-to-audio models achieve high fidelity but suffer from slow inference due to multi-step sampling with heavy attention backbones, while mask-based models on discrete tokens (e.g., MAGNET) are fast but lag in audio quality/fidelity.
method: IMPACT integrates iterative mask-based parallel decoding into a latent diffusion model operating on continuous audio latents. Audio is encoded with a VAE; during training, masked latents are reconstructed by a Transformer-based latent encoder conditioned on text and a lightweight MLP diffusion head that predicts noise only at masked positions. At inference, it decodes from an empty sequence using random position selection (no confidence scores in continuous space) and a cosine mask schedule, with diffusion sampling and classifier-free guidance. Replacing heavy attention diffusion cores with the small MLP head and leveraging parallel decoding yields low latency while preserving diffusion fidelity, achieving state-of-the-art FD and FAD on AudioCaps and competitive or better latency vs. MAGNET-S depending on iteration settings.
paper_title: IMPACT: Iterative Mask-based Parallel Decoding for Text-to-Audio Generation with Diffusion Modeling

problem: Text-conditional training must simultaneously learn audio generation and text alignment, but paired text–audio data is limited, reducing fidelity and text relevance.
method: IMPACT introduces an unconditional pre-training phase (mask-based generative modeling without text) on large unpaired audio (e.g., AudioSet), followed by text-conditional training. This curriculum first imparts general audio generative ability, then aligns to text, enabling the use of much larger datasets. Ablations show unconditional pre-training is indispensable: models with pre-training consistently improve FD/FAD and human REL/OVL ratings, with larger-scale pre-training further boosting fidelity despite minor trade-offs on some metrics.
paper_title: IMPACT: Iterative Mask-based Parallel Decoding for Text-to-Audio Generation with Diffusion Modeling

problem: In AVSR, training with video-frame dropout improves robustness to missing video but degrades accuracy on complete inputs by inducing over-reliance on audio (a shift toward a unimodal decision pattern).
method: Multimodal Distribution Approximation with Knowledge Distillation (MDA-KD): train a teacher on complete audio-video pairs and a student on video-dropout inputs with the same architecture. Distill hidden representations (from cross-modal encoder layers) by minimizing the distance between batch samples with temperature-softmax to anchor the student's latent space to the teacher’s multimodal distribution, preventing collapse to audio-only features. This preserves accuracy on full inputs while improving robustness to missing video (e.g., AV4/AV5 outperform dropout-only baselines and avoid the 0.0-dropout CER degradation vs. AV0; Table 1; discussion lines 85–115, 167).
paper_title: A Study of Dropout-Induced Modality Bias on Robustness to Missing Video Frames for Audio-Visual Speech Recognition

problem: When video is severely or entirely missing, synergistic fusion is unreliable; AVSR must dynamically switch to an audio-dominant decision mode and remain efficient.
method: Modality-Specific Adapter (MS-Adapter): insert LoRA adapters into the audio branch self-attention to enable a dynamic switch from p(y|zu) to p(y|za). Adapters can be trained with extra unimodal data and augmentations, and at inference skip the video branch to cut compute. This improves audio-only CER beyond ASR (24.94% vs. 25.13%) and reduces compute (3.89 GFLOPS vs. 12.64 GFLOPS when padding video) while retaining the unified model (lines 119–124, 167–181, 325).
paper_title: A Study of Dropout-Induced Modality Bias on Robustness to Missing Video Frames for Audio-Visual Speech Recognition

problem: Why does video-dropout both help robustness and harm complete-input performance in AVSR?
method: Modality Bias Hypothesis (MBH) and Modality Bias Venn Diagram (MBVD): formalize task-relevant latent features into audio-specific, visual-specific, and modality-general components and show that increasing video-dropout shifts the decisive latent distribution from multimodal to audio-unimodal (larger α, smaller β, γ). The paper validates this with (i) CER degradation curves converging to ASR (Figure 1), (ii) increased transcription similarity to ASR (Figure 2), and (iii) cosine-similarity of intermediate representations showing convergence to ASR as dropout rises (Figure 3), providing a conceptual guide for robust training (lines 65–69, 71–81).
paper_title: A Study of Dropout-Induced Modality Bias on Robustness to Missing Video Frames for Audio-Visual Speech Recognition

problem: ASR for Indonesian local languages lacks a unified multilingual benchmark and clear training strategies to leverage cross-lingual transfer.
method: Introduce NusaASR, the first multilingual ASR benchmark for Indonesian and local languages, spanning 17 datasets in 8 languages. Train wav2vec 2.0 LARGE (both pretrained XLS-R and an ASR-fine-tuned Indonesian/Javanese/Sundanese checkpoint) and Whisper SMALL under single-task, monolingual multi-task, and multilingual multi-task settings. Multilingual multi-task fine-tuning achieves the best overall performance (~20% average WER across languages), evidencing beneficial cross-lingual transfer, while certain languages (e.g., Buginese) show limited transfer and remain challenging.
paper_title: NusaCrowd: Open Source Initiative for Indonesian NLP Resources

problem: Autoregressive discrete-token TTS models are constrained to left-to-right generation, making them ill-suited for speech editing where both preceding and following contexts must be respected. Using acoustic tokens further adds RVQ prediction complexity and inherits audio quality limits from codec models.
method: CTX-txt2vec introduces contextual VQ-diffusion to generate semantic tokens conditioned on both sides of context. It concatenates corrupted target tokens with clean preceding and following context [cA, x_t, cB] and feeds them to a Transformer VQ-diffusion decoder, using a binary indicator embedding to distinguish context vs. target. Text is aligned to token length via a duration predictor and length regulator, and the aligned text encoding is injected by addition to self-attention outputs instead of cross-attention to respect strict alignment. Training randomly samples utterances with both/one/no context, while inference rescales predicted durations to match context speed and iteratively denoises from fully masked tokens.
paper_title: UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding

problem: Vocoding from semantic tokens lacks speaker identity, and prior approaches rely on speaker embeddings (e.g., x-vectors) or acoustic tokens from audio codecs, which can bottleneck quality or require codec dependencies.
method: CTX-vec2wav is a contextual vocoder that converts semantic tokens to waveforms while conditioning on acoustic context provided directly by mel-spectrograms from surrounding audio. It uses two Conformer-based semantic encoders augmented with cross-attention to mel features produced by a lightweight mel encoder (1D conv), eliminating the need for speaker embeddings or codec acoustic tokens. An auxiliary feature adaptor conditions on and predicts pitch, energy, and POV to improve fidelity. Training splits each utterance: a 2–3 s left segment supplies mel context, and the remaining segment supplies semantic tokens for vocoding.
paper_title: UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding

problem: Speech continuation and speech editing are typically handled by separate systems, lacking a unified approach that can seamlessly accommodate both tasks with the same model.
method: UniCATS unifies context-aware TTS by prompting semantics via semantic tokens and acoustics via mel-spectrograms of the surrounding context, then generating [cA, x0, cB] with the same CTX-txt2vec and CTX-vec2wav stack. The only difference between continuation and editing is the presence of the following context B; editing algorithms directly generalize to continuation by omitting B. This design supports seamless concatenation with context for both tasks and enables zero-shot speaker adaptation without speaker embeddings or codec tokens.
paper_title: UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding

problem: Rule-based text normalization (TN) for TTS is accurate but costly to engineer and hard to scale across languages, especially in low-resource settings and in contexts where the same surface form must be normalized differently depending on context and language.
method: PolyNorm frames TN as few-shot prompting with large language models using a language-agnostic instruction prompt and localized in-context learning (ICL) examples per language. The curated ICL set (≈80–100 examples) and a structured prompt template guide normalization across 27 categories; Japanese uses a supplementary prompt to guide katakana output. An error analysis–driven hillclimbing loop iteratively revises ICL examples to target systematic failure modes (e.g., numerals, date/currency formats). Evaluated with GPT-4o/4o-mini across eight languages, PolyNorm consistently reduces WER versus a production rule-based baseline (e.g., GPT-4o WER 4.17%–7.88%) and handles context-sensitive cases like URLs and dash usage in sports scores vs. phone numbers.
paper_title: PolyNorm: Few-Shot LLM-Based Text Normalization for Text-to-Speech

problem: There is a lack of standardized, multilingual benchmarks and a scalable, language-agnostic evaluation process for TN; prior attempts relying on machine translation produce inconsistent, low-quality ground truth across languages.
method: PolyNorm-Benchmark provides a multilingual TN dataset and evaluation pipeline: paired unnormalized/normalized text generated with DeepSeek-R1 and then expert-edited for eight languages, covering 27 normalization categories with 20 examples each (540 per language). The evaluation uses WER/BLEU (CER for non-whitespace languages) and standardizes conventions while ignoring non-phonetic differences (e.g., casing, certain orthographic variants) to focus on pronunciation/meaning. The benchmark and taxonomy are released publicly to enable consistent, scalable TN experimentation.
paper_title: PolyNorm: Few-Shot LLM-Based Text Normalization for Text-to-Speech

problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
method: Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.
paper_title: DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR

problem: Training and inference over entire meetings are constrained by memory and by limited alignment between segment-local ASR features and meeting-wide speaker label prediction, which hampers robust attribution in multi-talker and overlapping segments.
method: Introduce a two-stage joint fine-tuning scheme. Stage 1 jointly trains ASR and DNC with the DNC decoder linked to current-segment ASR features (past segments attend to a learnable pad), predicting speaker indices up to the current segment. Stage 2 freezes ASR, precomputes W_CA features for all word tokens across the meeting, and fine-tunes only the DNC decoder so each target speaker index attends to its full-meeting token features, enabling a single-pass DNC decoding. This improves speaker assignment, especially in overlaps: on AMI, cpWER drops to 31.3/32.1 (Dev/Eval) vs 34.8/34.6 for the parallel system, and cpWER-Multi to 43.4/44.8 vs 49.8/49.2; improvements are statistically significant (Wilcoxon p-values ≤ 1.1E-6).
paper_title: DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR

problem: Neural speaker clustering relies on limited real meeting data; standard Diaconis augmentation rotates speaker embeddings aggressively, which can degrade performance.
method: Propose Constrained Diaconis Augmentation (CDA), which controls the rotation angle of speaker-embedding augmentations by constraining the Householder-based random rotation (via a scale parameter). Applying CDA during fine-tuning further improves speaker attribution: on AMI, cpWER improves from 31.3/32.1 to 30.7/31.5 (Dev/Eval) and cpWER-Multi from 43.4/44.8 to 42.5/44.1.
paper_title: DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR

problem: Converting monaural video audio into binaural (left/right) audio typically requires large datasets with ground-truth binaural recordings, which are costly to collect.
method: Introduce an audio-visual consistency mechanism that aligns interaural level difference (ILD)-based left/right cues from audio with visually grounded localization. The model computes an audio-only left/right probability from the magnitude difference of predicted left/right spectrograms (|S_L|−|S_R|), and an audio-visual probability via co-attention: cosine-similarity between each spectrogram time-frequency patch and the visual feature map, modulated by learnable left/right weighting functions (W_L, W_R). A binary cross-entropy consistency loss (L_con) matches these probabilities, providing a self-supervised signal that does not require ground-truth binaural labels during training (can also be combined with supervised reconstruction when available).
paper_title: Exploiting Audio-Visual Consistency with Partial Supervision for Spatial Audio Generation

problem: Improve spatial audio synthesis quality while reducing reliance on labeled binaural data, and make training effective in both fully supervised and semi-supervised settings.
method: Embed the proposed consistency module into a U-Net-based spatial audio synthesizer that predicts complex masks (M_L, M_R) to decompose the mono spectrogram into left/right outputs, with visual features injected at the bottleneck. Train with a joint objective: standard binaural reconstruction (or left-right difference) plus the audio-visual consistency loss. This yields state-of-the-art STFT/ENV performance across FAIR-PLAY and processed 360° datasets, and achieves performance comparable to fully supervised baselines using only about 60% of labeled binaural data.
paper_title: Exploiting Audio-Visual Consistency with Partial Supervision for Spatial Audio Generation

problem: Live multilingual videoconference captions must be accurate, low-latency, and stable, but streaming ASR→MT with re-translation causes frequent caption revisions (flicker) that increase cognitive load.
method: Deploy a modular, cascaded streaming ASR→MT system (swap-in Kaldi/WeNet/Google ASR and DiDi/Google MT) that retranslates on each ASR update, augmented with anti-flicker captioning policies: translate-k/translate-t throttling, mask-k (using Mask-0 at utterance start then Mask-4), preserving linebreaks, and smooth pixel-wise scrolling. Further reduce instability by modifying OpenNMT CTranslate2 decoding with biased beam search to favor consistency with the previous MT output, and improve readability with NVIDIA NeMo punctuation/casing. On in-house meeting data, biased decoding lowers translation lag, normalized erasure, and word-burstiness versus un-biased MT.
paper_title: MeetDot: Videoconferencing with Live Translation Captions

problem: Existing intrinsic metrics (e.g., BLEU, translation lag, erasure) do not fully capture the user experience of incremental captions in meetings, and there is a lack of practical extrinsic evaluation for end-to-end captioning quality.
method: Provide an integrated evaluation suite that computes standard metrics plus new, videoconferencing-specific intrinsic metrics: initial lag (time to first word), incremental caption lag (update cadence), and mean/max word burstiness (words/characters added or removed per update). Introduce a cross-lingual, Taboo-style word-guessing game with automatic scoring to A/B test captioning algorithms and configurations, serving as an extrinsic measure of real-time communicative effectiveness.
paper_title: MeetDot: Videoconferencing with Live Translation Captions

problem: Text-to-audio generative models lack faithful, token-level explanations of how each word in the prompt influences the generated audio, and attention-based or single-token perturbation methods miss multi-token interactions and are inefficient over long sequences.
method: AudioGenX learns a soft importance mask over text tokens for each generated audio token by perturbing the cross-attention mechanism and optimizing an Explainer MLP (sigmoid + Gumbel-softmax) to predict the mask. It jointly uses factual and counterfactual objectives on the latent audio-token embedding e_t: maximize cosine similarity under factual masking and minimize it under counterfactual masking, with L1/L2 regularization for sparse, simple masks. Explanations are computed per audio token (parallelizable) and then aggregated over time to provide holistic or interval-specific explanations. The approach supports simultaneous multi-token perturbations and is model-agnostic for TAG models that use cross-attention.
paper_title: AudioGenX: Explainability on Text-to-Audio Generative Models

problem: There is no standard way to evaluate explanation faithfulness for text-to-audio generation, where class labels are absent and outputs are stochastic sequences.
method: AudioGenX introduces an evaluation protocol using a pre-trained audio classifier (PaSST) to compare original, factual-masked, and counterfactual-masked generations. It defines Fidelity metrics (Fid_F, Fid_CF) based on changes in top-1 class probabilities and KL-based metrics (KL_F, KL_CF) on full label distributions to capture sufficiency (small changes under factual masks) and necessity (large changes under counterfactual masks), along with reporting mask size.
paper_title: AudioGenX: Explainability on Text-to-Audio Generative Models

problem: Users need actionable control to correct misalignments between prompts and generated audio and to diagnose model behaviors (e.g., handling of negation) using explanations.
method: AudioGenX leverages learned token-importance masks to guide editing by reweighting cross-attention (scaling selected mask values) for specific tokens and time intervals, amplifying or suppressing their influence. This explanation-guided reweighting improves alignment to target concepts (e.g., reduced FAD and KL after edits) and supports analyses such as identifying token importance patterns and limitations with negation.
paper_title: AudioGenX: Explainability on Text-to-Audio Generative Models

problem: Vision models trained in simulation often fail on real robots due to the reality gap, making it hard to localize objects precisely under clutter and occlusion without collecting real training data or careful camera calibration.
method: Train an object localization network entirely on low‑fidelity simulated RGB images while heavily randomizing the rendering domain—object/distractor presence and pose, textures (table/floor/robot/skybox), lighting (count/pose/specularity), camera pose/FOV, and image noise—so the real world appears as another variation. Using simple, algorithmically generated non‑realistic textures and a modified VGG‑based regressor that predicts Cartesian coordinates assuming a fixed table height, the model transfers zero‑shot to real images with no real‑image fine‑tuning or domain adaptation, achieving ~1.5 cm average localization error and robustness to distractors and partial occlusions.
paper_title: Domain Randomization for Transferring Deep Neural Networks from Simulation to the Real World

problem: It is unclear which aspects of domain randomization and data regime are most important for successful sim‑to‑real transfer of vision-based object localization.
method: Perform a systematic ablation over number of training images, number of unique textures, additive noise, inclusion of distractors, camera pose randomization, and ImageNet pretraining, evaluating real‑world error. Findings: performance improves with more samples up to ~50k; large texture diversity is pivotal (sharp degradation below ~1k unique textures and comparable gains to adding more images); training with distractors is critical for robustness (removing them increases error with distractors/occlusions to ~7 cm); camera randomization provides a modest boost; additive noise has negligible effect. Pretraining helps in low‑data regimes but is unnecessary with sufficient synthetic data—models trained from scratch often match or outperform pretrained ones.
paper_title: Domain Randomization for Transferring Deep Neural Networks from Simulation to the Real World

problem: Can a detector trained only in simulation enable reliable real‑world robotic grasping in clutter and with realistic objects?
method: Integrate the simulated‑only trained detector with a Fetch robot and standard motion planning (MoveIt!) to grasp targets using predicted Cartesian positions. On geometric shapes amid clutter (including unseen distractor orientations), the system succeeds in 38/40 trials, and on a real YCB Spam can among unseen food‑item distractors it succeeds in 9/10 trials, demonstrating transfer to real manipulation tasks without any real‑image training.
