paper_title: Domain Randomization for Transferring Deep Neural Networks from Simulation to the Real World

problem: In fast, interactive tasks like traffic weaving, a robot must anticipate human responses that are inherently uncertain and multimodal, and that depend both on the interaction history and on the robot’s planned future actions.
method: Learn a sequence-to-sequence generative model p(y|x) of human future actions conditioned on joint interaction history and candidate robot future using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder. The model uses a discrete, factorized categorical latent variable z to represent high-level behavior modes, and an LSTM autoregressive decoder with a Gaussian Mixture Model output layer to capture within-mode variability and time-step-level multimodality. Training maximizes an ELBO with an inference network q(z|x,y) and employs scheduled sampling to mitigate error compounding. This structure yields interpretable modes and improved validation negative log-likelihood while explicitly modeling responses to candidate robot actions.
paper_title: Multimodal Probabilistic Model-Based Planning for Human-Robot Interaction

problem: Constructing real-time robot policies under multimodal human uncertainty requires efficiently evaluating how different robot action sequences affect likely human responses, while covering disparate interaction modes.
method: Discretize the robot’s short-horizon action space into 5 windows with 8 choices each (4096 candidate sequences) and evaluate expected cost via massive parallel sampling of human responses from the learned model. Use a two-stage pipeline: sample 16 human futures per sequence to rank candidates, then rescore the top 32 with 1024 samples each, and select the minimum-expected-cost sequence. The cost combines collision avoidance, control effort, lane-change urgency, and longitudinal disambiguation, and actions are executed in an MPC loop with 0.3 s replanning. This GPU-based procedure simulates nearly 100,000 human futures in about 0.25 s, enabling coverage of multimodal outcomes in real time.
paper_title: Multimodal Probabilistic Model-Based Planning for Human-Robot Interaction

problem: General video-to-command methods for robotic imitation rely on video captioning that emphasizes global scene understanding and is insensitive to the specific manipulated object and its state change, leading to errors in complex pick-and-place tasks.
method: Introduce a captioning network (CNet) that fuses global frame features with a local Video Difference Map (VDM). The VDM is computed as the difference between color masks of the start and end frames, where the color masks are produced by a trained grasp network (GNet) to suppress illumination/shadow effects. Features from uniformly sampled frames and the VDM are extracted with a pre-trained CNN and fed into an LSTM encoder-decoder, modifying the first LSTM layer to connect F(I) and F(V) to half of the input units to specialize inputs. This fusion yields large gains over V2C on BLEU-4, METEOR, ROUGE-L, and CIDEr (e.g., CIDEr 4.676 vs 1.437 with Inception v3).
paper_title: Learning Actions from Human Demonstration Video for Robotic Manipulation

problem: For executing the generated commands, the robot needs reliable grasp poses and object categories from RGBD images, while also supplying localized object features to aid captioning.
method: Propose GNet, an end-to-end grasp detection network that couples SegNet-based object segmentation with a grasp solution generator and an object classifier. From each object mask, the grasp generator computes the center and orientation by fitting a convex hull and minimum-area rectangle; a color mask of the object region mitigates illumination/shadow and is fed to a CNN classifier to predict a 17-way category label. Segmentation and classification are jointly optimized with a multi-task loss. On a UR5, GNet-RGBD achieves a 93% grasp success rate, outperforming baselines and GNet-RGB, demonstrating the benefit of depth.
paper_title: Learning Actions from Human Demonstration Video for Robotic Manipulation

problem: Sampling-based motion planners often fail or become inefficient in environments with narrow passages because uninformed or heuristic sampling rarely hits critical C-space regions; learned workspace-to-configuration mappings may not reliably place samples in those regions.
method: Decompose the workspace into local primitives (e.g., pairs of obstacles) and, for each primitive, solve targeted local motion-planning queries multiple times to harvest configurations that traverse difficult regions. Fit a local sampler per primitive as a Gaussian Mixture Model by placing one mixture per harvested configuration with a fixed covariance (no EM), then optionally reduce mixtures by merging cliques of straight-line-connected configurations to respect C-space connectivity. Store each primitive’s sampler parameters in a database for reuse.
paper_title: Using Local Experiences for Global Motion Planning

problem: To transfer prior experience and guide planning in a new environment, we need to efficiently adapt stored local knowledge to the current workspace and compose it into an effective global sampling distribution.
method: Given a new workspace, decompose it into local primitives and retrieve matching local samplers from the database using a similarity function (negative squared Euclidean distance) with a threshold; if no close match exists, compute and insert a new local sampler. Synthesize a global sampler by mixing the retrieved local samplers via a law-of-total-probability-based approximation, weighting each local sampler proportionally to its number of mixtures (a_i = M_i/N), and use this GL-Sampler alongside uniform sampling within standard planners (PRM/RRT/BiRRT).
paper_title: Using Local Experiences for Global Motion Planning

problem: A naive database of many local samplers can be large and slow to query online, and similar primitives across symmetric variants cause redundancy.
method: Reduce database size and redundancy by (i) merging cliques: build a graph over harvested configurations and greedily merge fully connected subgraphs into single GMM components; and (ii) exploiting robot symmetries to transform samplers across primitives (e.g., rotational invariance: rotate the primitive and shift the first joint’s GMM means by θ), effectively lowering the descriptor dimensionality and enabling millisecond-level retrieval in experiments.
paper_title: Using Local Experiences for Global Motion Planning

problem: Accurately predicting rigid-body motion with intermittent, planar non-convex contact patches without ad hoc point-contact approximations and while preventing artificial interpenetration.
method: Formulate a geometrically implicit time-stepping mixed nonlinear complementarity problem (MNCP) that solves contact detection and dynamics simultaneously. Model the non-convex contact by using the convex hull of the non-convex object and compute equivalent contact points (ECPs) as part of the solve via complementarity conditions derived from a closest-points optimization (Equations (8)–(12)), coupled with a maximum power dissipation/elliptic Coulomb friction model. Prove the ECP lies within the convex hull of the contact patch and that non-penetration holds at the end of each step (Propositions 1–2), even if the ECP may fall outside the physical patch.
paper_title: Rigid Body Motion Prediction with Planar Non-convex Contact Patch

problem: Efficiently simulating bodies with multiple disjoint contact patches (e.g., furniture with multiple feet) without per-patch modeling that increases computational cost or yields non-unique force distributions.
method: Replace multiple disjoint patches with a single convex-hull-based contact and solve one ECP/contact impulse jointly with the state in the MNCP. Empirically, this matches prior quadratic sliding and multi-patch MNCP models within 1e-8 in velocities while being far faster than the multi-patch MNCP (0.0053 s vs 0.0487 s per step) and only modestly slower than a specialized sliding-only model (0.0022 s per step).
paper_title: Rigid Body Motion Prediction with Planar Non-convex Contact Patch

problem: Automatically capturing transitions among surface, line, point contacts and separation during manipulation of non-convex objects while guaranteeing no penetration.
method: Use the convex-hull, geometrically implicit formulation to recompute the ECP and contact mode each step without manual mode switching. In a T-shaped bar example, the simulation transitions among contact modes under varying forces/torques while maintaining no penetration (ECP z ≈ 0 within 1e-12), demonstrating seamless mode changes handled by the unified constraints.
paper_title: Rigid Body Motion Prediction with Planar Non-convex Contact Patch

problem: Robotic manipulation of elasto-plastic solids (e.g., dough) requires safely applying controllable forces and sensing material properties, which is hard with rigid tools and complex contact dynamics.
method: Design a low-cost elastic end-effector that varies stiffness via the parallel-jaw gripper throw, built from two elastic bands and friction-fit to the fingers for easy integration. An overhead depth camera provides shape and deflection sensing. Using active palpation, fit a force model F = h(ℓ, d) from measurements on a rigid hemispherical indenter and estimate object stiffness as force/deflection to inform manipulation. This variable-stiffness hand both deforms the dough effectively and enables stiffness estimation for model initialization.
paper_title: Deformable Elasto-Plastic Object Shaping using an Elastic Hand and Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

problem: Planning efficient action sequences to roll a dough ball into a target length is intractable with high-dimensional states and unknown, nonlinear contact dynamics; a sample-efficient, real-time planner is needed.
method: Adopt model-based RL with a low-dimensional state S = {l, w, h, xh, yh} extracted from a depth camera and a constrained action space that reduces rolling to two controls: gripper throw (elastic length) and press depth, with heuristics fixing the roll location at the highest point and roll distance equal to the throw. Learn a dynamics model via linear regression with quadratic features over {l, w, h} and actions {ℓ, d} from ~1 hour of real robot random exploration per dough, then plan with MPC using either iterative CEM or Powell’s method and iteratively refit the model with on-policy data. On real dough, this achieves about 60% fewer steps than a heuristic policy and corrects poorly initialized models online.
paper_title: Deformable Elasto-Plastic Object Shaping using an Elastic Hand and Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

problem: Robots must handle previously unseen dough types (different hydration/stiffness) without retraining a full dynamics model from scratch, which otherwise degrades efficiency.
method: Use the hand’s palpation-based stiffness estimate to initialize the dynamics for an unknown dough by linearly interpolating between two endpoint models (soft/hydrated and stiff/dry) weighted by the measured stiffness, then run the same MPC-based RL with online refinement. This stiffness-informed initialization reduces steps to goal by ~40% versus incorrect model initialization and yields more consistent action choices, including on a medium-stiffness dough unseen in training.
paper_title: Deformable Elasto-Plastic Object Shaping using an Elastic Hand and Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

problem: Robots need real-time, robust 6-DoF tracking of arbitrary, previously unseen objects for manipulation, including under occlusion, without requiring object models or per-object training.
method: Combine a 2D RGB Siamese tracker (SiamMask) with the THOR long-/short-term template module to obtain a segmentation mask and robust template updates, then fuse depth by projecting masked pixels to a point cloud and register an initialization-time template point cloud via Generalized-ICP to recover the 6-DoF pose. The G-ICP stage uses the last pose (or a kinematic prior when the robot is holding the object) as initialization and applies voxel grid filtering for efficiency. This THOR + G-ICP pipeline is class-agnostic, model-free, requires no further training, runs in real time, and remains stable under occlusion because the segmentation excludes occluders before registration.
paper_title: Deep 6-DoF Tracking of Unknown Objects for Reactive Grasping

problem: Selecting feasible grasp configurations for unknown and potentially moving objects must account for the robot’s current state, avoid collisions, and remain reactive to environmental changes.
method: Introduce a dynamic grasp configuration observer that generates N grasp candidates by solving an inverse-kinematics optimization with costs for object-relative end-effector position, axis alignment to the object frame, collision avoidance with a distance threshold, and joint-limit/homing regularization. To diversify approaches, add a randomized alignment term using a random orthonormal basis with age-dependent weights that decrease over time, while increasing the main costs, preventing conflict with the alignment objective. Candidates are continuously updated each iteration and ranked by whether the end-effector ends inside the object’s cuboid and its distance to the cuboid center, with homing cost as a tie-breaker.
paper_title: Deep 6-DoF Tracking of Unknown Objects for Reactive Grasping

problem: Planning to a grasp in dynamic scenes must adapt online to moving objects and emerging collisions, which static planners or precomputed paths cannot handle reliably.
method: Use a reactive 'backstepping' planner that follows a target trajectory defined by linear interpolation between a homing configuration q0 and the selected grasp q*. At each control step, move toward the closest point on this target trajectory; if a step causes a collision, step back toward q0 and retry, effectively retreating until a collision-free segment is found before proceeding forward again. This simple mechanism enables online recovery from disturbances and dynamic changes without full replanning.
paper_title: Deep 6-DoF Tracking of Unknown Objects for Reactive Grasping

problem: Sim-to-real manipulation policies often overfit to large, distractor-laden state spaces, making them brittle under domain shifts and sample-inefficient to adapt. A key unmet need is to identify which context/state variables are causally relevant to a parameterized controller and to exploit this structure for transfer.
method: CREST performs simulation-based causal reasoning with an internal (approximate) model to discover the policy’s input structure. Phase 1 intervenes on one context variable at a time (||Δc||0=1) after solving for task-specific controller parameters θi, and marks a variable as relevant if reusing θi fails; this yields the relevant set τ. Phase 2 identifies parameter-specific inputs τj by re-solving under interventions on τ and using a breadth-first search to find a minimal set of parameter changes (min ||Δθ||0), assigning each θj the variables whose interventions necessitated changing θj. The learned structure is encoded in Reduced MLPs (inputs τ) and Partitioned MLPs (per-parameter towers with inputs τj), pretrained in the internal model with domain randomization only over τ, then transferred and fine-tuned in the target domain.
paper_title: Causal Reasoning in Simulation for Structure and Transfer Learning of Robot Manipulation Policies

problem: Learned manipulation policies must scale with many irrelevant objects and remain robust to distribution shifts (e.g., colors, dynamics) while minimizing target-domain data.
method: Using CREST’s causal structure, policies are constructed to depend only on relevant inputs (RMLP) or per-parameter subsets (PMLP), drastically shrinking inputs and parameter counts so sample complexity scales with the relevant context rather than total scene size. Pretrained with PPO in the internal model and fine-tuned on target, these structured policies (i) scale to many distractors in block stacking with bounded sample needs and strong zero-shot transfer (e.g., under an irrelevant color shift, PMLP needed 0.1k target samples vs. 17.3k for MLP and achieved 9/10 zero-shot; no-shift zero-shot 10/10), and (ii) improve robustness and fine-tuning efficiency under dynamics shifts in crate opening (e.g., RMLP consistently required fewer updates than MLP across light/nominal/stiff settings). Real-robot block stacking further shows improved zero-shot transfer (PMLP 10/10, RMLP 9/10 vs. MLP 3/10).
paper_title: Causal Reasoning in Simulation for Structure and Transfer Learning of Robot Manipulation Policies

problem: LLM-based robotic systems struggle to manipulate diverse articulated objects because they lack an explicit, uniform understanding of object kinematics (parts, joints, and contact points), especially in real-world settings.
method: Introduce a Unified Kinematic Knowledge Parser that converts each object's geometric-linked parts, joint types/properties, and contact locations into a structured textual (.xml-like) description that LLMs can reliably parse. In simulation this info is extracted directly; in the real world it is obtained via a perception pipeline (Grounding-DINO + Segment Anything for segmentation and GAPartNet for actionable parts and joint estimation). This unified, code-like description supplies LLMs with the precise kinematic context needed for manipulation policy generation.
paper_title: Kinematic-aware Prompting for Generalizable Articulated Object Manipulation with LLMs

problem: Translating LLMs’ abstract manipulation commonsense into precise low-level 3D control is difficult, particularly for articulated objects (e.g., revolute joints) that demand kinematically consistent trajectories.
method: Propose a Kinematic-aware Manipulation Planner using a hierarchical, chain-of-thought prompting scheme. Step 1: Given the kinematic description and instruction, prompt the LLM to produce an abstract manipulation sequence that explicitly references and copies relevant kinematic properties (e.g., coordinates, joint orientation) to ensure feasibility. Step 2: Conditioned on this sequence and few-shot demos, have the LLM generate formatted 3D waypoints and primitive actions (move, grasp, release, clockwise/anticlockwise rotate by 30°). An operational space controller then converts waypoints into low-level control, enabling precise execution.
paper_title: Kinematic-aware Prompting for Generalizable Articulated Object Manipulation with LLMs

problem: Achieving broad generalization to unseen instances and categories with minimal robotic data remains challenging for articulated object manipulation.
method: Leverage the kinematic-aware prompting pipeline with only 17 in-context demonstrations across 8 categories to enable zero-shot manipulation on both seen and unseen categories. The system generates 3D waypoints that generalize to prismatic and revolute objects, outperforming LLM2Skill, naive LLM2Waypoints, and behavior cloning across 48 instances in 16 categories, and transfers to real-world manipulation on a Franka Panda arm over 7 object categories.
paper_title: Kinematic-aware Prompting for Generalizable Articulated Object Manipulation with LLMs

problem: Robots need to predict how elastoplastic objects will deform and undergo topological changes (merging, splitting, self-merging) under different end-effector geometries and actions from partial RGB-D observations, which prior methods largely ignore or handle post hoc.
method: DoughNet introduces a Transformer-based denoising autoencoder that embeds a partial point cloud into a fixed-size set of latent codes, which a decoder turns into per-component 3D occupancy masks plus per-component genus labels. A topology-aware dynamics model then autoregressively predicts the next set of latents conditioned on the embedded end-effector geometry (sampled surface points over two time steps). Training uses permutation-invariant reconstruction with focal loss and a Huber loss directly on latent sets over two steps to mitigate exposure bias, enabling joint long-horizon prediction of geometry and topology without post-hoc topology checks.
paper_title: DoughNet: A Visual Predictive Model for Topological Manipulation of Deformable Objects

problem: Obtaining ground-truth dynamic topology (when and how components merge/split) for elastoplastic manipulation is ill-defined from particle data and infeasible to annotate on real observations.
method: The paper proposes physically plausible topology-checking operations within an MPM-based simulator: counterfactual velocity perturbations are applied to candidate regions to test whether they remain connected (merge) or separate (split), updating a scene-topology graph over time. Using particle labels, component surface meshes are extracted via signed-distance grids and Dual Contouring, and realistic single-view, noisy, partial depth observations are rendered. This synthetic data generator provides consistent geometry, component labels, and topology supervision to train DoughNet.
paper_title: DoughNet: A Visual Predictive Model for Topological Manipulation of Deformable Objects

problem: Planning topological manipulation from a single partial view requires selecting not only actions but also tool geometry and pose to achieve a target geometry/topology.
method: DoughNet is integrated with a CEM planner that rolls out action sequences in latent space and scores them by cosine similarity to the goal latents, naturally capturing both geometry and topology. The sampled action space includes in-plane translations/rotations and discrete end-effector geometry and opening width; distributions are iteratively refit to top-performing samples. This enables accurate selection of tool, pose, and opening width to reproduce robot- and human-made goals in simulation and on real hardware from single RGB-D observations.
paper_title: DoughNet: A Visual Predictive Model for Topological Manipulation of Deformable Objects

problem: Robotic Sim2Real transfer suffers from visual-domain gaps because simulators lack photorealism, robust geometry in complex scenes, and consistent lighting between background and interactive objects—especially for non-Lambertian, thin, or texture-poor targets.
method: A hierarchical Real2Sim pipeline that (i) reconstructs scene-level backgrounds as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) fields regularized by laser-scanned geometry to suppress floaters and blurs, and estimates HDR environment lighting from a single image via DiffusionLight to match appearance; (ii) reconstructs interactive objects via laser scanning (Artec Leo) or single-image 3D generation (CLAY), then performs PBR relighting offline in Blender using the estimated HDR map; and (iii) introduces Mesh-Gaussian transfer to integrate meshes into the 3DGS renderer by initializing one Gaussian per triangle, flattening to slanted planar primitives aligned with face normals, scaling by mean barycenter–vertex distance, and adding depth/opacity regularizers, with an optional 3DGS→mesh path via TSDF fusion. This dual 3DGS–Mesh setup yields photorealistic visuals with physics-ready meshes.
paper_title: DISCOVERSE: Efficient Robot Simulation in Complex High-Fidelity Environments

problem: Scaling end-to-end robot learning requires an efficient, easy-to-configure simulator that unifies high-fidelity rendering with accurate physics and supports diverse sensors and assets.
method: DISCOVERSE integrates a native tile-based 3DGS CUDA renderer with the MuJoCo physical engine and a ROS2 interface, using a dual 3DGS–Mesh (MJCF/XML) representation for interactive nodes to combine photorealism with accurate contact dynamics. It supports multi-modal sensing, including RGB/depth via Gaussian alpha compositing, a BVH-accelerated native Gaussian LiDAR ray tracer (>100 FPS), and tactile sensing via Tacchi, alongside broad robot/asset compatibility. The massively parallel implementation achieves 650 FPS rendering for 5 RGB-D cameras at 640×480, enabling rapid, large-scale training and deployment.
paper_title: DISCOVERSE: Efficient Robot Simulation in Complex High-Fidelity Environments

problem: Collecting large numbers of high-quality robot demonstrations in the real world is slow and costly, limiting data scale for imitation learning and hindering Sim2Real performance.
method: DISCOVERSE provides automated state generation—combining motion planners with a gamepad-based keypoint recorder of relative robot–object poses—to synthesize demonstrations quickly, yielding ~100× faster data collection than manual real-world logging. Coupled with image-based and generative domain randomization (video overlays, HSV/gamma perturbations, and ControlNet/GPT-4V–aided augmentation), policies trained in DISCOVERSE achieve state-of-the-art zero-shot Sim2Real results on three manipulation tasks, outperforming SplatSim by ~11% on average without augmentation and by 18.5% (ACT) / 11.4% (DP) with augmentation.
paper_title: DISCOVERSE: Efficient Robot Simulation in Complex High-Fidelity Environments

problem: Monocular depth estimation models (MDEMs) provide only affine-invariant or inaccurate depths and often fail under new camera setups, making them unusable for accurate metric depth needed in robotic manipulation. Existing post-hoc alignments (global scale–shift or locally weighted) are insufficient, especially when predictions invert relative depth.
method: Introduce MOMA with a scale–shift–rotation alignment (SSRA) that performs a one-shot calibration using a sparse set of ground-truth depth points for a fixed camera. Treat the MDEM output as a pseudo depth sensor with intrinsics and solve a non-linear optimization that aligns predicted 3D points to the camera frame using scale s, rotation R ∈ SO(3), and translation T, minimizing depth-only residuals. This single Θ̂ is then used to transform future MDEM outputs. SSRA consistently outperforms GSSA and LWLR across multiple poses (e.g., δ1.10 > 0.85 and MAE < 0.03) and corrects severe depth errors (e.g., relative-depth inversions), while running in milliseconds at inference and taking only a few seconds for one-shot calibration.
paper_title: Monocular One-Shot Metric-Depth Alignment for RGB-Based Robot Grasping

problem: Even with a fixed camera, raw MDEM depths fluctuate across scenes, invalidating one-shot calibration because the same pixels can receive different predicted depth ranges.
method: Normalize MDEM outputs prior to alignment and adopt a min–max normalization of the predicted depth to stabilize the depth range across scenes. Ablations show min–max normalization consistently improves all alignment methods and is crucial for SSRA (e.g., on Pose 1, SSRA achieves δ1.05 = 0.8492 and MAE = 0.0134 with min–max, while removing normalization significantly degrades performance), enabling reliable one-shot use.
paper_title: Monocular One-Shot Metric-Depth Alignment for RGB-Based Robot Grasping

problem: Transparent objects challenge depth sensors and zero-shot MDEMs, yet robotic grasping requires accurate metric depth on such objects using only RGB.
method: Fine-tune a strong MDEM (Depth Anything) on a large transparent-object mix (~500K images) using LoRA (rank 256) and a scale-invariant loss, then apply SSRA for metric alignment. This combination yields practical metric accuracy on transparent objects (e.g., standing: δ1.05 = 0.729, MAE = 0.0234; in-tote: MAE = 0.0242) and high real-robot success rates using only RGB (e.g., 70% two-finger and 78% suction on transparent objects).
paper_title: Monocular One-Shot Metric-Depth Alignment for RGB-Based Robot Grasping
